SDLP is the **most widely reported** single statistic for lane-keeping quality:

$$\sigma_{x_l} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_{l,i} - \bar{x}_l)^2}$$

**Interpretation:** SDLP quantifies the **weaving/swerving** of the vehicle. A low SDLP = stable lane keeping. A high SDLP = impaired or distracted driving.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical benchmarks (KTU 2024 reference values):**
> - Sober, alert driver: **$\sigma_{x_l} \approx 0.15$ – $0.25$ m**
> - Driver at legal BAC limit (0.08%): **$\sigma_{x_l} \approx 0.30$ – $0.45$ m**
> - Severely impaired driver: **$\sigma_{x_l} > 0.50$ m**

### 2.3.4 Root Mean Square (RMS) Lateral Deviation

When zero-mean is not guaranteed (e.g., instrument noise), RMS gives a more robust magnitude indicator:

$$x_{l,RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_{l,i}^2}$$

### 2.3.5 Time-to-Line Crossing (TLC)

TLC estimates the time horizon before the vehicle will cross a lane boundary, given current lateral position, lateral velocity, and heading:

$$TLC = \frac{x_{l,boundary} - x_l}{\dot{x}_l + \psi \cdot v_x}$$

where:
- $x_{l,boundary}$ = distance from lane centre to edge line
- $x_l$ = current lateral position
- $\dot{x}_l$ = lateral velocity
- $\psi$ = vehicle yaw angle
- $v_x$ = longitudinal velocity

A **low TLC** indicates an imminent lane departure — a critical safety warning.

### 2.3.6 Lane Exceedance Ratio (LER)

$$LER = \frac{T_{exceed}}{T_{total}}$$

where:
- $T_{exceed}$ = time during which any part of the vehicle was outside the lane boundary
- $T_{total}$ = total observation time

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{x}_l$ | $\frac{1}{N}\sum x_{l,i}$ | Mean Lateral Position (MLP) | m |
| 2 | $\sigma_{x_l}$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum (x_{l,i}-\bar{x}_l)^2}$ | Standard Deviation of Lateral Position (SDLP) | m |
| 3 | $x_{l,RMS}$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x_{l,i}^2}$ | RMS Lateral Deviation | m |
| 4 | $TLC$ | $\frac{x_{b}-x_l}{\dot{x}_l+\psi v_x}$ | Time-to-Line Crossing | s |
| 5 | $LER$ | $T_{exceed} \vert T_{total}$ | Lane Exceedance Ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $CV$ | $\sigma_{x_l} / \bar{x}_l$ | Coefficient of Variation (lateral) | % |
| 7 | $SRR$ | $f_{reversal}$ at threshold $\theta$ | Steering Reversal Rate | reversals/min |
| 8 | $MHF$ | $f_{lane exceed}$ per km | Mean Hazard Frequency | events/km |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of SDLP:** SDLP is the *de facto* legal threshold metric used in pharmacologically-impaired driving research. A change of **+0.05 m in SDLP** is often cited as the **minimum clinically meaningful impairment** threshold by international regulatory bodies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the SDLP Formula

We begin with the conceptual definition of "deviation from the lateral reference" and arrive at the formula statistically used in KTU assessments.

### Step 1 — Sample Definition

Let $\{x_{l,1}, x_{l,2}, \ldots, x_{l,N}\}$ be the $N$ sampled lateral position measurements (in metres) recorded at uniform time intervals $\Delta t$. Each $x_{l,i}$ is measured from the lane centreline.

### Step 2 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean

Sum all sampled lateral positions and divide by the number of samples. The mean represents the **central tendency** of the lateral trajectory:

$$\bar{x}_l = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_{l,i}$$

This is the **first moment** of the lateral position distribution.

### Step 3 — Compute the Deviations (Residuals)

For each sample, compute the deviation from the mean:

$$\epsilon_i = x_{l,i} - \bar{x}_l$$

These deviations can be positive (right of centre) or negative (left of centre). By the property of the mean, the sum of deviations is zero:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \epsilon_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_{l,i} - N\bar{x}_l = N\bar{x}_l - N\bar{x}_l = 0$$

### Step 4 — Square the Deviations

Squaring eliminates sign and emphasizes large excursions (which are precisely the safety-critical events):

$$\epsilon_i^2 = (x_{l,i} - \bar{x}_l)^2$$

### Step 5 — Sum the Squared Deviations

The sum of squared deviations is the **total scatter energy** of the lateral trajectory:

$$SS = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_{l,i} - \bar{x}_l)^2$$

### Step 6 — Divide by Degrees of Freedom

For an *unbiased* estimator of population variance, we divide by $(N-1)$ rather than $N$. This is Bessel's correction and reflects the loss of one degree of freedom when $\bar{x}_l$ is itself estimated from the same sample:

$$s^2 = \frac{SS}{N-1} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{l,i} - \bar{x}_l)^2$$

### Step 7 — Take the Square Root

The standard deviation is the square root of the variance, returning to the original units (metres):

$$\sigma_{x_l} = \sqrt{s^2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{l,i} - \bar{x}_l)^2}$$

This completes the derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $(N-1)$ and not $N$?** When $\bar{x}_l$ is computed *from the same data*, the residuals are no longer independent — they sum to zero by construction. One degree of freedom is "used up" in estimating the mean. Bessel's correction $(N-1)$ compensates for this and gives an unbiased estimator of the true population standard deviation.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

A vehicle's lateral position is sampled 6 times during a 30-second observation window: $\{+0.20, +0.18, +0.25, +0.15, +0.22, +0.19\}$ metres from lane centre.

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_l &= \frac{0.20 + 0.18 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.22 + 0.19}{6} \\
&= \frac{1.19}{6} \\
&= 0.1983 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Deviations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_1 &= 0.20 - 0.1983 = 0.0017 \\
\epsilon_2 &= 0.18 - 0.1983 = -0.0183 \\
\epsilon_3 &= 0.25 - 0.1983 = 0.0517 \\
\epsilon_4 &= 0.15 - 0.1983 = -0.0483 \\
\epsilon_5 &= 0.22 - 0.1983 = 0.0217 \\
\epsilon_6 &= 0.19 - 0.1983 = -0.0083
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Squared deviations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_1^2 &= 0.0000029 \\
\epsilon_2^2 &= 0.0003356 \\
\epsilon_3^2 &= 0.0026706 \\
\epsilon_4^2 &= 0.0023356 \\
\epsilon_5^2 &= 0.0004694 \\
\epsilon_6^2 &= 0.0000694
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$SS = 0.0000029 + 0.0003356 + 0.0026706 + 0.0023356 + 0.0004694 + 0.0000694 = 0.0058835$$

**Step 5 — Variance and SDLP:**

$$s^2 = \frac{0.0058835}{6-1} = \frac{0.0058835}{5} = 0.0011767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\sigma_{x_l} = \sqrt{0.0011767} = 0.0343 \text{ m} = 3.43 \text{ cm}$$

**Interpretation:** SDLP = **3.43 cm** is well below the **0.15 m** alert-driver threshold — this is an alert, non-impaired driver.

## 3.3 Derivation of TLC from Vehicle Kinematics

Consider a bicycle-model vehicle moving with longitudinal velocity $v_x$ and yaw angle $\psi$ relative to the lane. Let the front axle reference point have lateral position $x_l$, and the **left** lane boundary be at $x_b$ (signed).

**Step 1:** The lateral velocity of the reference point has two contributions: (a) direct lateral motion $\dot{x}_l$ and (b) lateral displacement due to yaw $\psi$ rotating the forward vector:

$$\dot{x}_{l,total} = \dot{x}_l + \psi \cdot v_x$$

**Step 2:** Assuming constant lateral velocity over short horizons (a first-order approximation valid for the sub-second scales used in lane-departure warning), the time to reach the boundary is:

$$TLC = \frac{x_b - x_l}{\dot{x}_l + \psi \cdot v_x}$$

**Step 3:** Two TLCs are usually computed (left and right), and the minimum is taken as the critical TLC:

$$TLC_{crit} = \min(TLC_{left},\; TLC_{right})$$

A common intervention threshold in **Lane Departure Warning (LDW)** systems is **$TLC_{crit} < 0.5$ s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Driver Tasks (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["DRIVER TASK HIERARCHY"]
    node2["STRATEGIC LEVEL - Navigation"]
    node3["Route Planning"]
    node4["Choice of Road"]
    node5["MANOEUVRING LEVEL - Guidance"]
    node6["Lane Keeping"]
    node7["Overtaking"]
    node8["Gap Acceptance"]
    node9["OPERATIONAL LEVEL - Control"]
    node10["Steering Modulation"]
    node11["Braking"]
    node12["Throttle Control"]
    node13["NON-DRIVING TASKS"]
    node14["Mobile Phone Use"]
    node15["Eating or Drinking"]
    node16["Infotainment Adjustment"]
    node17["Conversation"]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node5
    node1 --> node9
    node1 --> node13
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node9 --> node10
    node9 --> node11
    node9 --> node12
    node13 --> node14
    node13 --> node15
    node13 --> node16
    node13 --> node17
```

## 4.2 Driver Performance Measurement Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["Visual Input"] --> procA["PERCEPTUAL STAGE"]
    inpB["Auditory Input"] --> procA
    inpC["Haptic Input"] --> procA
    procA --> procB["COGNITIVE STAGE - Decision"]
    procB --> procC["MOTOR STAGE - Action"]
    procC --> outA["Steering"]
    procC --> outB["Braking"]
    procC --> outC["Acceleration"]
    outA --> meas1["LATERAL MEASURES - SDLP, TLC, LER"]
    outB --> meas2["LONGITUDINAL MEASURES - Speed SD, Headway"]
    outC --> meas2
    meas1 --> evalA["PERFORMANCE EVALUATION"]
    meas2 --> evalA
    procB --> physM["PHYSIOLOGICAL MEASURES - HRV, EDA, EEG, Pupil"]
    physM --> evalA
```

## 4.3 Lane Position Deviation – Geometric Schematic

```
   <------ Lane Width: 3.5 m (typical) ------>
   |                                          |
   |  <-- Left Edge -->       <-- Right Edge ->|
   |          (LE)         (RE)               |
   |             |----------|                 |
   |             |          |                 |
   |             |  Lane Centre              |
   |             |          |                 |
   |             |----------|                 |
   |                                          |
   |  x_b (left)  x_l (vehicle)   x_b (right) |
   |       <----------> <---------->          |
   |        d_left        d_right             |
   |                                          |
   |----|----------------|--------------------|---
   | -1.75 m           0 m                 +1.75 m
   |        (Lateral position x-axis)         |
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- Lane centre is the **zero reference** ($x_l = 0$).
- Left edge line is at $x_b = -1.75$ m, right edge at $x_b = +1.75$ m.
- Vehicle's lateral position $x_l$ is measured from centre.
- $d_{left} = x_l - x_b^{left}$ ; $d_{right} = x_b^{right} - x_l$.
- A lane exceedance occurs when $d_{left} < 0$ or $d_{right} < 0$.

## 4.4 Measurement Pipeline for Lateral Deviation Studies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    recA["Camera or LiDAR sensor"] --> recB["Lane Marking Detection"]
    recB --> recC["Vehicle Pose Estimation"]
    recC --> recD["Lateral Position Time-Series"]
    recD --> anaA["Signal Conditioning - Filter, Downsample"]
    anaA --> anaB["Compute MLP, SDLP, RMS, TLC, LER"]
    anaB --> anaC["Statistical Hypothesis Testing"]
    anaC --> anaD["Driver Performance Report"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Driver Performance Measurement. Differentiate between driving tasks and non-driving tasks with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Driver Performance Measurement is the systematic assessment of a driver's operational, behavioural, and physiological responses during vehicle control, typically expressed through quantitative indicators such as SDLP, speed variability, reaction time, and eye-glance behaviour.

**Driving Tasks (1 mark):** Tasks directly related to vehicle control, navigation, and guidance. Examples: (i) steering to maintain lane position; (ii) braking to maintain safe headway.

**Non-Driving Tasks (1 mark):** In-vehicle activities not directly related to vehicle control but which consume attentional resources. Examples: (i) operating a mobile phone; (ii) adjusting the radio/infotainment.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the Standard Deviation of Lateral Position (SDLP)? Why is it considered the gold-standard metric for lane-keeping performance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** SDLP is the standard deviation, computed over a sampled time-series, of the lateral distance between a reference point on the vehicle and the lane centre. It is given by:

$$\sigma_{x_l} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{l,i} - \bar{x}_l)^2}$$

**Why gold-standard (1.5 marks):** (i) It is a single, objective, continuous numerical index of lateral weaving/swerving; (ii) it is highly sensitive to impairment (alcohol, drugs, fatigue, distraction); (iii) it correlates strongly with crash risk; (iv) it is non-invasive, requires no driver-mounted sensors, and can be obtained from external cameras or vehicle CAN-bus data.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> When answering questions on SDLP/TLC, KTU examiners deduct marks for the following common errors:
> 1. **Using $N$ instead of $N-1$** in the SDLP denominator (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Failing to specify units** (metres for position, seconds for TLC) — lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing MLP and SDLP** — MLP is a measure of *bias*, SDLP is a measure of *variability*; both are needed.
> 4. **Ignoring the sign convention** in TLC (left vs. right boundary).
> 5. **Not interpreting numerical results** — KTU expects an explicit safety statement (e.g., "this SDLP falls in the impaired-driving range").

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Classify the different types and categories of driver performance measures with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The following lateral position data (in metres, positive = right of centre) was recorded for a vehicle in a 30-second trial:

$\{+0.18,\; +0.22,\; +0.15,\; +0.28,\; +0.20,\; +0.25,\; +0.12,\; +0.30\}$

Compute the **Mean Lateral Position (MLP)** and the **Standard Deviation of Lateral Position (SDLP)**. Comment on the lane-keeping quality. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

Driver performance measures can be classified along **three axes**:

**Axis 1 — Channel of Measurement (4 marks):**

1. **Vehicle-Based (Output) Measures:** Lateral position, speed, steering wheel angle, accelerator/brake pedal position, time-to-line crossing, headway.
2. **Driver-Based (Input) Measures:** Eye-glance behaviour, blink rate, pupil diameter, head pose, heart rate variability, skin conductance, EEG.
3. **Performance/Task-Based (Outcome) Measures:** Reaction time, number of lane exceedances, crash/near-crash frequency, NASA-TLX workload score, detection-response-task (DRT) latency.

**Axis 2 — Type of Variable (2 marks):**

- Continuous (e.g., lateral position)
- Discrete/Event (e.g., lane exceedances)
- Composite/Derived (e.g., SDLP, TLC)

**Axis 3 — Driving Sub-Task (1 mark):**

- Lateral control, longitudinal control, perceptual/cognitive, reactional.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Mean Lateral Position (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_l &= \frac{0.18 + 0.22 + 0.15 + 0.28 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.12 + 0.30}{8} \\
&= \frac{1.70}{8} \\
&= 0.2125 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Deviations and Squared Deviations (2.5 marks):**

| $i$ | $x_{l,i}$ | $\epsilon_i = x_{l,i} - 0.2125$ | $\epsilon_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.18 | -0.0325 | 0.0010563 |
| 2 | 0.22 | +0.0075 | 0.0000563 |
| 3 | 0.15 | -0.0625 | 0.0039063 |
| 4 | 0.28 | +0.0675 | 0.0045563 |
| 5 | 0.20 | -0.0125 | 0.0001563 |
| 6 | 0.25 | +0.0375 | 0.0014063 |
| 7 | 0.12 | -0.0925 | 0.0085563 |
| 8 | 0.30 | +0.0875 | 0.0076563 |
| **Sum** | | | **0.0273500** |

**Step 3 — SDLP (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_{x_l} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0273500}{8-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0273500}{7}} = \sqrt{0.0039071} = 0.0625 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

- MLP = **+0.21 m** (right of centre) — driver shows a slight rightward bias, but the magnitude is small.
- SDLP = **0.0625 m = 6.25 cm** — this is well below the **0.15 m** alert-driver threshold. The driver is performing within the lane-keeping envelope of a sober, attentive driver. **Lane-keeping quality: GOOD.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **Michon Hierarchical Model of driving tasks**. Discuss how non-driving tasks (such as mobile phone use) interfere with each level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vehicle is travelling at $v_x = 25$ m/s on a 3.5 m wide lane. The lateral position of the front axle reference is $x_l = +0.40$ m (right of centre), lateral velocity is $\dot{x}_l = -0.30$ m/s (moving leftward), and yaw angle is $\psi = +0.01$ rad. Compute the **Time-to-Line-Crossing (TLC)** for the right lane edge. Given an LDW intervention threshold of $TLC < 0.5$ s, will the warning be triggered? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Michon Hierarchical Model (3 marks):**

The Michon model (1985) decomposes driving into three nested, time-scaled hierarchical levels:

1. **Strategic Level (long time-scale, minutes to hours):** Navigation and trip planning — e.g., route choice, choice of highway vs. city road.
2. **Manoeuvring / Guidance Level (medium time-scale, seconds):** Tactical decisions in traffic — e.g., lane changing, overtaking, gap acceptance, intersection negotiation.
3. **Operational / Control Level (short time-scale, sub-second):** Continuous vehicle control — e.g., steering wheel modulation, braking, throttle, gear shifting.

The three levels are interactive: poor strategic decisions increase manoeuvring load, which in turn strains the operational level.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
    ╔══════════════════════════════════════╗
    ║   STRATEGIC LEVEL (Trip Planning)    ║   Time horizon: minutes–hours
    ║   • Route choice                     ║
    ║   • Mode selection                   ║
    ╚════════════════════╦═════════════════╝
                         ║
    ╔════════════════════╩═════════════════╗
    ║   MANOEUVRING LEVEL (Guidance)       ║   Time horizon: seconds
    ║   • Lane change                      ║
    ║   • Overtaking                       ║
    ║   • Gap acceptance                   ║
    ╚════════════════════╦═════════════════╝
                         ║
    ╔════════════════════╩═════════════════╗
    ║   OPERATIONAL LEVEL (Control)        ║   Time horizon: < 1 s
    ║   • Steering modulation              ║
    ║   • Braking                          ║
    ║   • Throttle                         ║
    ╚══════════════════════════════════════╝
```

**Interference from Mobile Phone Use (2 marks):**

- **Strategic:** Phone call may force a route deviation or distract from GPS instructions.
- **Manoeuvring:** Cognitive load from conversation delays gap-acceptance decisions; visual-manual load delays mirror checks and signal use.
- **Operational:** Hands off steering wheel; slower brake reaction time; SDLP rises sharply (studies show +0.10 to +0.20 m).

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the right-edge boundary (1 mark):**

For a 3.5 m lane, the right edge is at $x_b = +1.75$ m from lane centre.

**Step 2 — Compute the effective lateral velocity (2 marks):**

$$\dot{x}_{l,total} = \dot{x}_l + \psi \cdot v_x$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_{l,total} &= -0.30 + (0.01)(25) \\
&= -0.30 + 0.25 \\
&= -0.05 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

The negative sign indicates motion *toward the left* (towards lane centre), not towards the right edge.

**Step 3 — Compute TLC to the right edge (2.5 marks):**

$$TLC_{right} = \frac{x_b - x_l}{\dot{x}_{l,total}} = \frac{1.75 - 0.40}{-0.05} = \frac{1.35}{-0.05} = -27 \text{ s}$$

A **negative TLC** means the vehicle is moving *away* from the right edge — no right-lane-departure is imminent under the assumed linear extrapolation.

**Step 4 — LDW decision (1.5 marks):**

Since $TLC_{right} = -27$ s $\not< 0.5$ s, the **Lane Departure Warning (LDW) will NOT be triggered** for the right edge. (For completeness, one would also compute $TLC_{left}$ using $x_b = -1.75$ m to check the more critical boundary.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 — PEAUT861):**

- **Driver Performance Measurement (DPM)** = systematic, multi-channel assessment of driver behaviour and vehicle control.
- **Three Channels of Measurement:** Vehicle-based, Driver-based, Task/Outcome-based.
- **Driving Tasks** are classified by the **Michon Model** into Strategic, Manoeuvring, and Operational.
- **Non-Driving Tasks** (phone, eating, infotainment) compete for the driver's limited attentional resources.
- **Mean Lateral Position (MLP):** $\bar{x}_l = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_{l,i}$ → measures **bias**.
- **SDLP:** $\sigma_{x_l} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum(x_{l,i}-\bar{x}_l)^2}$ → measures **variability/weaving**; the gold-standard lane-keeping metric.
- **TLC:** $TLC = (x_b - x_l)/(\dot{x}_l + \psi v_x)$ → predicts **time-to-lane-departure**.
- **Lane Exceedance Ratio (LER):** time-outside-lane / total-time → binary safety event metric.
- **Empirical SDLP benchmarks:** Alert ≈ **0.15–0.25 m**; impaired (BAC 0.08%) ≈ **0.30–0.45 m**; severely impaired > **0.50 m**.
- **Minimum clinically meaningful impairment threshold:** $\Delta$SDLP = **+0.05 m**.
- **Bessel's correction** uses $(N-1)$ in SDLP to make the estimator unbiased.
- **Sign convention** for lateral position: typically +ve = right of centre, -ve = left of centre.
- **Lane width standard** for KTU reference problems: **3.5 m** (IRC:64 in India).
- **LDW intervention threshold:** $TLC < 0.5$ s.
- **Naturalistic Driving Studies (NDS)** = highest ecological validity for DPM data.
- **Steering Reversal Rate (SRR):** frequency of steering reversals above a threshold angle; a high SRR indicates correction-heavy, unstable control.
- **Coefficient of Variation (CV):** $\sigma/\mu$ — used to compare variability across drivers with different mean positions.
- **NASA-TLX** = subjective workload index, frequently paired with SDLP in dual-task studies.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steering Wheel Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steering Wheel Angle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — STEERING WHEEL ANGLE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steering Wheel Angle (θₛ)** is defined as the angular displacement of the steering wheel, measured in **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)**, from its neutral (straight-ahead) position to any instantaneous position during a turning maneuver.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the steering wheel angle is the primary **driver input parameter** in the human–vehicle–road closed-loop system. It is the first kinematic variable in the steering chain: **Driver → Steering Wheel → Steering Column → Steering Gearbox → Pitman Arm / Rack → Tie Rods → Steered Wheels**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Steering Wheel Angle bridges *driver ergonomics* (comfort) and *vehicle dynamics* (safety). It directly influences the **steering ratio**, **steering effort**, and **vehicle response time**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a **ship's helm** on a vessel. A small rotation of the wheel causes the rudder (the actual steering element) to swing through a much larger angle, which gently turns the massive ship. In a car, the steering wheel acts exactly like the helm: it **amplifies** the driver's small hand rotation into a proportionally larger rotation of the road wheels through a mechanical advantage known as the **steering ratio**.

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Steering Wheel Angle | θₛ | **Degrees (°)** or **radians** |
| Average Steering Wheel Angle (lock-to-lock) | θₛ,total | **~1080°** (3 full turns) |
| Driver Hand Input Torque Range | Tᵢ | **5 – 20 Nm** |
| Maximum Steering Effort (PAL) | T_max | **≤ 50 Nm** (power-assisted) |

> [!TIP]
> **PAL = Power-Assisted Limit.** KTU examiners frequently test whether the steering wheel angle must remain bounded for a given steering ratio so that the driver's hand torque never exceeds ergonomic limits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Steering Chain — Logic Breakdown

The steering wheel angle does not directly steer the vehicle. It is the **input** to a multi-stage mechanical transmission. Each stage obeys a kinematic relationship:

1. **Driver Input Stage** — Hands apply torque on the rim of the steering wheel. The wheel rotates by θₛ.
2. **Column Stage** — The steering column transmits θₛ unchanged (assuming a non-collapsible fixed column) to the steering gearbox.
3. **Gearbox Stage** — The steering gearbox converts small θₛ into large angular displacement at the pitman arm or large linear displacement at the rack. This conversion is the **steering ratio**.
4. **Linkage Stage** — Pitman arm or rack pushes/pulls the tie rods, turning the steered wheels by δ (steering angle of the road wheels).
5. **Wheel Stage** — The steered wheel angle δ acts on the tire contact patch, generating a **lateral force** that produces yaw.

### 2.2 The Core Relationship: Wheel Angle vs. Wheel Angle

The **steering ratio (iₛ)** is the dimensionless gain between steering wheel angle and the average steered wheel angle:

$$
i_s = \frac{\theta_s}{\delta} = \frac{\text{Steering Wheel Angle}}{\text{Average Steered Road Wheel Angle}}
$$

A typical passenger car has an **overall steering ratio between 12 : 1 and 20 : 1**. Sports cars favor lower ratios (more direct feel), while luxury sedans favor higher ratios (more comfort).

### 2.3 The Mechanical Linkage Output

For a **rack-and-pinion** system, the tie rod linear displacement *x* is:

$$
x = r_p \cdot \theta_s
$$

where *rₚ* is the pinion radius. The steered wheel angle δ becomes:

$$
\delta = \frac{x}{L_t} = \frac{r_p \cdot \theta_s}{L_t}
$$

For a **recirculating-ball** (Pitman-arm) system:

$$
\delta = \frac{\theta_s}{i_s \cdot \cos\tau}
$$

where **τ** is the caster-induced steering offset angle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = i_s \cdot \delta$ | Steering wheel angle from road wheel angle | rad or ° |
| 2 | $i_s = \theta_s \,/\, \delta$ | Steering ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 3 | $\delta_i = \cot^{-1}\!\left(\cot\delta_o + \dfrac{w}{L}\right)$ | Ackermann inner wheel angle | rad |
| 4 | $\delta_o = \cot^{-1}\!\left(\cot\delta_i - \dfrac{w}{L}\right)$ | Ackermann outer wheel angle | rad |
| 5 | $\theta_{s,\text{lock}} = i_s \cdot \delta_{\max}$ | Maximum steering wheel angle at full lock | rad or ° |
| 6 | $T_s = \dfrac{F_t \cdot L_t}{i_s \cdot \eta}$ | Steering wheel torque required (manual) | Nm |
| 7 | $\omega_\delta = \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_s}{i_s}$ | Wheel angle rate from steering rate | rad/s |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{L}{\sin\delta}$ | Turning radius from average wheel angle | m |
| 9 | $a_y = \dfrac{v^2 \cdot \tan\delta}{L}$ | Lateral acceleration (bicycle model) | m/s² |

> **Where:**
> * *w* = track width (m), *L* = wheelbase (m), *η* = steering efficiency (≈ 0.85), *Lₜ* = tie-rod lever arm (m), *Fₜ* = tie-rod force (N).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The steering wheel angle is the **measured input** in:
- **Electric Power Steering (EPS)** torque calculations.
- **Steer-by-Wire (SbW)** systems, where it is encoded by an **angle sensor** (typically ±1440° rotary encoder).
- **ADAS** features like Lane Keeping Assist (LKA), where the ECU commands a target θₛ.
- **Driver-in-the-Loop (DIL)** simulations and **HIL** testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steering Wheel Angle from Ackermann Geometry

**Given:**
A vehicle with wheelbase $L = 2.6\,\text{m}$ and track width $w = 1.5\,\text{m}$. The vehicle negotiates a turn such that the **average steering angle** is $\bar{\delta} = 18°$. The overall steering ratio is $i_s = 16$.

**Step 1 — Ackermann inner wheel angle:**
Using the improved Ackermann formula for the **inner (sharper) wheel**:

$$
\delta_i = \cot^{-1}\!\left(\cot\bar{\delta} + \frac{w}{2L}\right)
$$

Plug in the numerical values:

$$
\cot(18°) = 3.0777
$$

$$
\frac{w}{2L} = \frac{1.5}{2 \times 2.6} = \frac{1.5}{5.2} = 0.2885
$$

$$
\cot^{-1}(3.0777 + 0.2885) = \cot^{-1}(3.3662)
$$

$$
\delta_i = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{3.3662}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.2971)
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_i = 16.55°}
$$

**Step 2 — Ackermann outer wheel angle:**

$$
\delta_o = \cot^{-1}\!\left(\cot\bar{\delta} - \frac{w}{2L}\right)
$$

$$
\cot^{-1}(3.0777 - 0.2885) = \cot^{-1}(2.7892)
$$

$$
\delta_o = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{2.7892}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3585)
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_o = 19.72°}
$$

**Step 3 — Steering wheel angle from steering ratio:**

$$
\theta_s = i_s \cdot \bar{\delta} = 16 \times 18° = 288°
$$

**Step 4 — Lock-to-Lock verification:**
A typical sedan delivers lock-to-lock angle ≈ **3.5 turns × 360° = 1080°**. The required θₛ = 288° corresponds to only **0.8 turns** of the steering wheel — well within ergonomic limits.

### 3.2 Derivation: Steering Wheel Torque (Manual System, No Power Assist)

The mechanical advantage of the steering system means:

$$
T_s \cdot i_s \cdot \eta = F_t \cdot L_t
$$

Solving for steering wheel torque:

$$
T_s = \frac{F_t \cdot L_t}{i_s \cdot \eta}
$$

**Numerical check:**
- Tie-rod force $F_t = 1500\,\text{N}$ (cornering at slow speed)
- Tie-rod lever arm $L_t = 0.12\,\text{m}$
- Steering ratio $i_s = 16$
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.85$

$$
T_s = \frac{1500 \times 0.12}{16 \times 0.85} = \frac{180}{13.6}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_s = 13.24\,\text{Nm}}
$$

This lies within the comfortable ergonomic band (**5 – 20 Nm**), confirming the steering wheel angle range is correctly proportioned.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Steering Ratio on θₛ

| Steering Ratio $i_s$ | Avg. Road Wheel $\delta$ | Steering Wheel Angle $\theta_s$ | Driver Comfort | Vehicle Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 12 : 1 | 30° | 360° (1.0 turn) | High (sporty) | Sports / Sports Sedan |
| 16 : 1 | 30° | 480° (1.33 turn) | Balanced | Compact Sedan |
| 18 : 1 | 30° | 540° (1.5 turn) | Comfort | Mid-size Sedan |
| 20 : 1 | 30° | 600° (1.67 turn) | Maximum comfort | Luxury / MPV |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how a **higher $i_s$** reduces $\theta_s$ for the same $\delta$, thereby requiring *fewer* hand turns for parking but trading off steering *feel* and responsiveness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Hand Torque T_input] --> B[Steering Wheel Rotation Theta_s]
    B --> C[Steering Column Transmission]
    C --> D[Steering Gearbox Ratio i_s]
    D --> E[Pitman Arm OR Rack Linear Displacement]
    E --> F[Tie Rod Push or Pull Force F_t]
    F --> G[Steered Wheel Angle Delta]
    G --> H[Tire Lateral Force F_y]
    H --> I[Vehicle Yaw Rate r_dot]
    I --> J[Lateral Acceleration a_y]
    J --> K[Driver Feedback Loop Close]

    subgraph S1[Measurement Layer]
        B
        G
        I
    end

    subgraph S2[Mechanical Amplification Chain]
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph S3[Vehicle Dynamics Output]
        H
        J
    end
```

### 4.2 Ackermann Steering Geometry — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NodeA[Vehicle Centre of Gravity CG] --> NodeB[Wheelbase L]
    NodeA --> NodeC[Track Width w]
    NodeB --> NodeD[Inner Wheel Steered Angle delta_i]
    NodeB --> NodeE[Outer Wheel Steered Angle delta_o]
    NodeC --> NodeF[Instantaneous Centre of Turn ICT]
    NodeD --> NodeF
    NodeE --> NodeF
    NodeF --> NodeG[Turning Radius R]
    NodeG --> NodeH[Steering Wheel Angle theta_s = i_s times average delta]
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Human–Vehicle Steering System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NodeRef[Reference Path Driver Intended Trajectory] --> NodeDriver[Driver Brain Central Nervous System]
    NodeDriver --> NodeHands[Hands Apply Torque to Wheel]
    NodeHands --> NodeWheel[Steering Wheel Angle theta_s]
    NodeWheel --> NodeGear[Steering Ratio i_s Multiplication]
    NodeGear --> NodeRoadWheel[Road Wheel Angle delta]
    NodeRoadWheel --> NodeTire[Tire Generates Lateral Force]
    NodeTire --> NodeYaw[Vehicle Yaw Response]
    NodeYaw --> NodePath[Actual Vehicle Path]
    NodePath --> NodeSensor[Visual and Vestibular Feedback]
    NodeSensor --> NodeDriver
```

### 4.4 Description of the Physical Phenomenon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The block diagrams above map the **cause-effect chain** of steering. Notice that the steering wheel angle $\theta_s$ is the **only controllable input** in the loop. All downstream effects — wheel angle, lateral force, yaw rate — are deterministic functions of $\theta_s$ (under steady-state, constant-speed assumptions). This is why KTU examiners treat $\theta_s$ as the **primary independent variable** in steering-related numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **steering wheel angle** and **steering ratio**. How are they mathematically related?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Steering wheel angle (θₛ)** is the angular displacement of the steering wheel from its straight-ahead neutral position, measured in degrees or radians. **Steering ratio (iₛ)** is the dimensionless ratio between the steering wheel angle and the average steered road wheel angle (δ). They are related by: $i_s = \theta_s / \delta$, i.e., $\theta_s = i_s \cdot \delta$. For a typical sedan with $i_s = 16$ and $\delta = 20°$, $\theta_s = 320°$. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark ratio definition + 1 mark numerical example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
State the **Ackermann condition**. Why is it important for steering wheel angle design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Ackermann condition states that, during a turn, all four wheels must rotate about a **common instantaneous centre** to avoid tire scrub. Mathematically: $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = w/L$. It is important because the inner and outer wheel angles are different; the steering geometry must be tuned so that the **driver's commanded θₛ** produces the correct differential $\delta_i$ and $\delta_o$, minimizing tire wear and ensuring safety. *(2 marks condition + 1 mark importance.)*

---

### PART B — Full ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *Steering Ratio & Wheel Angle Computation*

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper Adapted]**

A passenger car has a wheelbase of **2.65 m** and a track width of **1.52 m**. The overall steering ratio is **17.5 : 1**.

**(a)** The driver wishes to negotiate a turn of radius **R = 12 m**. Determine the average steered wheel angle (δ) and the corresponding **steering wheel angle (θₛ)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the Ackermann geometry, compute the **inner and outer wheel angles**. Also find the steering wheel torque required at the rim if the tie-rod force is **2200 N**, the tie-rod lever arm is **0.13 m**, and steering efficiency is **85%**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Using the bicycle-model turning radius relation:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\sin\delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L}{R}\right)
$$

$$
\delta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2.65}{12}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.2208) = 12.76°
$$

Apply the steering ratio:

$$
\theta_s = i_s \cdot \delta = 17.5 \times 12.76° = 223.3°
$$

Convert to turns: $223.3 / 360 = 0.62$ turns.

> **Marks:** $\delta$ derivation (3), $\theta_s$ computation (2), turn conversion (2).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Inner wheel (Ackermann):

$$
\delta_i = \cot^{-1}\!\left(\cot(12.76°) + \frac{1.52}{2 \times 2.65}\right)
$$

$$
\cot(12.76°) = 4.413, \quad \frac{w}{2L} = \frac{1.52}{5.30} = 0.2868
$$

$$
\delta_i = \cot^{-1}(4.413 + 0.2868) = \cot^{-1}(4.700) = 12.02°
$$

Outer wheel:

$$
\delta_o = \cot^{-1}(4.413 - 0.2868) = \cot^{-1}(4.126) = 13.62°
$$

Steering torque:

$$
T_s = \frac{F_t \cdot L_t}{i_s \cdot \eta} = \frac{2200 \times 0.13}{17.5 \times 0.85}
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{286}{14.875} = 19.23\,\text{Nm}
$$

> **Marks:** Inner (2), Outer (2), Torque (2), Verification (1).

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

A commercial vehicle has a wheelbase of **3.8 m**, track width **1.8 m**, and overall steering ratio **20 : 1**. The driver executes a lane change requiring a **peak lateral acceleration of 3 m/s²** at a vehicle speed of **60 km/h**.

**(a)** Calculate the peak average steering angle (δ) and the corresponding steering wheel angle (θₛ). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** If the maximum allowed hand torque on the steering wheel is **25 Nm** and steering efficiency is **80%**, determine the maximum sustainable tie-rod force. Comment on whether power assistance is required. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Vehicle speed: $v = 60\,\text{km/h} = 16.67\,\text{m/s}$.
From the bicycle model:

$$
a_y = \frac{v^2}{R} \Rightarrow R = \frac{v^2}{a_y} = \frac{(16.67)^2}{3} = \frac{277.8}{3} = 92.6\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\delta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L}{R}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{3.8}{92.6}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.04104) = 2.35°
$$

$$
\theta_s = 20 \times 2.35° = 47.0°
$$

> **Marks:** Speed conversion (1), Radius (2), δ (2), θₛ (2).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

From $T_s = F_t \cdot L_t / (i_s \cdot \eta)$:

$$
F_t = \frac{T_s \cdot i_s \cdot \eta}{L_t}
$$

Assume a typical tie-rod lever arm $L_t = 0.18\,\text{m}$ (heavy commercial vehicle):

$$
F_t = \frac{25 \times 20 \times 0.80}{0.18} = \frac{400}{0.18} = 2222.2\,\text{N}
$$

> **Conclusion:** At only 2.35° of road wheel angle, the tie-rod force remains modest. However, during **static parking maneuvers** (where $\delta$ can exceed 30°), $F_t$ can rise above **6000 N**, demanding a **hydraulic power-assisted steering (HPAS)** system. Thus, **power assistance is mandatory** for a commercial vehicle of this class.

> **Marks:** Force formula (2), Numerical substitution (2), Force magnitude (1), Parking analysis (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing rad and deg in the same equation. Always state the unit explicitly at the end of every numerical answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the average:** Road wheel angle δ in formulas is the **average** of inner and outer wheels. Do not directly substitute the inner Ackermann value.
> 3. **Caster and king-pin offsets ignored:** A real vehicle has τ ≠ 0, which slightly modifies δ. In Module 2 problems, this is usually neglected unless specified.
> 4. **Steering ratio inversion:** Some students write $i_s = \delta / \theta_s$. KTU expects $i_s = \theta_s / \delta$ by convention.
> 5. **Lock-to-lock confusion:** Lock-to-lock θₛ,total is approximately **2.5 × i_s** degrees for a full deflection, not $i_s$ itself.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Steering wheel angle θₛ** is the **primary driver input** in the steering chain.
- ✅ **Steering ratio** $i_s = \theta_s / \delta$ is the mechanical amplification between wheel and road wheel.
- ✅ Typical $i_s$ for passenger cars: **12 – 20** (lower = sportier feel).
- ✅ **Ackermann condition:** $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = w / L$ — ensures zero tire scrub.
- ✅ **Turning radius:** $R = L / \sin\delta$ (bicycle model).
- ✅ **Steering torque (manual):** $T_s = F_t \cdot L_t / (i_s \cdot \eta)$.
- ✅ **Power-assist limit:** Driver hand torque ≤ **50 Nm** (PAL ergonomic limit per ISO 4040).
- ✅ Modern **steer-by-wire** systems eliminate the mechanical linkage but **retain** the θₛ → δ mapping electronically.
- ✅ Always convert **km/h to m/s** before applying dynamic formulas.
- ✅ Always specify units (**°** or **rad**) at the end of every numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Automotive Comfort and Safety Engineering**, **velocity** is defined as the time rate of change of an object's position with respect to a chosen reference frame. For a vehicle, velocity is a vector quantity that fully describes the *magnitude* (speed) and the *direction* of motion. In the context of this module, velocity is treated not merely as a kinematic variable, but as the **primary independent variable** governing both **ride comfort** (through velocity spectra, jerk, and ride profiles) and **occupant safety** (through crash velocity, Delta-V, and closing velocity).

Mathematically, the instantaneous velocity vector of a particle (or vehicle mass-center) is expressed as:

$$
\vec{v}(t) = \frac{d\vec{r}(t)}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\vec{r}(t+\Delta t)-\vec{r}(t)}{\Delta t}
$$

where $\vec{r}(t)$ is the position vector and $t$ is time. The scalar speed is $v = \vert \vec{v} \vert = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In automotive applications, velocity is **never treated as a single scalar**. Vehicles operate in a 3-D coordinate system (longitudinal, lateral, vertical) and a 3-D rotational system (roll, pitch, yaw). Therefore, velocity in comfort/safety analysis is a **six-component state vector**: $(v_x, v_y, v_z, p, q, r)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a car travelling on a highway:

- The **speedometer reading** is the *scalar speed* — it tells you only *how fast* the car is moving, not the direction.
- The **true velocity**, however, is the complete "story" — it includes whether the car is also drifting sideways due to a crosswind, bouncing up and down over a speed bump, or rotating as it enters a curve.

In **safety**, the velocity at the moment of impact is what determines whether the occupants survive. Two crashes with the *same speed* but different *closing angles* produce vastly different injuries because velocity is a vector.

In **comfort**, it is the *change* in velocity (and especially the *rate of change of acceleration*, called **jerk**) that passengers perceive, not speed alone. That is why smooth velocity profiles — even at high cruise speeds — feel comfortable, while jerky low-speed city driving feels uncomfortable.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$ (used in crash pulse normalisation).
- **Standard reference velocity for comfort tests:** often $v_{\text{ref}} = 60\ \text{km/h}$ to $120\ \text{km/h}$.
- **Crash-test standard impact velocities (Euro NCAP / AIS):** $v_{\text{impact}} = 50\ \text{km/h}$, $64\ \text{km/h}$ frontal offset.
- **Delta-V** is reported in **km/h** or **m/s** depending on jurisdiction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** *Speed* is a scalar; *velocity* is a vector. Many board-exam answers lose marks by treating them as the same. Always mention direction or reference frame when discussing velocity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Vehicle Velocity

For a rigid body (vehicle) moving in 3-D space, the velocity state is fully described by the **six degrees of freedom (6-DOF)** velocity vector.

#### A. Translational Velocities (linear, m/s)

1. **Longitudinal velocity $v_x$** — along the vehicle's roll axis (forward/backward). This is what the speedometer reads when the car moves straight on level ground.
2. **Lateral velocity $v_y$** — across the vehicle (left/right). Generated during lane changes, evasive manoeuvres, and skidding.
3. **Vertical velocity $v_z$** — along the vehicle's yaw axis (up/down). Generated by road undulations, causing the sprung mass to bounce.

#### B. Angular Velocities (rotational, rad/s)

4. **Roll rate $p$** — rotation about the longitudinal axis $X$.
5. **Pitch rate $q$** — rotation about the lateral axis $Y$.
6. **Yaw rate $r$** — rotation about the vertical axis $Z$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Module 2:** A comfort study needs $v_z$, $p$, and $q$ (vertical and rotational excitations). A safety study needs $v_x$, $v_y$, and $r$ (the dominant crash and handling variables).

### 2.2 Kinematic Relationships — The "Why" Behind the Equations

1. **Velocity is the integral of acceleration.** If you know the accelerometer trace (which is what an **inertial measurement unit (IMU)** records), you recover velocity by integration:
   
   $$
   v(t) = v(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} a(\tau)\, d\tau
   $$

2. **Velocity is the derivative of displacement.** Knowing the position history (from GPS or wheel odometry) lets you compute velocity by differentiation:
   
   $$
   v(t) = \frac{ds}{dt}
   $$

3. **Average velocity is the displacement divided by elapsed time:**
   
   $$
   \bar{v} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t} = \frac{s(t_2) - s(t_1)}{t_2 - t_1}
   $$

4. **The velocity-energy relationship** is the **single most important safety equation in this module.** A vehicle's kinetic energy grows with the *square* of velocity:
   
   $$
   E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
   $$

   Doubling speed **quadruples** the crash energy. This non-linearity is why a 60 km/h crash is not "twice as bad" as a 30 km/h crash — it is **four times** as bad.

5. **Delta-V ($\Delta v$)** is the change in velocity experienced by a vehicle during a crash. It is the **gold-standard injury predictor** in modern biomechanics:
   
   $$
   \Delta v = \vert \vec{v}_{\text{after}} - \vec{v}_{\text{before}} \vert
   $$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $\vec{v} = d\vec{r}/dt$ | Instantaneous velocity vector | m/s |
| 2 | $v = ds/dt$ | Scalar speed along a path | m/s |
| 3 | $\bar{v} = \Delta s / \Delta t$ | Average velocity over interval | m/s |
| 4 | $v(t) = v_0 + \int a\, dt$ | Velocity from acceleration | m/s |
| 5 | $s(t) = s_0 + \int v\, dt$ | Displacement from velocity | m |
| 6 | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Translational kinetic energy | J |
| 7 | $\Delta v = \vert \vec{v}_f - \vec{v}_i \vert$ | Delta-V (crash severity index) | m/s |
| 8 | $v_{\text{closing}} = v_1 + v_2$ | Closing velocity in head-on collision | m/s |
| 9 | $\omega = d\theta/dt$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| 10 | $v = r\omega$ | Linear velocity from angular velocity | m/s |
| 11 | $E_{\text{rot}} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Rotational kinetic energy | J |
| 12 | $a_c = v^2 / R$ | Centripetal acceleration (cornering) | m/s$^2$ |
| 13 | $a_{\text{tan}} = r\alpha$ | Tangential acceleration | m/s$^2$ |
| 14 | $v = v_0 + a t$ | Uniform acceleration (1-D) | m/s |
| 15 | $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s$ | Velocity-displacement (1-D) | m$^2$/s$^2$ |
| 16 | $v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2\, dt}$ | RMS velocity (comfort metric) | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table-Format Reminder:** All absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser safe. Do not use literal `\vert x \vert` with bare pipes inside a row.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crashworthiness design:** The *crash pulse* (acceleration vs. time) is integrated to obtain velocity vs. time; from that, $\Delta v$ is read off directly. The *crumple zone* is sized so that $\Delta v$ builds up gradually, keeping the deceleration below the human injury threshold (~**20–25 g** for short durations).
- **Ride comfort:** $v_{\text{rms}}$ over the frequency range 0.5–80 Hz correlates well with subjective discomfort scores in **ISO 2631**.
- **ABS / ESC tuning:** Wheel-velocity sensors feed the ECU at 1000 Hz; slip ratio $s = (v - \omega r)/v$ controls brake force.
- **Airbag deployment:** Algorithms use the *velocity change rate* ($d\Delta v / dt$, i.e., the deceleration) and the *crush velocity* to fire the inflator at the optimal moment (~**15–30 ms** after impact).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Acceleration Trace to Velocity Trace

**Statement:** Given an acceleration history $a(t)$ and an initial velocity $v_0$, derive the velocity at any time $t$.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of acceleration.** Acceleration is the time-rate of change of velocity:

$$
a(t) = \frac{dv(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the differential equation.** Multiply both sides by $dt$:

$$
dv(t) = a(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from the initial instant $t_0$ to an arbitrary instant $t$.** The limits of integration on the left change the velocity from $v_0$ to $v(t)$:

$$
\int_{v_0}^{v(t)} dv = \int_{t_0}^{t} a(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the left integral.** The integral of $dv$ is just $v$:

$$
v(t) - v_0 = \int_{t_0}^{t} a(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v(t)$.** Move $v_0$ to the right-hand side:

$$
v(t) = v_0 + \int_{t_0}^{t} a(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *fundamental equation* of every crash-data analysis. An event-data recorder (EDR) stores $a(t)$ sampled at, say, 10 kHz; the engineer numerically integrates to recover $v(t)$ and then $\Delta v$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Delta-V from a Triangular Crash Pulse

**Statement:** A vehicle undergoes a frontal collision. The crash pulse is idealised as a triangular acceleration profile: $a(t)$ rises linearly from **0** at $t=0$ to a peak $a_{\max}$ at $t = t_p$, then drops linearly to **0** at $t = t_e$. Derive the Delta-V.

**Step 1 — Write the analytical form of $a(t)$ for the rising segment ($0 \le t \le t_p$).** The line from $(0,0)$ to $(t_p, a_{\max})$ has slope $a_{\max}/t_p$:

$$
a_1(t) = \frac{a_{\max}}{t_p}\, t, \qquad 0 \le t \le t_p
$$

**Step 2 — Write the analytical form of $a(t)$ for the falling segment ($t_p \le t \le t_e$).** The line from $(t_p, a_{\max})$ to $(t_e, 0)$ has slope $-a_{\max}/(t_e - t_p)$:

$$
a_2(t) = a_{\max}\left(1 - \frac{t - t_p}{t_e - t_p}\right), \qquad t_p \le t \le t_e
$$

**Step 3 — Set up the velocity integral.** Velocity is the time integral of acceleration. The Delta-V is the total change, i.e. $v(t_e) - v(0)$ assuming $v(0) = 0$ in the vehicle frame:

$$
\Delta v = \int_0^{t_e} a(t)\, dt = \int_0^{t_p} a_1(t)\, dt + \int_{t_p}^{t_e} a_2(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the first integral (rising triangle).** Integrate $a_1(t)$ from 0 to $t_p$:

$$
\int_0^{t_p} \frac{a_{\max}}{t_p}\, t\, dt = \frac{a_{\max}}{t_p} \cdot \frac{t_p^2}{2} = \frac{a_{\max}\, t_p}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the second integral (falling triangle).** Let $u = t - t_p$, so $du = dt$ and the limits run from $0$ to $(t_e - t_p)$:

$$
\int_0^{t_e - t_p} a_{\max}\left(1 - \frac{u}{t_e - t_p}\right) du
$$

**Step 6 — Split the integrand and integrate term by term.** The integral of a constant is the constant times the interval; the integral of $u$ is $u^2/2$:

$$
a_{\max}\left[u - \frac{u^2}{2(t_e - t_p)}\right]_0^{t_e - t_p} = a_{\max}\left[(t_e - t_p) - \frac{t_e - t_p}{2}\right] = \frac{a_{\max}(t_e - t_p)}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Add the two integrals.** This is simply the area of the triangle (1/2 × base × height):

$$
\Delta v = \frac{a_{\max}\, t_p}{2} + \frac{a_{\max}(t_e - t_p)}{2} = \frac{a_{\max}\, t_e}{2}
$$

**Step 8 — State the result in words.** For a triangular crash pulse, Delta-V equals **one-half the peak deceleration times the total pulse duration**. Engineers use this quick estimate during preliminary crashworthiness design.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stopping Distance from Initial Velocity

**Statement:** A vehicle travelling at initial velocity $v_0$ brakes uniformly with deceleration $a$ (where $a < 0$ in the sign convention of motion). Derive the stopping distance $s_{\text{stop}}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the kinematic equation linking velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and displacement:**

$$
v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s
$$

**Step 2 — Set the final velocity to zero (the vehicle has stopped):**

$$
0 = v_0^2 + 2 a s_{\text{stop}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $s_{\text{stop}}$:**

$$
s_{\text{stop}} = -\frac{v_0^2}{2a}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the magnitude (since stopping distance is positive) and substitute the magnitude of deceleration $a_{\text{dec}} = \vert a \vert$:**

$$
s_{\text{stop}} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{\text{dec}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Insert a numerical check.** If $v_0 = 27.78\ \text{m/s}$ (i.e. $100\ \text{km/h}$) and $a_{\text{dec}} = 7\ \text{m/s}^2$ (typical emergency braking):

$$
s_{\text{stop}} = \frac{(27.78)^2}{2 \times 7} = \frac{771.7}{14} \approx 55.1\ \text{m}
$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the speed **quadruples** the stopping distance. This is the same non-linearity that makes high-speed crashes so dangerous, and is why velocity is the dominant design variable in both safety (braking, crash energy) and comfort (low-speed ride quality).

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Closing Velocity in a Head-On Collision

**Statement:** Two vehicles of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ approach each other head-on with speeds $v_1$ and $v_2$. Derive an expression for the *closing velocity* and the *relative kinetic energy* of approach.

**Step 1 — Choose a sign convention.** Let vehicle 1 move in the $+x$ direction with velocity $+v_1$, and vehicle 2 move in the $-x$ direction with velocity $-v_2$.

**Step 2 — Closing velocity is the rate at which the gap between them shrinks.** It is the magnitude of the relative velocity vector:

$$
v_{\text{closing}} = \vert \vec{v}_1 - \vec{v}_2 \vert = \vert (+v_1) - (-v_2) \vert = v_1 + v_2
$$

**Step 3 — Relative kinetic energy** is the kinetic energy in the centre-of-mass frame. First, find the velocity of the centre of mass:

$$
v_{\text{cm}} = \frac{m_1 v_1 + m_2 (-v_2)}{m_1 + m_2}
$$

**Step 4 — The relative velocity of vehicle 1 with respect to the centre of mass is:**

$$
v_{1,\text{rel}} = v_1 - v_{\text{cm}} = v_1 - \frac{m_1 v_1 - m_2 v_2}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{m_2 (v_1 + v_2)}{m_1 + m_2}
$$

**Step 5 — The total kinetic energy in the centre-of-mass frame is:**

$$
E_{\text{rel}} = \frac{1}{2} m_1 v_{1,\text{rel}}^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 v_{2,\text{rel}}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Recognise the standard result** (reduced-mass form). The relative kinetic energy equals one-half times the *reduced mass* times the *square of the closing velocity*:

$$
E_{\text{rel}} = \frac{1}{2}\, \mu\, v_{\text{closing}}^2, \qquad \mu = \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 50 km/h car hitting a stationary object experiences the same crash energy as a 25 km/h car hitting another 25 km/h car head-on, because the *closing velocity* in both cases is 50 km/h. This is why the safety literature always quotes closing velocity, not individual vehicle speed.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Problem Types

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crash energy | $m$, $v$ | $E_k$ | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ |
| Stopping distance | $v_0$, $a_{\text{dec}}$ | $s_{\text{stop}}$ | $s_{\text{stop}} = v_0^2 / (2a_{\text{dec}})$ |
| Delta-V from pulse | $a(t)$ | $\Delta v$ | $\Delta v = \int a\, dt$ |
| Closing velocity | $v_1$, $v_2$ | $v_{\text{closing}}$ | $v_{\text{closing}} = v_1 + v_2$ (head-on) |
| Cornering acceleration | $v$, $R$ | $a_c$ | $a_c = v^2 / R$ |
| RMS velocity | $v(t)$ | $v_{\text{rms}}$ | $v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\langle v^2 \rangle}$ |
| Energy absorbed by crumple zone | $E_k$, $E_{\text{rebound}}$ | $E_{\text{abs}}$ | $E_{\text{abs}} = E_k - E_{\text{rebound}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Velocity Computation Pipeline in a Vehicle ECU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel Speed Sensors] --> B[Raw Pulse Train]
    C[Inertial Measurement Unit IMU] --> D[Raw Acceleration Signal a t]
    E[GPS Receiver] --> F[Ground Truth Speed]
    B --> G1[Signal Conditioning and Filtering]
    D --> G1
    F --> G1
    G1 --> H[Sensor Fusion Block]
    H --> I1[Longitudinal Velocity vx]
    H --> I2[Lateral Velocity vy]
    H --> I3[Vertical Velocity vz]
    H --> I4[Roll Rate p]
    H --> I5[Pitch Rate q]
    H --> I6[Yaw Rate r]
    I1 --> J1[Powertrain Control]
    I1 --> J2[ABS and ESC Module]
    I1 --> J3[Airbag Decision Algorithm]
    I2 --> J2
    I3 --> J4[Ride Comfort Estimator ISO 2631]
    I4 --> J4
    I5 --> J4
    I6 --> J5[Electronic Stability Control]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The vehicle estimates its full 6-DOF velocity state from three independent sensor sources — wheel-speed encoders, the IMU, and GPS. Each velocity component is then routed to the relevant subsystem. Note that **$v_x$ alone drives four different safety and comfort modules** — it is the single most important velocity component in this module.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: From Crash to Delta-V

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Impact Event Occurs] --> K2[Accelerometer Records a of t]
    K2 --> K3[Numerical Integration over Time]
    K3 --> K4[Velocity Time History v of t]
    K4 --> K5[Read v at t equals 0 minus v at t equals t final]
    K5 --> K6[Delta V magnitude in m per s]
    K6 --> K7[Compare to Injury Risk Curve AIS]
    K6 --> K8[Compare to Regulatory Limit ECE R94]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every modern Event Data Recorder (EDR) follows this exact pipeline. The Delta-V value is the principal output used in post-crash injury assessment.

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: Velocity Sub-Topics in Module 2

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((Velocity in Automotive Comfort and Safety))
        Translational
            Longitudinal vx
            Lateral vy
            Vertical vz
        Rotational
            Roll rate p
            Pitch rate q
            Yaw rate r
        Safety Metrics
            Crash Velocity
            Delta V
            Closing Velocity
            Crash Energy Ek
        Comfort Metrics
            RMS Velocity
            Velocity Spectrum
            ISO 2631 Weighted Vrms
        Kinematic Relations
            v equals integral of a dt
            a equals dv dt
            v squared equals v0 squared plus 2as
        Energy Relations
            Ek equals one half m v squared
            Erot equals one half I omega squared
            Closing Energy Formula
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**Define velocity. Differentiate between average velocity and instantaneous velocity as applied to a moving vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Velocity** is the time rate of change of position of a vehicle's mass-centre with respect to a reference frame. It is a vector quantity with SI unit m/s.
- (1 mark) **Average velocity** is the total displacement divided by the total time taken: $\bar{v} = \Delta s / \Delta t$. For a car covering 100 km in 2 hours, $\bar{v} = 50\ \text{km/h}$ regardless of stops or speed variations.
- (1 mark) **Instantaneous velocity** is the limit of the average velocity as the time interval shrinks to zero: $v(t) = ds/dt$. It is the value the speedometer reads at a given instant. The two are equal only when the motion is uniform.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is Delta-V? Why is it considered the most reliable crash severity index in occupant safety analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Delta-V** is defined as the magnitude of the change in velocity of the vehicle's mass-centre during a crash: $\Delta v = \vert \vec{v}_f - \vec{v}_i \vert$. Units: m/s or km/h.
- (1 mark) It is the **change** in velocity, not the impact speed itself, that the human body experiences. Two crashes at the same impact speed can have very different $\Delta v$ values (e.g. hitting a rigid wall vs. a yielding barrier).
- (1 mark) It correlates directly with the **AIS (Abbreviated Injury Scale)** and the **HIC (Head Injury Criterion)**. Regulatory standards such as **ECE R94** and **Euro NCAP** use $\Delta v$ thresholds to certify vehicle crashworthiness.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, PEAUT861]**

**(a)** A car of mass **1500 kg** is travelling at a uniform velocity of **72 km/h** on a level road. Suddenly the driver sees an obstacle and applies the brakes uniformly, bringing the car to rest in **4 seconds**. Calculate:
   (i) the initial velocity in m/s, (ii) the deceleration, (iii) the stopping distance, and (iv) the kinetic energy dissipated by the brakes. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches how velocity and Delta-V are used to evaluate crash severity in modern automotive safety analysis. Discuss the relationship between velocity and crash energy, and state why doubling the speed quadruples the crash energy. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Step-by-step:**

- (i) Convert 72 km/h to m/s by multiplying by 1000/3600 = 1/3.6:

  $$
  v_0 = \frac{72}{3.6} = 20\ \text{m/s}
  $$

- (ii) Uniform deceleration. Use $v = v_0 + a t$ with $v = 0$ and $t = 4$ s:

  $$
  0 = 20 + a (4) \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = -\frac{20}{4} = -5\ \text{m/s}^2
  $$

  Deceleration magnitude is **5 m/s²** (≈ **0.51 g**).

- (iii) Stopping distance using $s = v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$:

  $$
  s = (20)(4) + \tfrac{1}{2}(-5)(4)^2 = 80 - 40 = 40\ \text{m}
  $$

  Cross-check using $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s$:

  $$
  0 = 400 + 2(-5)s \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = 40\ \text{m} \quad \checkmark
  $$

- (iv) Kinetic energy dissipated = initial kinetic energy (final KE is zero since the car is at rest):

  $$
  E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1500)(20)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1500)(400) = 300{,}000\ \text{J} = 300\ \text{kJ}
  $$

  *(Alternative: average braking force × stopping distance. Average force = $m \cdot \vert a \vert = 1500 \times 5 = 7500$ N. Energy = $7500 \times 40 = 300{,}000$ J. Same answer.)*

  **\[7 marks distributed: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2\]**

**(b) — Concept explanation:**

Velocity is the primary independent variable in crashworthiness analysis. The crash severity is judged not by the impact velocity alone, but by the **change in velocity (Delta-V)** that the vehicle's mass-centre undergoes during the impact event.

- **Crash severity evaluation pipeline (sketch description):** A crash test dummy is instrumented; on-board accelerometers record $a(t)$ at high sampling rate; this is integrated to obtain the velocity profile $v(t)$; the difference between pre- and post-impact velocities gives $\Delta v$.
- **Velocity–energy relationship:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$. Energy is proportional to $v^2$.
- **Why doubling speed quadruples energy:** Substitute $v_2 = 2 v_1$ into the energy equation:

  $$
  E_{k,2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m (2v_1)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} m \cdot 4 v_1^2 = 4 E_{k,1}
  $$

  This quadratic dependence is the single most important design constraint on vehicle speed limits, brake systems, and crumple-zone sizing. Modern safety design uses *managed deceleration* — the crumple zone deforms over a designed distance and time so that the *rate* of velocity change (i.e. the deceleration, and hence the *jerk*) is kept within human-tolerance limits.

  **\[7 marks distributed: 2 for sketch and pipeline, 2 for velocity-energy relationship, 2 for the squaring proof, 1 for design implication\]**

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Past Paper Pattern, PEAUT861]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Delta-V of a vehicle undergoing a frontal crash, given a triangular crash pulse with peak deceleration $a_{\max}$ and total pulse duration $t_e$. Assume the pulse rises linearly from 0 to $a_{\max}$ in time $t_p$ and then falls linearly back to 0 in time $(t_e - t_p)$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Two cars of masses **1000 kg** and **1500 kg** approach each other head-on, each at a speed of **54 km/h**.
   (i) Find the closing velocity.
   (ii) Find the velocity of the centre of mass of the system.
   (iii) Compute the relative kinetic energy of approach using the reduced-mass formula.
   (iv) If the cars collide and stick together (perfectly plastic collision), find the common velocity after impact. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation:**

- Step 1: Write the rising-segment acceleration: $a_1(t) = (a_{\max}/t_p)\, t$.
- Step 2: Write the falling-segment acceleration: $a_2(t) = a_{\max}\bigl[1 - (t-t_p)/(t_e - t_p)\bigr]$.
- Step 3: Delta-V is the integral of acceleration over the pulse:

  $$
  \Delta v = \int_0^{t_p} a_1(t)\, dt + \int_{t_p}^{t_e} a_2(t)\, dt
  $$

- Step 4: First integral:

  $$
  \int_0^{t_p} \frac{a_{\max}}{t_p}\, t\, dt = \frac{a_{\max}\, t_p}{2}
  $$

- Step 5: Second integral (geometric interpretation: area of a triangle of base $(t_e - t_p)$ and height $a_{\max}$):

  $$
  \int_{t_p}^{t_e} a_2(t)\, dt = \frac{a_{\max}(t_e - t_p)}{2}
  $$

- Step 6: Add the two:

  $$
  \Delta v = \frac{a_{\max}\, t_p}{2} + \frac{a_{\max}(t_e - t_p)}{2} = \frac{a_{\max}\, t_e}{2}
  $$

  **Final result:** $\Delta v = \tfrac{1}{2}\, a_{\max}\, t_e$.

  **\[7 marks distributed: 1 each for steps 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 1 for step 6 result\]**

**(b) — Numerical solution:**

- (i) Convert 54 km/h to m/s: $v = 54/3.6 = 15\ \text{m/s}$. Closing velocity in a head-on collision is the sum:

  $$
  v_{\text{closing}} = 15 + 15 = 30\ \text{m/s} = 108\ \text{km/h}
  $$

- (ii) Centre-of-mass velocity. Take car 1 moving in $+x$ and car 2 in $-x$:

  $$
  v_{\text{cm}} = \frac{m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{(1000)(15) + (1500)(-15)}{1000 + 1500} = \frac{15000 - 22500}{2500} = \frac{-7500}{2500} = -3\ \text{m/s}
  $$

  The centre of mass moves in the $-x$ direction at **3 m/s** (i.e. in the original direction of the heavier car).

- (iii) Reduced mass and relative kinetic energy:

  $$
  \mu = \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{(1000)(1500)}{2500} = 600\ \text{kg}
  $$

  $$
  E_{\text{rel}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu\, v_{\text{closing}}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(600)(30)^2 = 300 \times 900 = 270{,}000\ \text{J} = 270\ \text{kJ}
  $$

- (iv) Perfectly plastic collision. By conservation of momentum:

  $$
  (m_1 + m_2) v_f = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2
  $$

  $$
  v_f = \frac{(1000)(15) + (1500)(-15)}{2500} = -3\ \text{m/s}
  $$

  The common post-impact velocity is **$-3$ m/s** (i.e. 3 m/s in the original direction of the 1500 kg car). Energy dissipated in the crash = total KE before − total KE after:

  $$
  E_{\text{before}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1000)(15)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(1500)(15)^2 = 112{,}500 + 168{,}750 = 281{,}250\ \text{J}
  $$

  $$
  E_{\text{after}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(2500)(3)^2 = 11{,}250\ \text{J}
  $$

  $$
  E_{\text{dissipated}} = 281{,}250 - 11{,}250 = 270{,}000\ \text{J} = 270\ \text{kJ}
  $$

  Note that the dissipated energy equals $E_{\text{rel}}$ from part (iii) — a classic check.

  **\[7 marks distributed: 1 + 1 + 2 + 3\]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points**
> 1. **Treating speed and velocity as the same.** The exam often asks "velocity", and many students write only the magnitude. Always quote both magnitude *and* direction (or reference frame). **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion from km/h to m/s.** A common slip in numericals. The conversion factor is **1/3.6**. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Sign convention in Delta-V.** Delta-V is the *magnitude* of the velocity change. Do not leave a negative sign in the final answer. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Linear vs. rotational kinetic energy.** When the question says a wheel is rotating, you must add $E_{\text{rot}} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ to the translational $E_k$. Students frequently forget this. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 5. **Confusing average and instantaneous velocity** in Part A. Always state the limit definition for instantaneous velocity. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 6. **Skipping the integration step in derivation questions.** A KTU derivation must show the *integration limits* explicitly, not just the final formula. **(Lose 2–3 marks)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Velocity is a vector**, speed is a scalar — always quote direction or reference frame.
- **Six-component velocity state** in vehicle dynamics: $(v_x, v_y, v_z, p, q, r)$.
- **Velocity is the integral of acceleration**: $v(t) = v_0 + \int a\, dt$ — this is the foundation of all crash-data analysis.
- **Velocity is the derivative of displacement**: $v = ds/dt$ — the basis of GPS-based and odometry-based speed estimation.
- **Kinetic energy scales with $v^2$**: $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$. Doubling speed = 4× crash energy; tripling = 9×.
- **Delta-V ($\Delta v$) is the gold-standard crash severity metric** in safety engineering and biomechanics.
- **Closing velocity** in a head-on collision is $v_1 + v_2$, not $|v_1 - v_2|$.
- **Relative kinetic energy** in a two-body collision is $\tfrac{1}{2}\mu v_{\text{closing}}^2$, where $\mu$ is the reduced mass.
- **Centripetal acceleration** during cornering: $a_c = v^2 / R$. Higher speed → tighter cornering demands more grip.
- **Angular velocity $\omega$** is to rotation what $v$ is to translation. The relation $v = r\omega$ links the two.
- **RMS velocity** is the comfort-domain metric; peak velocity is the safety-domain metric.
- **Uniform-acceleration kinematic triplets** to memorise:
  - $v = v_0 + at$
  - $s = v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$
  - $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s$
- **Standard conversions**: 1 km/h = 1/3.6 m/s; 1 g = 9.80665 m/s²; 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h.
- **Triangular crash pulse shortcut**: $\Delta v = \tfrac{1}{2} a_{\max} t_e$ — useful for quick design estimates.
- **Plastic-collision result**: post-impact common velocity equals the centre-of-mass velocity of the system before impact — this is also the maximum recoverable momentum check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Task Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Task Time** in the context of Automotive Comfort and Safety Engineering is defined as the total elapsed duration required by a vehicle occupant (primarily the driver) to perceive a stimulus, cognitively process the required action, physically execute the corresponding motor response, and confirm the completion of a predefined operational subtask within the driver-vehicle interface (DVI).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
T_{task} = T_{perception} + T_{cognition} + T_{motor} + T_{verification}
$$

Where each component represents a distinct phase in the human information processing model (perception–cognition–motor response), applied to in-vehicle human-machine interaction (HMI) scenarios such as activating a wiper, adjusting HVAC controls, or initiating a turn signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Task Time is a fundamental ergonomic metric used to quantify driver workload, evaluate control reachability, validate HMI design against regulatory benchmarks (e.g., **ISO 15005**, **SAE J2364**, **NHTSA Driver Distraction Guidelines**), and certify the comfort and safety quotient of an automotive cockpit layout.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving at 60 km/h on a busy road and want to turn on the windshield wiper. Your brain has to:

1. **See** the rain on the windshield (Perception)
2. **Decide** to turn on the wiper and identify the right stalk (Cognition)
3. **Reach and push** the stalk (Motor Action)
4. **See/hear** that the wiper is now working (Verification)

The total time from "I noticed the rain" to "I confirmed the wiper is moving" is the **Task Time**. If this time is too long (typically > **2.0 seconds** for secondary controls, per NHTSA guidelines), the driver's eyes linger off the road, increasing crash risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Task Time = "How long does it take the driver to do something inside the car while driving?" — measured in seconds, used to judge if the car's interior is comfortable and safe to operate.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Source |
|--------|----------------|--------|
| Single glance duration | ≤ **2.0 s** | NHTSA Visual-Manual Guidelines |
| Total task time (secondary) | ≤ **12.0 s** (preferred) | Alliance of Automobile Manufacturers |
| Mean simple reaction time | **0.25 s** (visual), **0.17 s** (auditory) | Human Factors literature |
| Eye glance frequency | ≤ **6 glances** per task | NHTSA |
| Standard MTMA reach envelope | **0.6 – 0.7 s** (within easy reach) | SAE J287 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of Task Time

Task Time is not a single monolithic value — it is a **cascade of four sequential sub-processes**:

#### Stage 1: Perception Time ($T_{perception}$)
- The latency between stimulus onset and its registration by the sensory organs (eyes for displays, ears for buzzers, skin for haptic feedback).
- Driven by stimulus intensity, contrast, luminance, and ambient conditions.
- Typical range: **0.10 – 0.30 s**.

#### Stage 2: Cognitive (Decision) Time ($T_{cognition}$)
- Brain processes the perceived information, selects the appropriate response from long-term memory (LTM), and issues a motor command.
- Governed by the **Hick-Hyman Law**: more alternatives → more decision time.
- Typical range: **0.20 – 0.50 s**.

#### Stage 3: Motor Execution Time ($T_{motor}$)
- The physical limb movement — reaching, grasping, pushing, twisting, sliding.
- Quantified using **Fitts' Law** (pointing) or **MTM** (Methods-Time Measurement).
- Typical range: **0.30 – 1.50 s**, highly dependent on control location.

#### Stage 4: Verification Time ($T_{verification}$)
- Feedback loop where the driver confirms the action took effect (visual confirmation on cluster, tactile click, auditory beep).
- Typical range: **0.10 – 0.40 s**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable in Part A and Part B of KTU ESE under this module.

| # | Formula / Law | Equation | Domain of Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Task Time** | $T_{task} = T_{perception} + T_{cognition} + T_{motor} + T_{verification}$ | Ergonomic evaluation of any in-vehicle task | seconds (s) |
| 2 | **Hick-Hyman Law** | $T_{cognition} = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | Decision time with $n$ equally probable choices | s |
| 3 | **Fitts' Law (Pointing)** | $T_{motor} = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$ | Time to acquire a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ | s |
| 4 | **MTM Reach (R)** | $T_{motor}^{reach} \approx 0.08 \text{ to } 0.12 \text{ s}$ (per 5 cm increment) | Reaching for cockpit controls | s |
| 5 | **Simple Reaction Time** | $T_{R} = 0.25$ s (visual) ; $0.17$ s (auditory) | Baseline stimulus-response latency | s |
| 6 | **Total Eyes-Off-Road Time** | $T_{EOR} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{glance,i}$ | Glance-based driver distraction metric | s |
| 7 | **MTM Basic Motion: Move (M)** | $T_{M} = 0.08 + 0.064 \cdot d$ (d in cm, ≤ 50 cm) | Standard Methods-Time Measurement motion | TMU (1 TMU = 0.036 s) |
| 8 | **Workload Index Proxy** | $W_{proxy} = \dfrac{T_{task}}{T_{threshold}}$ | Ratio > 1 indicates unsafe workload | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Automotive OEMs (Toyota, Ford, Tata, Mahindra) use Task Time analysis during Digital Mock-Up (DMU) and Package Engineering to optimize control placement, minimize reach distances, and validate designs against NHTSA's **Phase 1 Distraction Guidelines** before physical prototyping.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Task Time Model

**Starting Premise:** Human information processing, when applied to an in-vehicle operation, can be modeled as a serial pipeline of four stages, each with its own latency. Let each stage be independent.

#### Step 1 — Define the Stage Latencies
- Let $T_P$ = perception time
- Let $T_C$ = cognition (decision) time
- Let $T_M$ = motor (movement) time
- Let $T_V$ = verification (feedback) time

#### Step 2 — Assume Serial (Non-Overlapping) Processing
In safety-critical driving tasks, parallel processing is unsafe (eyes-off-road glances are sequential). Therefore, the total elapsed time is the arithmetic sum.

$$
T_{task} = T_P + T_C + T_M + T_V
$$

This is the **composite Task Time equation**.

#### Step 3 — Substitute Each Stage with Its Governing Law

**Perception** (for a typical visual cluster display):

$$
T_P \approx 0.10 + 0.020 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{C}\right) \quad \text{(s)}
$$

where $C$ is the contrast ratio between the display and the ambient cockpit.

**Cognition** (Hick-Hyman Law for $n$ equally probable responses):

$$
T_C = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)
$$

For a typical driver, empirical values are $a = 0.155$ s and $b = 0.145$ s.

**Motor** (Fitts' Law for a control of width $W$ at distance $D$):

$$
T_M = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

Empirically, $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.10$ s.

**Verification** (one visual confirmation glance):

$$
T_V \approx 0.25 \text{ to } 0.40 \text{ s}
$$

#### Step 4 — Form the Fully Expanded Composite

$$
T_{task} = \underbrace{0.10 + \frac{0.020}{C}}_{\text{Perception}} + \underbrace{0.155 + 0.145\log_2(n+1)}_{\text{Cognition}} + \underbrace{0.05 + 0.10\log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)}_{\text{Motor}} + \underbrace{0.30}_{\text{Verification}}
$$

#### Step 5 — Simplify with Representative Constants
Insert typical values: $C = 0.8$, $n = 4$, $D = 0.40$ m, $W = 0.02$ m.

**Numerical Evaluation (worked example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_P &= 0.10 + \frac{0.020}{0.8} = 0.10 + 0.025 = 0.125 \text{ s} \\
T_C &= 0.155 + 0.145 \cdot \log_2(4+1) = 0.155 + 0.145 \cdot \log_2(5) \\
    &= 0.155 + 0.145 \cdot 2.3219 = 0.155 + 0.3367 = 0.4917 \text{ s} \\
T_M &= 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \cdot 0.40}{0.02}\right) = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot \log_2(40) \\
    &= 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot 5.3219 = 0.05 + 0.5322 = 0.5822 \text{ s} \\
T_V &= 0.30 \text{ s} \\
T_{task} &= 0.125 + 0.4917 + 0.5822 + 0.30 = 1.4989 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{task} \approx 1.50 \text{ s}}
$$

#### Step 6 — Interpretation against Regulatory Threshold
Since **1.50 s < 2.00 s** (NHTSA single-task ceiling), this control qualifies as "low-distraction." If the control had been placed further away (say $D = 0.70$ m), recomputing $T_M$ would yield $0.05 + 0.10 \cdot \log_2(70) = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot 6.129 = 0.6629$ s, pushing $T_{task}$ to ≈ **1.58 s** — still acceptable, but trending toward the threshold.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Task Time Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Stimulus Onset - e.g. Rain Detected] --> B[Stage 1: Perception - Sensory Register]
```
