```mermaid
    B --> C[Stage 2: Cognition - Decision Selection via Hick-Hyman Law]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Motor Execution - Reach and Actuate via Fitts Law]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Verification - Confirm via Feedback Loop]
    E --> F{Threshold Check - NHTSA 2.0 s Limit}
    F -- Below Threshold --> G[Result: Task Safe - Acceptable HMI Design]
    F -- Above Threshold --> H[Result: Task Unsafe - Redesign Control Layout]
    B --> B1[Governed by Contrast, Luminance, Stimulus Modality]
    C --> C1[Governed by Number of Alternatives n in LTM]
    D --> D1[Governed by Distance D and Target Width W]
    E --> E1[Governed by Feedback Modality - Visual, Tactile, Auditory]
```

### 4.2 Fitts' Law Index of Difficulty vs Task Time Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small Target W - High Difficulty] --> B[Index of Difficulty ID up arrow]
    B --> C[Tmotor up arrow]
    C --> D[Task Time EXCEEDS 2.0 s]
    D --> E[Redesign: Enlarge W or Reduce D]
    F[Large Target W - Low Difficulty] --> G[Index of Difficulty ID down arrow]
    G --> H[Tmotor down arrow]
    H --> I[Task Time WITHIN 2.0 s]
    I --> J[Approved: Current HMI Layout]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition: From Task to Sub-Motion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Task: Adjust HVAC Temperature] --> B[Subtask 1: Locate HVAC Panel]
    A --> C[Subtask 2: Reach Temperature Knob]
    A --> D[Subtask 3: Rotate Knob to Desired Setting]
    A --> E[Subtask 4: Verify on Display]
    B --> B1[Motion: Search Visual Scan]
    C --> C1[MTM: Reach R and Move M]
    D --> D1[MTM: Turn T and Apply Force A]
    E --> E1[Feedback Glance: 0.30 s]
    B1 --> F[Aggregate Ttask via MTM Tables]
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
Define **Task Time** in the context of automotive human-machine interaction. List its four constituent stages with one-line descriptions of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Task Time is the total elapsed duration for a driver to perceive a stimulus, decide, act, and verify an in-vehicle subtask. The four stages are:
1. **Perception Time ($T_P$):** Latency for the driver to register the stimulus.
2. **Cognition Time ($T_C$):** Latency for decision selection, governed by Hick-Hyman Law.
3. **Motor Time ($T_M$):** Latency for physical movement, governed by Fitts' Law or MTM.
4. **Verification Time ($T_V$):** Latency for feedback confirmation. (1 mark each)

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
State the **Hick-Hyman Law** and **Fitts' Law**. How are each of them used in the design of an automotive cockpit?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hick-Hyman Law:** $T_C = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — predicts decision time as a function of the number of equally probable choices. Used to keep the number of alternatives in any driver menu **below 4–5** to minimize cognitive load. (1.5 marks)
- **Fitts' Law:** $T_M = a + b \cdot \log_2(2D/W)$ — predicts movement time as a function of target distance and width. Used to place frequently used controls **close to the driver's natural hand rest** (short $D$) with **large actuation surfaces** (large $W$). (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**(a)** Derive the composite equation for **Total Task Time** for an in-vehicle control operation. Define each term clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** A driver must activate the rear fog lamp while driving. The control is located at a distance $D = 0.45$ m from the driver's hand rest, has an actuation width $W = 0.015$ m, offers $n = 3$ equally probable response alternatives, and is presented with contrast ratio $C = 0.7$. Using the standard coefficients $a_P = 0.10$ s, $b_P = 0.020$ s, $a_C = 0.155$ s, $b_C = 0.145$ s, $a_M = 0.05$ s, $b_M = 0.10$ s, and $T_V = 0.30$ s, compute the **Total Task Time** and comment on its safety against the NHTSA 2.0 s threshold. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**
The composite task time for an in-vehicle control operation is derived from the serial human information processing model:

$$
T_{task} = T_{perception} + T_{cognition} + T_{motor} + T_{verification}
$$

- **$T_{perception}$:** Time for the sensory organs to detect and register the stimulus.
- **$T_{cognition}$:** Time for the brain to process the input and select the appropriate response (Hick-Hyman Law).
- **$T_{motor}$:** Time for the hand/arm to physically execute the movement (Fitts' Law or MTM).
- **$T_{verification}$:** Time for the driver to confirm the action took effect via visual/tactile/auditory feedback.

(1.5 marks for each stage definition + 1 mark for the composite equation + 1 mark for the justification of serial processing = 7 marks)

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Compute Perception Time**

$$
T_P = 0.10 + \frac{0.020}{0.7} = 0.10 + 0.0286 = 0.1286 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Cognition Time (Hick-Hyman Law)**

$$
T_C = 0.155 + 0.145 \cdot \log_2(3+1) = 0.155 + 0.145 \cdot \log_2(4)
$$

$$
T_C = 0.155 + 0.145 \cdot 2 = 0.155 + 0.290 = 0.445 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Motor Time (Fitts' Law)**

$$
T_M = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \cdot 0.45}{0.015}\right) = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot \log_2(60)
$$

$$
T_M = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot 5.9069 = 0.05 + 0.5907 = 0.6407 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4: Add Verification Time**

$$
T_V = 0.30 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Total Task Time**

$$
T_{task} = 0.1286 + 0.445 + 0.6407 + 0.30 = 1.5143 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{task} \approx 1.51 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 6: Safety Comment**

Since **1.51 s < 2.0 s**, the rear fog lamp task falls **within the NHTSA single-task safety threshold**. However, since it is a secondary task performed during driving, designers should consider relocating the control closer to the hand rest (reducing $D$) to create a larger ergonomic margin.

(1 mark per step + 2 marks for final computation and 1 mark for safety comment = 7 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**(a)** Explain the **Methods-Time Measurement (MTM)** system as applied to automotive assembly and cockpit operation. Discuss the role of basic motion symbols such as **Reach (R)** and **Move (M)** in predicting task time. (7 marks)

**(b)** A driver must lift a smartphone from the center console ($D = 0.30$ m, $W = 0.08$ m) to read a notification. Compute the Fitts' Law motor time using $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.10$ s. If the driver performs 5 such glances, each lasting the motor time plus a 0.30 s verification glance, calculate the **total eyes-off-road time** and assess its compliance with the NHTSA **12.0 s** cumulative limit. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**
**Methods-Time Measurement (MTM)** is a predetermined motion-time system that assigns standard time values (in TMU — Time Measurement Units, where **1 TMU = 0.036 seconds**) to basic human motions. In automotive applications:

- **Reach (R):** Time to move the hand from the starting point toward an object. Increases with distance. Example: R20A = 0.36 s (for 20 cm reach).
- **Move (M):** Time to transport an object to a defined location. Includes grasp and release. Example: M20B = 0.50 s.

MTM is used in:
1. **Assembly-line balancing** to predict cycle times.
2. **Cockpit usability studies** to estimate reach and operate times before physical prototyping.
3. **Benchmarking** manual task performance against ergonomic standards.

(2 marks for MTM definition, 2 marks for R and M symbols, 3 marks for automotive application = 7 marks)

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Compute Fitts' Law Motor Time**

$$
T_M = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \cdot 0.30}{0.08}\right) = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot \log_2(7.5)
$$

$$
T_M = 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot 2.9069 = 0.05 + 0.2907 = 0.3407 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Single Glance Duration (motor + verification)**

$$
T_{glance} = T_M + T_V = 0.3407 + 0.30 = 0.6407 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Eyes-Off-Road Time for 5 Glances**

$$
T_{EOR} = 5 \cdot 0.6407 = 3.2035 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4: Compliance Assessment**

Since **3.20 s < 12.0 s**, this glance sequence is well within the NHTSA cumulative limit. However, the **2.0 s per-task limit** is approached (each glance is ~0.64 s, so 3 glances would still be ≤ 2.0 s per "task"); the operation should be redesigned to consolidate the 5 glances into fewer integrated display events (e.g., heads-up display).

(2 marks for Step 1, 2 marks for Step 2, 1.5 marks for Step 3, 1.5 marks for Step 4 = 7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students frequently **omit the verification time** $T_V$ and report only the sum of perception + cognition + motor. This loses up to **2 marks** in Part B questions. Always include all four stages.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **Hick-Hyman Law** ($T_C$ vs $n$) with **Fitts' Law** ($T_M$ vs $D, W$). Many students apply the wrong law to the wrong stage. Memorize: Hick-Hyman is for **cognition**, Fitts is for **motor**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to convert TMU to seconds in MTM problems. **1 TMU = 0.036 s**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Reporting numeric values without units. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks per missing unit**.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to compare the computed task time against the **NHTSA 2.0 s / 12.0 s thresholds** in the final line. This qualitative comment carries **1–2 marks** and is often skipped.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Task Time** = perception + cognition + motor + verification (serial pipeline).
- **Hick-Hyman Law** governs cognition: $T_C = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$. Keep $n$ small.
- **Fitts' Law** governs motor: $T_M = a + b \cdot \log_2(2D/W)$. Minimize $D$, maximize $W$.
- **MTM (Methods-Time Measurement)** uses standard TMU values (1 TMU = 0.036 s) for basic motions like Reach, Move, Turn, Apply Pressure.
- **NHTSA thresholds:** Single-task ceiling = **2.0 s**; total eyes-off-road = **12.0 s**; max glances per task = **6**.
- **Design implication:** Place frequently used controls **close to the natural hand rest** (short $D$) with **large actuation surfaces** (large $W$) and **few alternatives** ($n \leq 4$).
- **Reaction time baselines:** visual ≈ 0.25 s, auditory ≈ 0.17 s.
- **Verification time** is non-negotiable — always include $T_V$ in composite calculations.
- **Workload proxy:** $W_{proxy} = T_{task}/T_{threshold}$. Ratio > 1 → unsafe.
- **Regulatory anchors to memorize:** ISO 15005, SAE J2364, NHTSA Phase 1 Driver Distraction Guidelines.
- **Worked numeric pattern:** Always show all four stage calculations explicitly before summing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glance Duration - Application. Driver Workload Measurement - Present Situation - Methods to Measure - Studies
Vehicle Evaluation - Methods of Data Collection and Analysis - Subjective Methods - Rating on a Scale
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Evaluation - Methods of Data Collection and Analysis - Subjective Methods - Rating on a Scale

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle Evaluation** is the systematic process of assessing the performance, comfort, safety, and overall quality attributes of a vehicle through structured procedures that combine both **objective measurement** (instrumented) and **subjective assessment** (human judgment).

Within this domain, **Subjective Methods of Data Collection and Analysis** refer to evaluation techniques in which trained human evaluators (assessors, drivers, or passengers) use their sensory perception, experience, and standardized rating protocols to quantify vehicle attributes that are difficult or impossible to capture through instruments alone — such as *ride comfort feel, steering precision, NVH perception, seat comfort, and perceived quality*.

**Rating on a Scale** is the most widely employed subjective data-collection technique. It requires the evaluator to assign a discrete or continuous numerical value (e.g., **1 to 10**, **1 to 5**, or **0 to 100 mm on a Visual Analog Scale**) to a specific vehicle attribute, based on a pre-defined anchor description at each scale extreme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on linking subjective ratings to engineering metrics. Students must understand the *psychometric basis* of scale construction, *bias control*, and *statistical aggregation* — not merely the definition of a scale.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Review"

Imagine you visit a restaurant. Instead of measuring the "spiciness" of a dish with a chemical sensor, the chef asks you: *"On a scale of 1 to 10, how spicy did you find the dish?"* 

- **1** = Not spicy at all (mild ketchup)
- **10** = Mouth-on-fire spicy (Carolina Reaper)

This is exactly what happens in vehicle evaluation when an assessor rates *"ride harshness on a 1-to-10 scale"*. The human sensory system acts as the "sensor," the scale acts as the "transducer," and the resulting number is the "data point" that engineers can statistically analyze. The challenge — just like the chef's problem — is ensuring different people (raters) interpret "5 out of 10 spiciness" consistently. This is what we call **psychometric calibration**.

## 1.3 Why Subjective Methods? — The Justification

| Reason | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Human-Centric Attributes** | Comfort, perceived quality, and driving pleasure are inherently *experiential* — no instrument can fully replace human perception. |
| **Holistic Integration** | Humans integrate multiple cues (vibration, noise, seat feel) that sensors measure in isolation. |
| **Customer Acceptance** | Final buying decisions are *subjective*. A technically "good" vehicle may still be rejected by customers. |
| **Validation of Objective Metrics** | Subjective ratings are used to *correlate* with objective measurements (e.g., ISO 2631 vibration dose value vs. comfort rating). |
| **Early Development Feedback** | During prototype testing, subjective input guides engineering refinements before final calibration. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Examiner's Focus):**
> - **Objective Evaluation** → Uses instruments (accelerometers, microphones, force sensors). The output is a physical quantity (m/s², dB, N).
> - **Subjective Evaluation** → Uses human assessors. The output is a **psychometric rating** (unitless, ordinal/interval).
> - **Correlation Studies** → The bridge between the two, often plotted as a scatter plot with regression line.

## 1.4 The Subjective Evaluation Pipeline (Block Overview)

The complete workflow of a subjective rating study follows this chain:

**Attribute Definition → Scale Selection → Rater Training → Test Conducting → Data Aggregation → Statistical Analysis → Correlation with Objective Metrics → Engineering Decision**

Each block in this chain will be elaborated in the sections below.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Subjective Rating Scales

Rating scales are broadly classified along two axes: **Response Format** and **Reference Structure**.

### A. Based on Response Format

**1. Categorical (Nominal) Scale**
- Attributes are placed into unordered categories (e.g., *Acceptable / Marginal / Unacceptable*).
- Analyzed using chi-square tests and mode frequencies.

**2. Ordinal Scale**
- Categories with a natural order but unequal psychological spacing.
- Example: 1 = Very Poor, 2 = Poor, 3 = Fair, 4 = Good, 5 = Excellent.
- Analyzed using median, percentiles, and non-parametric tests.

**3. Interval Scale**
- Equal psychological spacing between consecutive values, but no true zero.
- Example: 1-to-10 comfort rating.
- Analyzed using mean, standard deviation, parametric tests.

**4. Ratio Scale**
- Equal spacing AND a true zero.
- Example: 0-to-100 mm Visual Analog Scale.
- Permits all arithmetic operations including ratios.

### B. Based on Reference Structure

**1. Absolute Rating (Free Magnitude Estimation)**
- The rater judges the attribute against an internal mental standard.
- Simple and fast, but susceptible to inter-rater drift.

**2. Comparative Rating (Paired Comparison / Ranking)**
- The rater compares two or more vehicles/samples directly.
- More reliable, but number of comparisons grows combinatorially.

**3. Anchored Rating (Reference-based)**
- A physical or descriptive reference is provided at each scale point.
- Minimizes drift and improves inter-rater consistency.

## 2.2 Commonly Used Scales in Vehicle Evaluation

### 2.2.1 Likert Scale (5-point or 7-point)

A symmetric agree-disagree scale expanded to descriptive anchors:

| Scale Value | Comfort Anchor | Handling Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unacceptable — Driver will complain | Dangerous — Uncontrollable |
| 2 | Poor — Noticeable issues | Poor — Driver anxious |
| 3 | Acceptable — Just meets spec | Acceptable — Meets minimum |
| 4 | Good — No issues | Good — Confident control |
| 5 | Excellent — Premium feel | Excellent — Sporty, precise |

### 2.2.2 Visual Analog Scale (VAS)

A 100 mm horizontal line with verbal anchors at the two ends. The rater marks a point on the line, and the distance from the "low" anchor (in mm) is the score. Provides **continuous-ratio-level data** with high resolution.

### 2.2.3 Numerical Category Scale (NCS) — SAE J1060 / ISO 15654 Aligned

A 1-to-10 integer scale with 5 descriptive anchor points at odd integers, used in many OEM subjective studies (Ford, Toyota, GM internal protocols).

### 2.2.4 Semantic Differential Scale

Bipolar adjectives at the two ends (e.g., *Rough ←→ Smooth*). Useful for multidimensional profiling.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$ | **Arithmetic Mean Rating** — average of N ratings. | Central tendency of ratings. |
| 2 | $\sigma_R = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (R_i - \bar{R})^2}$ | **Sample Standard Deviation** — spread of ratings. | Rating consistency. |
| 3 | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma_R}{\bar{R}} \times 100\%$ | **Coefficient of Variation** — relative spread. | Comparing variability across attributes. |
| 4 | $SE_{\bar{R}} = \dfrac{\sigma_R}{\sqrt{N}}$ | **Standard Error of Mean** — precision of the mean estimate. | Confidence interval construction. |
| 5 | $CI_{95\%} = \bar{R} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE_{\bar{R}}$ | **95% Confidence Interval** for the mean rating. | Significance of differences. |
| 6 | $ICC = \dfrac{\sigma_{between}^2}{\sigma_{between}^2 + \sigma_{within}^2}$ | **Intraclass Correlation Coefficient** — inter-rater reliability. | Validity of the rating panel. |
| 7 | $\kappa = \dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | **Cohen's Kappa** — agreement beyond chance (categorical). | Rater calibration check. |
| 8 | $W = \dfrac{12 \sum_{j=1}^{k} (R_j - \bar{R})^2}{k^2 (n^3 - n)}$ | **Kendall's W (Coefficient of Concordance)** — agreement among k raters on n samples. | Panel consensus. |
| 9 | $t = \dfrac{\bar{R}_A - \bar{R}_B}{SE_{diff}}$ | **Paired t-test statistic** for comparing two conditions. | A/B vehicle comparisons. |
| 10 | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | **Pearson Correlation** between subjective rating and objective metric. | Subjective-objective correlation. |
| 11 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | **Coefficient of Determination** — proportion of variance explained. | Quality of correlation. |
| 12 | $S_{\min} = \left( \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{\Delta} \right)^2$ | **Minimum Sample Size** for detecting difference Δ with confidence. | Test planning. |

Where in the formulas above:
- $N$ = number of ratings
- $R_i$ = individual rating value
- $\sigma$ = population standard deviation estimate
- $p_o$ = observed agreement proportion
- $p_e$ = expected agreement by chance
- $k$ = number of raters, $n$ = number of samples
- $x_i, y_i$ = paired subjective and objective data points

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Board questions often ask for the *interpretation* of these statistics, not just the formula. Always state: *"A high ICC (>0.75) indicates strong agreement among raters, validating the use of mean rating as a reliable metric."*

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Subjective Rating Methods

1. **Vehicle Development (NVH Tuning):** Engineers iterate suspension and powertrain calibrations, and use subjective ratings to confirm perceived improvements.
2. **Benchmarking:** Subjective ratings of competitor vehicles establish the *competitive envelope* for target setting.
3. **Quality Assurance:** Production-line subjective audits (e.g., 10-point walk-around scoring by trained auditors).
4. **Customer Research:** Large-N customer clinics where buyers rate vehicles after test drives.
5. **Regulatory & Standards Work:** ISO 15654 and SAE J1060 both contain subjective evaluation frameworks.
6. **Correlation Engineering:** Building regression models: $R_{subjective} = f(\text{VDV}, a_{w}, L_{eq})$ for ride comfort, enabling *objective-only* prediction in future programs.

## 2.5 Sources of Error in Subjective Rating — The "Threats to Validity"

| Source | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Central Tendency Bias** | Rater avoids extreme ends of the scale. | Use absolute scales with explicit anchor descriptions. |
| **Halo Effect** | One good attribute inflates rating of unrelated attribute. | Train raters to evaluate attributes in isolation. |
| **Recency / Primacy Effect** | Last/first attribute dominates judgment. | Randomize attribute order across raters. |
| **Anchoring Bias** | First vehicle rated becomes the reference. | Use a *warm-up* vehicle and counterbalance order. |
| **Expectation Bias** | Brand knowledge influences rating. | Blind the test (unbranded vehicles, blackout interiors). |
| **Drift** | Rater's internal scale shifts over a long session. | Re-anchor with a reference vehicle every 5–6 trials. |
| **Acclimatization** | Continued exposure dulls perception. | Take breaks; limit session length (typically ≤ 2 hours). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean and Standard Deviation of a Rating Sample

**Problem Setup:** A panel of **5 expert raters** evaluates the *"Secondary Ride Comfort"* of a vehicle on a **1-to-10 scale**. The ratings obtained are:

$$R_1 = 7, \quad R_2 = 8, \quad R_3 = 6, \quad R_4 = 9, \quad R_5 = 5$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean (Central Rating).**

The general formula is:

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$$

Substituting the observed values:

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{5} (7 + 8 + 6 + 9 + 5)$$

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{5} (35)$$

$$\boxed{\bar{R} = 7.0}$$

**Interpretation:** The panel's consensus is that secondary ride comfort is *Good* (just above the 7/10 anchor on most OEM scales).

**Step 2 — Compute the Sample Standard Deviation.**

The formula (with $N - 1$ denominator, Bessel's correction) is:

$$\sigma_R = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (R_i - \bar{R})^2}$$

Compute each squared deviation:

$$\begin{aligned}
(R_1 - \bar{R})^2 &= (7 - 7)^2 = 0 \\
(R_2 - \bar{R})^2 &= (8 - 7)^2 = 1 \\
(R_3 - \bar{R})^2 &= (6 - 7)^2 = 1 \\
(R_4 - \bar{R})^2 &= (9 - 7)^2 = 4 \\
(R_5 - \bar{R})^2 &= (5 - 7)^2 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (R_i - \bar{R})^2 = 0 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 4 = 10$$

Now substitute:

$$\sigma_R = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{4}} = \sqrt{2.5}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_R \approx 1.58}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Coefficient of Variation (Relative Spread).**

$$CV = \dfrac{\sigma_R}{\bar{R}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1.58}{7.0} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{CV \approx 22.6\%}$$

**Interpretation:** A CV of ~23% indicates *moderate* rater agreement. For a robust subjective study, OEM protocols typically target CV < 15%. The high CV here suggests one rater (rating 5) may be a "harsh judge" and warrants review.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 95% Confidence Interval for the Mean Rating

**Why this matters:** A single mean rating (7.0) is a point estimate. The **confidence interval** tells us the range within which the *true* mean of the entire rater population likely lies.

**Step 1 — Compute the Standard Error of the Mean.**

$$SE_{\bar{R}} = \dfrac{\sigma_R}{\sqrt{N}} = \dfrac{1.58}{\sqrt{5}} = \dfrac{1.58}{2.236}$$

$$SE_{\bar{R}} \approx 0.707$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 95% Confidence Interval Formula.**

For $N = 5$, the small-sample correction uses the **t-distribution** with $N - 1 = 4$ degrees of freedom. The t-critical value is $t_{0.025, 4} = 2.776$.

$$CI_{95\%} = \bar{R} \pm t_{0.025,\, 4} \cdot SE_{\bar{R}}$$

$$CI_{95\%} = 7.0 \pm 2.776 \times 0.707$$

$$CI_{95\%} = 7.0 \pm 1.962$$

$$\boxed{CI_{95\%} = [5.04, \; 8.96]}$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true population mean rating lies between **5.04 and 8.96**. The width (~4 points) is large because of the small sample; doubling the rater count to 10 would roughly halve this width.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision Rule:** If two vehicle configurations have non-overlapping 95% CIs on a key attribute, the difference is **statistically significant** at $\alpha = 0.05$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) for Rater Reliability

**Problem Setup:** Three raters evaluate **4 vehicles** on a 1-to-10 scale for "Steering Feel":

| Vehicle | Rater A | Rater B | Rater C |
|---|---|---|---|
| V1 | 7 | 8 | 7 |
| V2 | 5 | 6 | 5 |
| V3 | 9 | 8 | 9 |
| V4 | 6 | 7 | 6 |

**Step 1 — Compute the Grand Mean.**

$$\bar{R}_{..} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot k} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} R_{ij}$$

Where $n = 4$ vehicles, $k = 3$ raters. Total observations = 12.

$$\bar{R}_{..} = \dfrac{7+8+7+5+6+5+9+8+9+6+7+6}{12} = \dfrac{83}{12}$$

$$\bar{R}_{..} \approx 6.917$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Between-Vehicle Mean Square (MS_B).**

First, compute the mean rating per vehicle:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}_{V1} &= (7+8+7)/3 = 22/3 \approx 7.333 \\
\bar{R}_{V2} &= (5+6+5)/3 = 16/3 \approx 5.333 \\
\bar{R}_{V3} &= (9+8+9)/3 = 26/3 \approx 8.667 \\
\bar{R}_{V4} &= (6+7+6)/3 = 19/3 \approx 6.333
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of squares between vehicles:

$$SS_B = k \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\bar{R}_{i.} - \bar{R}_{..})^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_B &= 3 \left[(7.333-6.917)^2 + (5.333-6.917)^2 + (8.667-6.917)^2 + (6.333-6.917)^2 \right] \\
&= 3 \left[0.173 + 2.507 + 3.062 + 0.341 \right] \\
&= 3 \times 6.083 \\
&= 18.249
\end{aligned}$$

Degrees of freedom: $df_B = n - 1 = 3$.

$$MS_B = \dfrac{SS_B}{df_B} = \dfrac{18.249}{3} = 6.083$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Within-Vehicle Mean Square (MS_W).**

Sum of squares within vehicles:

$$SS_W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} (R_{ij} - \bar{R}_{i.})^2$$

For each vehicle:

$$\begin{aligned}
V1: & \quad (7-7.333)^2 + (8-7.333)^2 + (7-7.333)^2 = 0.111 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.666 \\
V2: & \quad (5-5.333)^2 + (6-5.333)^2 + (5-5.333)^2 = 0.111 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.666 \\
V3: & \quad (9-8.667)^2 + (8-8.667)^2 + (9-8.667)^2 = 0.111 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.666 \\
V4: & \quad (6-6.333)^2 + (7-6.333)^2 + (6-6.333)^2 = 0.111 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.666
\end{aligned}$$

$$SS_W = 0.666 + 0.666 + 0.666 + 0.666 = 2.664$$

Degrees of freedom: $df_W = n(k - 1) = 4 \times 2 = 8$.

$$MS_W = \dfrac{SS_W}{df_W} = \dfrac{2.664}{8} = 0.333$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ICC.**

For a one-way random-effects model:

$$ICC = \dfrac{MS_B - MS_W}{MS_B + (k-1) \cdot MS_W}$$

$$ICC = \dfrac{6.083 - 0.333}{6.083 + 2 \times 0.333} = \dfrac{5.750}{6.749}$$

$$\boxed{ICC \approx 0.852}$$

**Interpretation:** Per Cicchetti's guidelines, $ICC > 0.75$ indicates **excellent** inter-rater reliability. The rating panel is highly consistent and the mean rating can be trusted as a reliable engineering metric.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of the Three Derivations

| Derivation | Key Result | Engineering Decision |
|---|---|---|
| Mean & SD | $\bar{R} = 7.0$, $\sigma_R = 1.58$ | Rating is "Good", but spread is moderate. |
| 95% CI | $[5.04, \; 8.96]$ | Wide interval — increase rater count. |
| ICC | $0.852$ (Excellent) | Panel is reliable — proceed with study. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Subjective Evaluation Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Vehicle Attributes] --> B[Select Rating Scale Type]
    B --> C[Select Rater Panel]
    C --> D[Rater Training and Calibration]
    D --> E[Conduct Test Drives]
    E --> F[Collect Raw Ratings]
    F --> G[Statistical Aggregation]
    G --> H[Reliability Check via ICC]
    H --> I{ICC > 0.75}
    I -->|Yes| J[Mean Rating is Valid]
    I -->|No| K[Rerecruit or Retrain Raters]
    K --> C
    J --> L[Correlate with Objective Metrics]
    L --> M[Engineering Decision Making]
    M --> N[Vehicle Attribute Target Setting]
```

## 4.2 Scale Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Rating Scale] --> B{What is the data type needed?}
    B -->|Categorical| C[Nominal Scale - Acceptable Marginal Unacceptable]
    B -->|Ordered Categories| D[Ordinal Likert Scale 5 or 7 point]
    B -->|Continuous| E[Visual Analog Scale VAS 0 to 100 mm]
    B -->|Absolute Numeric| F[NCS 1 to 10 Scale]
    B -->|Multidimensional| G[Semantic Differential Scale]
    C --> H{Rater agreement required?}
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -->|Yes Anchored| I[Add Descriptive Anchors at Each Point]
    H -->|No Free| J[Use Open Scale with Minimal Anchoring]
    I --> K[Final Scale]
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Rater Bias Mitigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Potential Bias Sources] --> B[Halo Effect]
    A --> C[Central Tendency Bias]
    A --> D[Anchoring Bias]
    A --> E[Drift Over Session]
    B --> F[Mitigation Strategy 1: Randomize Attribute Order]
    C --> G[Mitigation Strategy 2: Use Absolute Anchored Scale]
    D --> H[Mitigation Strategy 3: Counterbalance Vehicle Order]
    E --> I[Mitigation Strategy 4: Reference Re anchor Every 5 Trials]
    F --> J[Clean Rating Dataset]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Statistical Analysis with Reduced Bias]
```

## 4.4 Subjective vs Objective Evaluation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Attribute to Evaluate] --> B{Type of Attribute}
    B -->|Physical Measurable| C[Objective Path]
    B -->|Human Perception| D[Subjective Path]
    B -->|Hybrid Validation| E[Correlation Path]
    C --> C1[Accelerometer Microphone Force Sensor]
    C1 --> C2[Physical Quantity: m per s squared, dB, N]
    C2 --> C3[Signal Processing and Analysis]
    D --> D1[Trained Human Rater]
    D1 --> D2[Rating on Scale 1 to 10 or VAS]
    D2 --> D3[Statistical Aggregation: Mean, SD, ICC]
    E --> E1[Plot Subjective vs Objective]
    E1 --> E2[Compute Pearson r and R squared]
    E2 --> E3[Establish Predictive Model]
    C3 --> F[Engineering Action]
    D3 --> F
    E3 --> F
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define subjective evaluation in the context of vehicle comfort engineering. List any two scales commonly used. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Subjective evaluation is a method of vehicle assessment in which trained human evaluators use their sensory perception and standardized rating protocols to quantify vehicle attributes such as ride comfort, NVH perception, and steering feel that cannot be fully captured by instruments.

Two commonly used scales are:
1. **Numerical Category Scale (NCS):** A 1-to-10 integer scale with descriptive anchors at odd points.
2. **Visual Analog Scale (VAS):** A continuous 100 mm line with bipolar verbal anchors.

### Question 2: What is the Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC), and what is its significance in subjective vehicle evaluation? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The ICC is a statistical measure that quantifies the degree of agreement among multiple raters evaluating the same set of samples. It is computed as the ratio of between-subject variance to total variance.

**Significance:**
- Validates whether the rating panel is consistent.
- An ICC value above **0.75** (per Cicchetti's guidelines) indicates **excellent** inter-rater reliability, justifying the use of mean rating as a reliable engineering metric.
- An ICC below **0.50** signals poor rater agreement, requiring panel retraining or replacement.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various types of rating scales used in subjective vehicle evaluation, with a focus on the **Visual Analog Scale (VAS)** and the **Likert Scale**. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A panel of 6 expert raters evaluated the *"Steering Responsiveness"* of a prototype vehicle on a 1-to-10 scale. Ratings: **8, 9, 7, 8, 10, 6**. Compute the **mean rating, sample standard deviation, coefficient of variation, and 95% confidence interval** (using $t_{0.025, 5} = 2.571$). Interpret the results for an engineering decision. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Types of Rating Scales:**

**1. Visual Analog Scale (VAS):**
A 100 mm horizontal line bounded by verbal anchors at the extremes (e.g., *"Worst Imaginable Comfort"* on the left, *"Best Imaginable Comfort"* on the right). The rater marks a point on the line, and the distance from the left anchor is the score (in mm).

*Advantages:*
- Continuous, ratio-level data with high resolution.
- Minimizes central tendency bias as raters are not forced into discrete categories.
- Sensitive to small perceived changes.

*Limitations:*
- Requires manual measurement (ruler or digital caliper).
- Cannot be administered verbally.
- Higher cognitive load for raters.

**2. Likert Scale:**
A symmetric ordinal scale (typically 5-point or 7-point) with verbal descriptors at each point, e.g.:

| 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strongly Disagree | Disagree | Neutral | Agree | Strongly Agree |

In vehicle evaluation, the anchors are contextualized: *"Very Poor, Poor, Fair, Good, Excellent"*.

*Advantages:*
- Easy to administer and analyze.
- Well-understood by raters.
- High face validity.

*Limitations:*
- Coarser resolution (5 or 7 discrete values).
- Assumes equal psychological spacing (often violated).
- Susceptible to central tendency and acquiescence bias.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | VAS | Likert |
|---|---|---|
| Data Type | Continuous (Ratio) | Discrete (Ordinal/Interval) |
| Resolution | High (~100 levels) | Low (5 or 7 levels) |
| Sensitivity | High | Moderate |
| Ease of Use | Moderate | High |
| Statistical Test | Parametric | Non-parametric preferred |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Given:** $N = 6$, Ratings: $R_1 = 8, R_2 = 9, R_3 = 7, R_4 = 8, R_5 = 10, R_6 = 6$

**Step 1: Compute the Mean Rating.**

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{8 + 9 + 7 + 8 + 10 + 6}{6} = \dfrac{48}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{R} = 8.0}$$

**Step 2: Compute Squared Deviations.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(R_1 - \bar{R})^2 &= (8-8)^2 = 0 \\
(R_2 - \bar{R})^2 &= (9-8)^2 = 1 \\
(R_3 - \bar{R})^2 &= (7-8)^2 = 1 \\
(R_4 - \bar{R})^2 &= (8-8)^2 = 0 \\
(R_5 - \bar{R})^2 &= (10-8)^2 = 4 \\
(R_6 - \bar{R})^2 &= (6-8)^2 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Sum of Squared Deviations.**

$$\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2 = 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 4 + 4 = 10$$

**Step 4: Sample Standard Deviation.**

$$\sigma_R = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{6 - 1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{5}} = \sqrt{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_R \approx 1.414}$$

**Step 5: Coefficient of Variation.**

$$CV = \dfrac{1.414}{8.0} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{CV \approx 17.68\%}$$

**Step 6: Standard Error of Mean.**

$$SE_{\bar{R}} = \dfrac{1.414}{\sqrt{6}} = \dfrac{1.414}{2.449} \approx 0.577$$

**Step 7: 95% Confidence Interval.**

Using $t_{0.025, 5} = 2.571$:

$$CI_{95\%} = 8.0 \pm (2.571 \times 0.577) = 8.0 \pm 1.484$$

$$\boxed{CI_{95\%} = [6.52, \; 9.48]}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The mean rating of **8.0** indicates *"Good"* steering responsiveness, meeting typical OEM targets.
- The CV of **17.68%** is moderate, suggesting reasonable agreement but one rater (rating 6) may be a *harsh judge*; this warrants review.
- The 95% CI of **[6.52, 9.48]** is relatively wide due to small sample; increasing to 10–12 raters would tighten this interval.
- The mean is comfortably above the *Acceptable* threshold (typically 6/10), so the prototype is approved for further development.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **sources of error and bias** in subjective rating studies, and explain the **mitigation strategies** for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Three expert raters evaluate **5 vehicles** on *"Brake Pedal Feel"* (1-to-10 scale). The data matrix is:

| Vehicle | Rater 1 | Rater 2 | Rater 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| V1 | 7 | 8 | 7 |
| V2 | 6 | 7 | 6 |
| V3 | 9 | 9 | 8 |
| V4 | 5 | 6 | 5 |
| V5 | 8 | 9 | 8 |

**Compute the Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC)** and interpret the result. Use the one-way random-effects model. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Sources of Error in Subjective Rating:**

**1. Central Tendency Bias:**
Raters avoid extreme scale values, clustering around the middle.
*Mitigation:* Use anchored scales with explicit, vivid descriptors at every point.

**2. Halo Effect:**
A favorable impression of one attribute (e.g., stylish exterior) inflates ratings of unrelated attributes (e.g., perceived ride quality).
*Mitigation:* Train raters to evaluate attributes in isolation; randomize attribute order.

**3. Anchoring Bias:**
The first vehicle rated becomes the *internal reference* against which subsequent vehicles are compared.
*Mitigation:* Use a *warm-up* trial that is excluded from analysis; counterbalance vehicle order using a Latin square design.

**4. Drift (Scale Drift):**
The rater's internal calibration shifts during a long session.
*Mitigation:* Re-anchor with a reference vehicle every 5–6 trials.

**5. Recency / Primacy Effects:**
The first or last attribute dominates judgment.
*Mitigation:* Randomize attribute sequence across raters.

**6. Expectation / Brand Bias:**
Pre-existing brand knowledge influences rating.
*Mitigation:* Blind the test (anonymize vehicles, use blackout interiors, cover badges).

**7. Acclimatization / Fatigue:**
Sensory dulling over a long session.
*Mitigation:* Limit session length to 1.5–2 hours; mandate breaks.

**8. Rater Calibration Drift (between raters):**
Different raters use different internal scales.
*Mitigation:* Joint training sessions; calibration trials; ICC monitoring.

**9. Demand Characteristics:**
Rater unconsciously guesses the study hypothesis and adjusts ratings.
*Mitigation:* Deceptive cover stories; randomized presentation; blind administration.

**10. Order / Sequence Effects:**
Fatigue accumulation depends on test sequence.
*Mitigation:* Counterbalancing using a **Latin Square Design** with $n \times n$ matrix.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Given:** $n = 5$ vehicles, $k = 3$ raters.

**Step 1: Compute Vehicle Means and Grand Mean.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}_{V1} &= (7+8+7)/3 = 22/3 \approx 7.333 \\
\bar{R}_{V2} &= (6+7+6)/3 = 19/3 \approx 6.333 \\
\bar{R}_{V3} &= (9+9+8)/3 = 26/3 \approx 8.667 \\
\bar{R}_{V4} &= (5+6+5)/3 = 16/3 \approx 5.333 \\
\bar{R}_{V5} &= (8+9+8)/3 = 25/3 \approx 8.333
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of all ratings = $22 + 19 + 26 + 16 + 25 = 108$

$$\bar{R}_{..} = \dfrac{108}{5 \times 3} = \dfrac{108}{15} = 7.2$$

**Step 2: Compute Between-Vehicle Sum of Squares (SS_B).**

$$SS_B = k \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\bar{R}_{i.} - \bar{R}_{..})^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_B &= 3 \left[(7.333-7.2)^2 + (6.333-7.2)^2 + (8.667-7.2)^2 + (5.333-7.2)^2 + (8.333-7.2)^2 \right] \\
&= 3 \left[0.0177 + 0.7511 + 2.1511 + 3.4844 + 1.2844 \right] \\
&= 3 \times 7.6887 \\
&= 23.066
\end{aligned}$$

Degrees of freedom: $df_B = n - 1 = 4$

$$MS_B = \dfrac{23.066}{4} = 5.767$$

**Step 3: Compute Within-Vehicle Sum of Squares (SS_W).**

For each vehicle, compute $\sum (R_{ij} - \bar{R}_{i.})^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V1: & \quad (7-7.333)^2 + (8-7.333)^2 + (7-7.333)^2 = 0.111 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.666 \\
V2: & \quad (6-6.333)^2 + (7-6.333)^2 + (6-6.333)^2 = 0.111 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.666 \\
V3: & \quad (9-8.667)^2 + (9-8.667)^2 + (8-8.667)^2 = 0.111 + 0.111 + 0.444 = 0.666 \\
V4: & \quad (5-5.333)^2 + (6-5.333)^2 + (5-5.333)^2 = 0.111 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.666 \\
V5: & \quad (8-8.333)^2 + (9-8.333)^2 + (8-8.333)^2 = 0.111 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 0.666
\end{aligned}$$

$$SS_W = 5 \times 0.666 = 3.330$$

Degrees of freedom: $df_W = n(k - 1) = 5 \times 2 = 10$

$$MS_W = \dfrac{3.330}{10} = 0.333$$

**Step 4: Compute ICC.**

$$ICC = \dfrac{MS_B - MS_W}{MS_B + (k-1) \cdot MS_W} = \dfrac{5.767 - 0.333}{5.767 + 2 \times 0.333}$$

$$ICC = \dfrac{5.434}{6.433} = 0.845$$

$$\boxed{ICC \approx 0.845}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Per **Cicchetti's guidelines**, $ICC = 0.845 > 0.75$ indicates **excellent** inter-rater reliability.
- The 3-rater panel is highly consistent in evaluating brake pedal feel across different vehicles.
- The mean ratings per vehicle can be confidently used as a reliable engineering metric for benchmarking and target setting.
- No rater retraining is required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Subjective Methods questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Mean Rating" with "Mode Rating"** — Likert data technically has a *mode*, but for parametric correlation work, the **mean** is the correct central tendency. Always state your assumption.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Bessel's Correction** — Using $\sqrt{N}$ instead of $\sqrt{N-1}$ in the sample standard deviation formula. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this common slip.
> 
> 3. **Wrong Degrees of Freedom in ICC** — The within-vehicle df is $n(k-1)$, **not** $(n-1)(k-1)$. Mixing these up is the #1 ICC error.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring Bias Mitigation in Descriptive Answers** — A common KTU expectation: every subjective study discussion must include *at least three* bias mitigation strategies. Skipping this loses 2–3 marks.
> 
> 5. **Not Stating the Engineering Interpretation** — A numerical answer without an engineering interpretation (e.g., *"This CV indicates moderate rater agreement"*) loses **1–2 marks**. Always close with a sentence linking the number to the decision.
> 
> 6. **Confusing ICC with Cohen's Kappa** — ICC is for *continuous/interval* data; Kappa is for *categorical* data. Examiners test this distinction.
> 
> 7. **Using z = 1.96 instead of t-value** — For small samples (N < 30), the **t-distribution** must be used, not the z-distribution. This is a frequent KTU trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Subjective Evaluation** = Human-judgment-based assessment of vehicle attributes; essential for comfort, NVH, and quality perception.
- **Rating on a Scale** is the most common data-collection technique: rater assigns a number to a defined attribute.
- **Scale Types:** Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio — each with progressively richer statistical properties.
- **Common Vehicle-Industry Scales:** Likert (5/7-point), VAS (0–100 mm), NCS (1–10), Semantic Differential.
- **Absolute vs Comparative vs Anchored:** Anchored scales give the highest reliability.
- **Mean Rating Formula:** $\bar{R} = \frac{1}{N}\sum R_i$
- **Sample SD Formula:** $\sigma_R = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2}$ — use $N-1$, not $N$.
- **CV** expresses relative spread; **< 15%** is typically considered good agreement.
- **95% CI** uses **t-distribution** for $N < 30$.
- **ICC > 0.75** = excellent inter-rater reliability (Cicchetti's guideline).
- **Cohen's Kappa** is for *categorical* data; **ICC** is for *continuous* data — know when to use which.
- **Kendall's W** is used to measure agreement among many raters on rankings.
- **Key Biases:** Halo effect, central tendency, anchoring, drift, expectation, order effects.
- **Mitigations:** Training, anchoring, counterbalancing (Latin Square), blinding, re-anchoring, breaks.
- **Subjective-Objective Correlation:** Pearson $r$ and $R^2$ are the standard metrics; $R^2 > 0.7$ is a strong correlation.
- **Minimum Sample Size:** $S_{\min} = \left( \frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{\Delta} \right)^2$ — for detecting a meaningful difference $\Delta$.
- **OEM Best Practice:** Use **8–15 expert raters**, **2–3 reference trials per session**, and **session length ≤ 2 hours**.
- **KTU 2024 Focus:** Students must be able to *compute* mean, SD, CV, CI, and ICC, and *interpret* the result in an engineering context.
- **For 14-mark questions:** Always present a structured answer with **(i) Theory, (ii) Formula, (iii) Step-by-step Computation, (iv) Engineering Interpretation** — this is the KTU valuation key pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paired Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Paired Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Paired Comparison (PC)** is a fundamental psychophysical and ergonomic measurement technique wherein a human subject (evaluator) is presented with **two stimuli simultaneously (a "pair")** and is required to judge one of the following:

- **Preference**: Which stimulus is more comfortable / pleasant / acceptable.
- **Magnitude**: Which stimulus exhibits a higher intensity of a given attribute (e.g., louder, softer, harsher).
- **Similarity/Difference**: How the two stimuli differ in a specified attribute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Paired Comparison is classified under *Subjective Evaluation Methods* in automotive comfort and safety engineering. It is one of the simplest, most reliable, and most statistically robust methods of human-judgment-based assessment, forming a core building block for higher-order techniques like **Semantic Differential Scaling** and **Magnitude Estimation**.

In the automotive context, paired comparison is used extensively to evaluate subjective qualities that cannot be measured by instruments alone — such as **seat comfort, ride comfort, steering feel, NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) harshness, HVAC blower noise, interior trim aesthetics, and ergonomics of controls**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Blind Taste Test"**
>
> Imagine you are at a market, and the shopkeeper offers you two cups of tea — Cup A and Cup B. He asks, *"Which tea is sweeter?"* You sip from A, then from B, and confidently say **"B is sweeter."*
>
> You have just performed a **Paired Comparison**. You did not rate the tea on a 1-to-10 scale; you simply made a binary, comparative judgment between two options. This is precisely the principle behind Paired Comparison in automotive engineering — instead of asking *"Rate seat comfort from 1 to 10,"* we ask *"Compare Seat X and Seat Y — which is more comfortable?"*

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Stimulus** | The physical condition presented to the subject (e.g., a vehicle seat, a road surface, an HVAC setting). |
| **Pair** | A combination of two stimuli presented together for comparison. |
| **Subject/Evaluator** | The human participant rendering the comparative judgment. |
| **Judgment** | The response — usually a forced binary choice or a graded comparison. |
| **Proportion (p)** | The fraction of times a stimulus is judged "greater" across all its pairings. |
| **Scale Value (S)** | The interval-scale measure of the subjective magnitude of each stimulus. |

## 1.4 Physical & Statistical Constants Used

- **Number of Pairs for n stimuli**: $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$
- **Z-score thresholds** (Standard Normal Distribution): For two-tailed $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical value is **z = 1.96**.
- **Total possible responses per subject**: $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ judgments.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** Always remember the formula **n(n-1)/2** for total pairs. A common examiner trick is to ask: *"If 6 seats are to be compared using paired comparison, how many judgments are required per subject?"* — Answer: $6 \times 5 / 2 = \mathbf{15}$ pairs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of the Paired Comparison Method

The Paired Comparison procedure follows a strict, sequential logic. Below is the step-by-step operational framework used in automotive subjective evaluations:

- **Step 1 — Stimulus Selection:** Choose **n** stimuli (e.g., n = 4 different car seats, n = 5 road profiles, n = 6 HVAC blower modes). The stimuli must represent the full range of the attribute under study.
- **Step 2 — Pair Generation:** Construct all possible unique pairs. The number of pairs is given by the **binomial coefficient** $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$. For n = 4, this gives 6 pairs.
- **Step 3 — Presentation Order Randomization:** To eliminate **order bias** and **position bias**, the order in which pairs are presented, and the order of stimuli *within* each pair, must be **randomized** for each subject. This is critical because the first stimulus in a pair is statistically more likely to be "preferred" (a phenomenon called **primacy effect**).
- **Step 4 — Subject Judgment:** Each subject independently judges every pair. The most common format is a **forced binary choice** ("A is more comfortable" or "B is more comfortable") or a graded comparison (e.g., "A is *much more* comfortable", "A is *slightly more* comfortable", "no difference", etc.).
- **Step 5 — Tabulation:** Count the number of times each stimulus was judged "greater" (or "preferred"). Sum these counts across all subjects to get a **frequency matrix**.
- **Step 6 — Proportion Calculation:** Convert the frequency of "wins" for each stimulus into a **proportion** $p_i$, where $p_i$ is the fraction of times stimulus *i* was chosen over its competitors.
- **Step 7 — Scale Value Computation:** Using either **Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment (Case V)** or the **Bradley-Terry Model**, convert the proportions $p_i$ into **interval-scale values** $S_i$ on a psychological magnitude axis.
- **Step 8 — Statistical Validation:** Test the internal consistency of judgments using the **Coefficient of Consistency** (Kendall's u) or **Coefficient of Agreement** (Kappa). Investigate any circular triads (e.g., A > B, B > C, but C > A).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Pair Generation | Ensures **completeness** — every stimulus is directly compared with every other. |
| Randomization | Eliminates **systematic bias** from presentation order, fatigue, and learning effects. |
| Forced Choice | Forces discrimination; avoids the **central tendency bias** common in rating scales. |
| Proportion Calculation | Aggregates discrete judgments into a **continuous preference measure**. |
| Scale Value Computation | Transforms proportions (which are nonlinear and bounded) into an **interval scale** suitable for parametric statistics. |
| Consistency Check | Verifies that subjects are **internally consistent** — a hallmark of reliable subjective data. |

## 2.3 Models for Scale Value Computation

### Model A — Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment (Case V)

This is the **classical and most commonly examined** model in KTU. Its assumptions are:

- The **discriminative process** for each stimulus is normally distributed along a psychological continuum.
- All discriminal dispersions have **equal variance** ($\sigma_i = \sigma$ for all *i*).
- The correlations between discriminal dispersions for pairs of stimuli are **zero**.

Under Case V, the scale value of stimulus *i* relative to stimulus *j* is:

$$S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

where $z_{ij}$ is the **standard normal deviate (z-score)** corresponding to the proportion $p_{ij}$ of times stimulus *i* is judged greater than stimulus *j*.

In practice, the scale value of each stimulus is obtained by setting the scale value of the **least preferred stimulus** to **zero** (or the most preferred to zero, depending on convention) and computing relative scale values.

### Model B — Bradley-Terry Model

A more modern alternative that models the **probability** that stimulus *i* is preferred over stimulus *j*:

$$P(i > j) = \dfrac{\pi_i}{\pi_i + \pi_j}$$

where $\pi_i$ and $\pi_j$ are the **"worth" parameters** (analogous to scale values) for stimuli *i* and *j*. The parameters are estimated via **maximum likelihood estimation (MLE)** or, in a simplified form, via the proportion of wins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Focus:** Of the two models, **Thurstone's Case V** is the one most frequently asked in KTU University Exams for Automotive Comfort and Safety Engineering. Master the z-score table lookup and the formula $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seat Comfort Development:** Automotive OEMs (e.g., Tata, Mahindra, Toyota) use paired comparison to benchmark seat designs against competitors during the vehicle development cycle.
- **NVH Tuning:** Engineers compare different mounting systems, bushings, and damping materials by asking drivers which configuration produces a more pleasant ride feel.
- **HVAC Acoustics:** Subjective evaluation of blower noise across different fan speeds and vent designs.
- **Steering Feel:** Comparison of electric power steering (EPS) tuning maps for "on-center feel" vs. "high-speed stability."
- **Vibro-Acoustic Comfort:** Comparing the perceived harshness of different suspension setups over the same road profile.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{pairs} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Total number of unique pairs for *n* stimuli | Dimensionless integer |
| 2 | $p_i = \dfrac{w_i}{n-1}$ | Proportion of "wins" for stimulus *i* | $0 \le p_i \le 1$ |
| 3 | $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$ | Thurstone's Case V scale value difference | Interval scale (arbitrary units) |
| 4 | $S_i = \sum_{j \neq i} (S_i - S_j) / (n-1)$ | Mean of all pairwise scale differences for stimulus *i* | Interval scale |
| 5 | $u = \dfrac{8}{n(n-1)(n-2)} \left[ \sum c_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum c_i)^2}{n} \right]$ | Kendall's Coefficient of Consistency | $-1/3 \le u \le 1$ |
| 6 | $P(i > j) = \dfrac{\pi_i}{\pi_i + \pi_j}$ | Bradley-Terry preference probability | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| 7 | $z = \Phi^{-1}(p)$ | Inverse normal CDF lookup for proportion *p* | Read from standard normal table |
| 8 | $N_{total} = N_{pairs} \times N_{subjects}$ | Total number of judgments in the experiment | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Safeguard:** In the formula for Kendall's *u* above, the expression $\dfrac{8}{n(n-1)(n-2)}$ uses LaTeX `\dfrac` to avoid any vertical-pipe collision with the markdown table. In written exams, use the fraction form $\frac{8}{n(n-1)(n-2)}$.

## 2.6 Advantages and Limitations

### Advantages:
- **Simplicity:** Easy to administer; minimal training required for subjects.
- **High Reliability:** Avoids the granularity and scale-anchoring problems of rating scales.
- **Discrimination Power:** Forces subjects to make a clear distinction, reducing central-tendency bias.
- **Statistical Rigor:** Foundation for sophisticated models (Thurstone, Bradley-Terry).

### Limitations:
- **Combinatorial Explosion:** For large *n*, the number of pairs grows quadratically. For n = 10, there are **45 pairs**; for n = 20, there are **190 pairs** — leading to subject fatigue.
- **Intransitivity:** A subject may judge A > B, B > C, but C > A (circular triad), complicating analysis.
- **Binary Nature:** The simple version loses magnitude information (how much better A is than B).
- **Context Effects:** Judgment of a pair may depend on the overall set of stimuli presented (the "context" of comparison).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Unique Pairs

**Problem:** Prove that for *n* stimuli, the number of unique unordered pairs is $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.

**Derivation:**

We must choose 2 stimuli from a set of *n* stimuli, where order does **not** matter (the pair {A, B} is the same as {B, A}).

This is the definition of the **binomial coefficient** $\binom{n}{2}$.

$$\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}$$

Expanding the factorials:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n \times (n-1) \times (n-2)!}{2 \times 1 \times (n-2)!}$$

The $(n-2)!$ terms cancel:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This formula tells us the experimental workload. For n = 5, we get 5×4/2 = **10 pairs** per subject. If 20 subjects participate, the total experiment requires 200 judgments.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment (Case V)

**Problem:** Derive the scale value formula $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$ for the paired comparison method.

**Derivation:**

**Assumption 1:** When a subject is presented with stimulus *i* and asked to judge its subjective magnitude, the subject's internal response is a **random variable** $X_i$ drawn from a normal distribution:

$$X_i \sim N(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$$

where $\mu_i = S_i$ is the **scale value** (psychological magnitude) and $\sigma^2$ is the **discriminal dispersion** (assumed equal for all stimuli in Case V).

**Assumption 2:** The subject judges stimulus *i* as "greater than" stimulus *j* if and only if the internal response to *i* exceeds the internal response to *j*:

$$X_i > X_j$$

**Step 1 — Form the difference variable:** Define the difference $D = X_i - X_j$.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean of D:**

$$E[D] = E[X_i] - E[X_j] = S_i - S_j$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance of D:** Since $X_i$ and $X_j$ are **independent** (zero correlation, a Case V assumption):

$$\text{Var}(D) = \text{Var}(X_i) + \text{Var}(X_j) - 2 \cdot \text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)$$

$$\text{Var}(D) = \sigma^2 + \sigma^2 - 0 = 2\sigma^2$$

**Step 4 — Standardize the difference:** The standardized normal variable is:

$$Z = \dfrac{D - E[D]}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(D)}} = \dfrac{(X_i - X_j) - (S_i - S_j)}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the probability that i is judged greater than j:**

$$P(i > j) = P(X_i > X_j) = P(D > 0) = P\left(Z > \dfrac{0 - (S_i - S_j)}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

$$P(i > j) = P\left(Z < \dfrac{S_i - S_j}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{S_i - S_j}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF.

**Step 6 — Equate to the observed proportion:** In the experiment, $P(i > j)$ is estimated by the observed proportion $p_{ij}$ of subjects who judged *i* as greater:

$$p_{ij} = \Phi\left(\dfrac{S_i - S_j}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Invert using the inverse normal CDF:** The z-score corresponding to $p_{ij}$ is:

$$z_{ij} = \Phi^{-1}(p_{ij}) = \dfrac{S_i - S_j}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify by setting $\sigma = 1$ (the standard convention for Case V):**

$$z_{ij} = \dfrac{S_i - S_j}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 9 — Solve for the scale value difference:**

$$\boxed{S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}}$$

This is the foundational equation of Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment, Case V.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem:** Four vehicle seats (A, B, C, D) are compared for **comfort** using the paired comparison method with **20 subjects**. The table below gives the proportion of times the row seat was judged "more comfortable" than the column seat.

| | **A** | **B** | **C** | **D** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | — | 0.75 | 0.60 | 0.85 |
| **B** | 0.25 | — | 0.55 | 0.70 |
| **C** | 0.40 | 0.45 | — | 0.65 |
| **D** | 0.15 | 0.30 | 0.35 | — |

**Find the Thurstone scale values for all four seats.**

### Solution — Step by Step:

**Step 1:** Set the scale value of the **least preferred** stimulus (D) to **0** as the reference.

$$S_D = 0$$

**Step 2:** For each row, look up the z-score corresponding to the proportion $p_{ij}$ in the standard normal table.

Standard normal z-values for key proportions:

| $p$ | 0.15 | 0.25 | 0.30 | 0.35 | 0.40 | 0.45 | 0.55 | 0.60 | 0.65 | 0.70 | 0.75 | 0.85 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $z$ | -1.04 | -0.67 | -0.52 | -0.39 | -0.25 | -0.13 | 0.13 | 0.25 | 0.39 | 0.52 | 0.67 | 1.04 |

**Step 3:** Compute the scale value difference for each pair using $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$.

Recall $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

**Row A:**
- $S_A - S_B = 0.67 \times 1.414 = 0.947$
- $S_A - S_C = 0.25 \times 1.414 = 0.354$
- $S_A - S_D = 1.04 \times 1.414 = 1.470$

**Row B:**
- $S_B - S_A = -0.67 \times 1.414 = -0.947$ (consistent with above)
- $S_B - S_C = 0.13 \times 1.414 = 0.184$
- $S_B - S_D = 0.52 \times 1.414 = 0.735$

**Row C:**
- $S_C - S_A = -0.25 \times 1.414 = -0.354$
- $S_C - S_B = -0.13 \times 1.414 = -0.184$
- $S_C - S_D = 0.39 \times 1.414 = 0.551$

**Step 4:** Compute the mean scale value for each stimulus by averaging all pairwise differences relative to the reference.

Using $S_D = 0$ as the reference:

$$S_A = \frac{(S_A - S_B) + (S_A - S_C) + (S_A - S_D)}{3} = \frac{0.947 + 0.354 + 1.470}{3} = \frac{2.771}{3} \approx 0.924$$

$$S_B = \frac{(S_B - S_A) + (S_B - S_C) + (S_B - S_D)}{3} = \frac{-0.947 + 0.184 + 0.735}{3} = \frac{-0.028}{3} \approx -0.009$$

$$S_C = \frac{(S_C - S_A) + (S_C - S_B) + (S_C - S_D)}{3} = \frac{-0.354 - 0.184 + 0.551}{3} = \frac{0.013}{3} \approx 0.004$$

$$S_D = 0 \text{ (reference)}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

| Seat | Scale Value $S_i$ | Comfort Rank |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | **0.924** | **1 (Most Comfortable)** |
| **B** | -0.009 | 3 |
| **C** | 0.004 | 2 |
| **D** | **0.000** | **4 (Least Comfortable)** |

> **Engineering Conclusion:** Seat A is overwhelmingly the most comfortable. Seats B and C are nearly equivalent (both close to zero). Seat D is the least comfortable. The OEM would adopt Seat A's design features as the comfort benchmark.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kendall's Coefficient of Consistency

**Problem:** Derive the formula for Kendall's *u*, which measures the internal consistency of a single subject's paired comparison judgments.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For *n* stimuli, the total number of triplets (groups of 3) is:

$$\binom{n}{3} = \dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6}$$

**Step 2:** A **circular triad** occurs when a subject judges A > B, B > C, but C > A (a logical inconsistency). Let $c$ be the number of circular triads.

**Step 3:** A **consistent triad** (no cycle) occurs when A > B and A > C, or B > A and B > C, etc. The number of consistent triads is:

$$\binom{n}{3} - c = \dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6} - c$$

**Step 4:** The maximum possible number of circular triads for *n* stimuli is:

$$c_{max} = \dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{24} \text{ for odd } n$$

$$c_{max} = \dfrac{n^3 - 4n}{24} \text{ for even } n$$

**Step 5:** Kendall's coefficient of consistency *u* is defined as:

$$u = 1 - \dfrac{c}{c_{max}}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting the expression for $c_{max}$ (for odd *n*):

$$u = 1 - \dfrac{24c}{n(n-1)(n-2)}$$

This can also be written in the alternative form using $c_i$ (number of stimuli less preferred than *i*):

$$u = \dfrac{8}{n(n-1)(n-2)} \left[ \sum c_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum c_i)^2}{n} \right]$$

where $c_i$ is the **row sum** (number of stimuli judged less preferred than stimulus *i*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range of u:** $-1/3 \le u \le 1$.
> - $u = 1$: Perfect consistency (no circular triads).
> - $u = 0$: Random judgments.
> - $u < 0$: Anti-consistency (more circular triads than expected by chance).
