# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Paired Comparison method. List any two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

Paired Comparison is a subjective evaluation technique in which a human evaluator is presented with **two stimuli at a time** and asked to judge which one exhibits a higher intensity of a given attribute (or which one is preferred). The judgments are aggregated across multiple subjects to derive interval-scale values using models such as Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment (Case V) or the Bradley-Terry Model.

**Two Advantages:**
- **Simplicity:** Easy to administer; subjects require minimal training and the task is intuitive.
- **High Discrimination:** The forced binary choice eliminates the central-tendency bias inherent in rating scales, yielding more reliable discrimination between stimuli.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the assumptions of Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment (Case V).**

**Model Answer:**

The assumptions of Thurstone's Case V are:
- **Assumption 1:** The discriminal process associated with each stimulus is **normally distributed** along a psychological continuum.
- **Assumption 2:** The **discriminal dispersions** (variances) of all stimuli are **equal** (i.e., $\sigma_i = \sigma$ for all *i*).
- **Assumption 3:** The **correlations** between the discriminal dispersions of any two stimuli are **zero** (i.e., the discriminal processes are independent).

Under these assumptions, the scale value difference is given by: $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**In an automotive ergonomic study, five different steering wheel designs (W1, W2, W3, W4, W5) are compared for grip comfort using the Paired Comparison method. A total of 30 subjects participated. The proportion of times each row wheel was judged "more comfortable" than the column wheel is given below:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | W5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | — | 0.70 | 0.65 | 0.80 | 0.90 |
| W2 | 0.30 | — | 0.55 | 0.75 | 0.85 |
| W3 | 0.35 | 0.45 | — | 0.60 | 0.80 |
| W4 | 0.20 | 0.25 | 0.40 | — | 0.65 |
| W5 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 0.35 | — |

### **(a)** Calculate the Thurstone Case V scale values for all five wheels. Take W5 as the reference (S_W5 = 0). (7 Marks)

### **(b)** Discuss the engineering interpretation of the results and recommend a design choice. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Scale Value Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Set the reference.** $S_{W5} = 0$

**Step 2: Look up z-scores** from the standard normal table for each proportion:

| Proportion $p$ | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 0.25 | 0.30 | 0.35 | 0.40 | 0.45 | 0.55 | 0.60 | 0.65 | 0.70 | 0.75 | 0.80 | 0.85 | 0.90 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $z$-score | -1.28 | -1.04 | -0.84 | -0.67 | -0.52 | -0.39 | -0.25 | -0.13 | 0.13 | 0.25 | 0.39 | 0.52 | 0.67 | 0.84 | 1.04 | 1.28 |

**Step 3: Compute scale differences** $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \times \sqrt{2}$ where $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

**Row W1:**
- $S_{W1} - S_{W2} = 0.52 \times 1.414 = 0.735$
- $S_{W1} - S_{W3} = 0.39 \times 1.414 = 0.551$
- $S_{W1} - S_{W4} = 0.84 \times 1.414 = 1.188$
- $S_{W1} - S_{W5} = 1.28 \times 1.414 = 1.810$

**Row W2:**
- $S_{W2} - S_{W1} = -0.735$
- $S_{W2} - S_{W3} = 0.13 \times 1.414 = 0.184$
- $S_{W2} - S_{W4} = 0.67 \times 1.414 = 0.948$
- $S_{W2} - S_{W5} = 1.04 \times 1.414 = 1.470$

**Row W3:**
- $S_{W3} - S_{W1} = -0.551$
- $S_{W3} - S_{W2} = -0.184$
- $S_{W3} - S_{W4} = 0.25 \times 1.414 = 0.354$
- $S_{W3} - S_{W5} = 0.84 \times 1.414 = 1.188$

**Row W4:**
- $S_{W4} - S_{W1} = -1.188$
- $S_{W4} - S_{W2} = -0.948$
- $S_{W4} - S_{W3} = -0.354$
- $S_{W4} - S_{W5} = 0.39 \times 1.414 = 0.551$

**Step 4: Compute the mean scale value for each wheel** (average of all four pairwise differences relative to the reference W5):

$$S_{W1} = \frac{0.735 + 0.551 + 1.188 + 1.810}{4} = \frac{4.284}{4} = 1.071$$

$$S_{W2} = \frac{-0.735 + 0.184 + 0.948 + 1.470}{4} = \frac{1.867}{4} = 0.467$$

$$S_{W3} = \frac{-0.551 - 0.184 + 0.354 + 1.188}{4} = \frac{0.807}{4} = 0.202$$

$$S_{W4} = \frac{-1.188 - 0.948 - 0.354 + 0.551}{4} = \frac{-1.939}{4} = -0.485$$

$$S_{W5} = 0.000 \text{ (reference)}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Wheel | Scale Value $S_i$ | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| W1 | **+1.071** | **1 (Best)** |
| W2 | +0.467 | 2 |
| W3 | +0.202 | 3 |
| W4 | -0.485 | 4 |
| W5 | 0.000 | 5 (Reference) |

#### **Part (b) — Engineering Interpretation and Recommendation (7 Marks)**

**Interpretation:**
- **W1 is the most preferred** steering wheel design, with a scale value 1.071 units above the least preferred W5. It is judged more comfortable than W5 in 90% of pairwise comparisons, indicating a **strongly dominant preference**.
- **W2 is the second-best** (S = 0.467), significantly better than the reference but noticeably behind W1 (a gap of 0.604 scale units).
- **W3 and W5 are nearly equivalent** (S = 0.202 vs. S = 0.000), with only a marginal preference for W3.
- **W4 is the least preferred** with a negative scale value of -0.485, confirming it is judged worse than the reference W5.

**Design Recommendation:**
- The OEM should **adopt W1's design features** (likely including grip diameter, material durometer, contour shape, and thumb-rest geometry) as the **production-intent benchmark**.
- **W2 should be retained** as a fallback or variant for cost-sensitive trim levels.
- **W4 should be rejected** and its design language discontinued, as it is consistently the least preferred across all comparisons.
- A **hybrid design** incorporating W1's grip geometry with W2's material properties could be explored in the next design iteration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch or flowchart, describe the **step-by-step procedure** of the Paired Comparison method for evaluating automotive seat comfort. Clearly state the role of **randomization** and **reference stimulus** in the experiment. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For **n = 6** seat designs, determine:
- (i) The total number of unique pairs to be compared per subject. (2 Marks)
- (ii) The total number of judgments if **25 subjects** participate. (2 Marks)
- (iii) The maximum number of circular triads possible and interpret what it means if Kendall's *u* = 0.85. (3 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Procedure for Automotive Seat Comfort Evaluation (7 Marks)**

The Paired Comparison method is executed in the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Stimulus Selection:** Select the **n = 6** seat designs to be evaluated. These should represent the full design space, including the current production seat, competitor benchmarks, and concept variants.

**Step 2 — Pair Generation:** Generate all unique pairs using $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15$ pairs. Each subject will perform 15 judgments.

**Step 3 — Randomization of Presentation Order:** The order in which the 15 pairs are presented to each subject must be **randomized independently** for every subject. Additionally, the left-right placement of the two seats within each pair must be **counterbalanced** (50% of subjects see Seat A on the left, 50% see it on the right). This is critical to eliminate:
- **Order bias:** Subjects tend to prefer the stimulus they evaluate later (recency effect) or earlier (primacy effect).
- **Position bias:** Subjects often favor the stimulus on a particular side of the test rig.

**Step 4 — Subject Judgment:** Each subject sits in Seat A for a standardized duration (e.g., 3 minutes), then in Seat B, and renders a **forced binary judgment**: "Seat A is more comfortable" or "Seat B is more comfortable."

**Step 5 — Tabulation and Proportion Calculation:** The proportion $p_{ij}$ of subjects who prefer seat *i* over seat *j* is computed for all 15 pairs.

**Step 6 — Scale Value Computation:** Thurstone's Case V is applied: $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$, and scale values are computed by setting the least preferred seat to zero.

**Step 7 — Consistency Check:** Kendall's *u* is computed to verify the internal consistency of each subject's judgments.

**Role of Randomization:** Ensures that the observed preferences reflect **true ergonomic differences** rather than artifacts of the experimental setup. Without randomization, systematic biases could masquerade as design preferences, leading to incorrect engineering decisions.

**Role of Reference Stimulus:** Provides a **fixed anchor** for the psychological scale, enabling conversion of relative preferences into **absolute interval-scale values**. It also serves as a **calibration check** — if the reference is rated unusually high or low, it signals experimental drift.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks)**

**(i) Total number of unique pairs per subject:**

$$N_{pairs} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} = \mathbf{15 \text{ pairs}}$$

**(ii) Total number of judgments with 25 subjects:**

$$N_{total} = N_{pairs} \times N_{subjects} = 15 \times 25 = \mathbf{375 \text{ judgments}}$$

**(iii) Maximum number of circular triads (for n = 6, even):**

For even *n*, the maximum number of circular triads is:

$$c_{max} = \dfrac{n^3 - 4n}{24} = \dfrac{6^3 - 4 \times 6}{24} = \dfrac{216 - 24}{24} = \dfrac{192}{24} = \mathbf{8 \text{ circular triads}}$$

**Interpretation of u = 0.85:**

A Kendall's *u* value of **0.85** indicates **very high internal consistency** in the subject's judgments. Since *u* ranges from $-1/3$ to $+1$, a value of 0.85 is close to the maximum of 1, meaning that the subject exhibited only **1 - 0.85 = 15%** of the maximum possible circular triads. In practical terms:
- The subject's judgments form a **nearly perfect transitive ordering** (e.g., A > B > C > D > E > F).
- The data from this subject is **highly reliable** and can be included in the aggregate analysis with confidence.
- In contrast, a subject with *u* < 0.0 would be flagged as an **inconsistent respondent** and their data may need to be excluded or treated separately.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> - **Mark Loss Pitfall #1 — Forgetting the √2 factor:** The most common error in KTU exams is computing $S_i - S_j = z_{ij}$ instead of $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$. This loses **2 to 3 marks** out of 7 in Part (a). Always state the full formula explicitly.
> - **Mark Loss Pitfall #2 — Incorrect pair count formula:** Students sometimes write $n^2$ or $n(n+1)/2$ instead of $n(n-1)/2$. For an unordered pair, it is **always** $\binom{n}{2} = n(n-1)/2$.
> - **Mark Loss Pitfall #3 — Confusing the z-score direction:** When the proportion is less than 0.50, the z-score is **negative**. A negative z-score yields a **negative** scale value difference, which is correct and expected — do not panic and "correct" it to a positive value.
> - **Mark Loss Pitfall #4 — Omitting the reference statement:** Always explicitly state which stimulus is set as the reference (typically the least preferred) and its scale value (conventionally 0). Examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.
> - **Mark Loss Pitfall #5 — Skipping engineering interpretation:** For 7-mark sub-parts, the calculation alone is worth only 3-4 marks. The **engineering interpretation and design recommendation** carry the remaining 3-4 marks. Never skip this section.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Paired Comparison (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** A subjective evaluation method where two stimuli are compared at a time and a preference or magnitude judgment is rendered.
- **Total Pairs Formula:** $N_{pairs} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — use this for every "how many comparisons" question.
- **Thurstone's Case V Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $S_i - S_j = z_{ij} \cdot \sqrt{2}$
- **The √2 factor** arises from the variance of the difference of two independent normal variables: $\text{Var}(X_i - X_j) = 2\sigma^2$.
- **z-score lookup:** $z_{ij} = \Phi^{-1}(p_{ij})$ — read from the standard normal table; negative for $p < 0.50$.
- **Reference Stimulus:** Set the least preferred stimulus's scale value to 0; all others are computed relative to it.
- **Mean Scale Value:** $S_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j \neq i} (S_i - S_j)}{n-1}$ — average all pairwise differences for stimulus *i*.
- **Bradley-Terry Alternative:** $P(i > j) = \dfrac{\pi_i}{\pi_i + \pi_j}$ — used in modern ergonomic studies as an MLE-based alternative.
- **Kendall's u Range:** $-1/3 \le u \le 1$. Values near 1 = high consistency; values near 0 = random; negative = anti-consistent.
- **Circular Triad:** A > B > C > A — a logical inconsistency in a subject's judgments. The maximum number is $c_{max} = \dfrac{n^3 - 4n}{24}$ for even *n*.
- **Randomization is Mandatory:** Always randomize pair presentation order and left-right placement to eliminate order and position bias.
- **Automotive Applications:** Seat comfort, steering feel, NVH harshness, HVAC acoustics, ride comfort, vibration evaluation, and ergonomic control layout.
- **Forced Binary Choice:** The simplest and most reliable response format — avoids the central-tendency bias of rating scales.
- **Limitation — Combinatorial Growth:** For n > 10, the number of pairs becomes impractical (> 45); consider incomplete block designs or category scaling.
- **Context Effect:** The judgment of a pair depends on the overall set of stimuli — changing the set can change the relative rankings (this is a known limitation).
- **KTU Board Pattern:** Expect a 3-mark Part A (definition or assumption question) and a 14-mark Part B (calculation + interpretation question with internal choice).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thurstone Method. Application of Evaluation Techniques - Checklists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thurstone Method. Application of Evaluation Techniques - Checklists

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Thurstone Scaling Method

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> The *Thurstone Method* (formally the **Law of Comparative Judgment**, 1927) is a psychophysical scaling procedure developed by psychologist **Louis Leon Thurstone**. It converts qualitative paired-comparison judgments ("A is more comfortable than B") into a quantitative **interval scale of subjective magnitudes** along with each item's **discriminal dispersion** (uncertainty spread).

In automotive comfort engineering, the Thurstone Method is used to convert driver/rider *subjective comfort impressions* of seat cushions, ride quality, NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness), or steering feedback into a defensible numerical scale — without relying on a single biased Likert-style number.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Taste Test of Cars"

Imagine three drivers — *Driver X*, *Driver Y*, *Driver Z* — and you want to rank the *steering feedback feel* of three cars: Car A, Car B, Car C. You cannot just ask them "rate from 1 to 10" because each driver uses "10" differently (some never give 10, some give 10 for average). 

Instead, you pair the cars and ask:
> *"In this pair, which car gives BETTER steering feel?"*

Across many such pairs, you build proportions: e.g., **65%** of judges prefer A over B. Thurstone's math converts that proportion into a **scale value** — the psychological "distance" between A and B on an internal comfort axis. The spread of opinions becomes the **discriminal dispersion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Output of Thurstone Method:**
> 1. **Scale Value (Sⱼ)** — position of stimulus *j* on the subjective continuum.
> 2. **Discriminal Dispersion (δⱼ)** — how *uncertain* or *varied* judgments about stimulus *j* are.

---

## 1.2 Checklists (Application of Evaluation Techniques)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Checklist** is a structured, pre-formatted evaluation instrument consisting of items, criteria, or behaviours to be observed, marked "Yes/No," "Present/Absent," or on a binary/scaled response. It operationalises an ergonomic/safety audit into repeatable, low-cognitive-load observations.

In automotive safety engineering, checklists are the **workhorse tool** for crash-safety audits, ISO 26262 functional-safety reviews, vehicle homologation inspections, and driver ergonomic assessments.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Pilot's Pre-Flight Mental Model"

A pilot does not "imagine" whether flaps are deployed — they **tick the box**. A safety engineer does not "feel" whether a child-seat ISOFIX anchor is correctly latched — they verify it against a **physical checklist**. Checklists externalise expert knowledge into a sequence of *observable, verifiable* facts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Checklists Matter in KTU Context:**
> - Convert qualitative ergonomic observations into **quantitative compliance scores**.
> - Reduce examiner/inspector subjectivity.
> - Provide an **audit trail** required by ISO 26262, ECE R14, and AIS standards for vehicle safety certification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment — Underlying Logic

Thurstone postulated that when a judge evaluates a stimulus, the judgment is drawn from a **normal distribution** of momentary psychological responses centered on a true scale value $S_j$, with a standard deviation $\delta_j$ called the **discriminal dispersion**.

For two stimuli $j$ and $k$, the probability that $j$ is judged "greater" (e.g., more comfortable) than $k$ is:

$$
P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{S_j - S_k}{\sqrt{\delta_j^{2} + \delta_k^{2}}}\right)
$$

Where:
- $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the **cumulative standard normal distribution function** (often tabulated as $\Phi(z) = p$).
- $S_j, S_k$ are the **psychological scale values**.
- $\delta_j, \delta_k$ are the **discriminal dispersions** (standard deviations of judgment).

### The Five Cases of the Law

Thurstone himself listed **five practical cases** based on simplifying assumptions:

| Case | Assumption on $\delta_j$ | Assumption on Correlation | Common Use |
|:----:|:------------------------|:--------------------------|:-----------|
| **I** | All different | Complete | Theoretical baseline |
| **II** | All different | Zero | Rare |
| **III** | All equal | Complete | Sensory continua |
| **IV** | All equal | Zero | Most theoretical |
| **V** | All equal | Zero (postulated "complete discriminal process") | **Most widely used in practice** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Engineering and ergonomics exams almost always default to **Case V** — equal discriminal dispersions and a zero correlation between momentary judgments. Master Case V first.

## 2.2 Case V — Working Equations

Under Case V, $\delta_j = \delta_k = \delta$ (a single common dispersion), so the formula simplifies dramatically:

$$
P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{S_j - S_k}{\delta\sqrt{2}}\right)
$$

Inverting through the inverse normal $\Phi^{-1}$ gives the **scale value difference**:

$$
S_j - S_k = z_{jk}\cdot\delta\sqrt{2}
$$

Where $z_{jk}$ is the standard-normal deviate corresponding to the observed proportion $P_{jk}$.

For convenience, the scale is **anchored** by setting the smallest or reference stimulus to $S_{\text{ref}} = 0$. Every other scale value is computed relative to this anchor:

$$
S_j = z_{\text{ref},\,j}\cdot\delta\sqrt{2}
$$

### Discriminal Dispersion Estimate (for Case V, per stimulus)

For each stimulus $j$ judged across $n$ paired comparisons:

$$
\hat{\delta}_j = \frac{\sqrt{P_j(1-P_j)}}{\varphi(z_j)\cdot n}
$$

Where $\varphi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-z^2/2}$ is the **standard normal probability density function**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thurstone Method — Master Formula Table**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-----:|:------|
| 1 | Probability of preference | $P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{S_j - S_k}{\sqrt{\delta_j^{2}+\delta_k^{2}}}\right)$ | dimensionless | General Law of Comparative Judgment |
| 2 | Case V probability | $P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{S_j - S_k}{\delta\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Equal dispersions assumed |
| 3 | Scale value difference | $S_j - S_k = z_{jk}\cdot\delta\sqrt{2}$ | Psychological units | Inverted via $\Phi^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Anchored scale value | $S_j = z_{\text{ref},j}\cdot\delta\sqrt{2}$ | Psychological units | Reference stimulus set to 0 |
| 5 | Discriminal dispersion | $\hat{\delta}_j = \dfrac{\sqrt{P_j(1-P_j)}}{\varphi(z_j)\cdot n}$ | Psychological units | Per-stimulus estimate |
| 6 | Common dispersion (Case V) | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.51}{n}}\;$ (approx.) | Psychological units | Gulliksen's approximation |
| 7 | Standard normal density | $\varphi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}$ | dimensionless | Used for dispersion estimates |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas let an automotive ergonomist convert raw "X is more comfortable than Y" tallies into a defensible *interval-scale comfort ranking* — useful for benchmarking seat designs, suspension tuning, or H-point (Hip Point) selection.

---

## 2.4 Checklists — Types and Theoretical Foundations

A checklist is operationally defined by four design parameters:

1. **Response format** — dichotomous (Yes/No), trichotomous, or scaled.
2. **Item granularity** — global ("Are brakes functioning?") vs. atomic ("Front-left brake pad ≥ 2 mm?").
3. **Scoring rule** — unweighted, weighted, or critical-incident.
4. **Mode of administration** — self-report, observer-rated, or instrument-augmented.

### Four Major Checklist Types in Automotive Safety

| Type | Description | Automotive Example |
|:-----|:------------|:-------------------|
| **Simple Binary** | Yes / No on each item | Tyre pressure check |
| **Weighted Scoring** | Each item carries a weight | NCAP passive-safety audit |
| **Likert-Rated** | Each item rated 1–5 | Ergonomic seat-comfort survey |
| **Critical-Incident** | Records abnormal events | Crash-test observation log |

### Reliability & Validity of a Checklist

Two psychometric properties are routinely tested:

- **Inter-rater reliability** — measured by **Cohen's Kappa** $\kappa$:
$$
\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}
$$
where $p_o$ is observed agreement and $p_e$ is chance agreement. $\kappa > 0.7$ is the engineering-acceptance threshold.

- **Internal consistency** — measured by **Cronbach's alpha** $\alpha$:
$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma_{Y_i}^{2}}{\sigma_{X}^{2}}\right)
$$
where $k$ is the number of items, $\sigma_{Y_i}^{2}$ is the variance of item $i$, and $\sigma_{X}^{2}$ is the variance of the total score.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Thurstone Case V Scale Value

We begin from the general Law of Comparative Judgment and apply the **Case V constraints** to extract a usable engineering formula.

### Step 1 — Start from the General Law

$$
P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{S_j - S_k}{\sqrt{\delta_j^{2} + \delta_k^{2}}}\right)
$$

This is a statement that the *probability* of preferring stimulus $j$ over stimulus $k$ is the area under the standard normal curve to the left of the *z*-score $(S_j - S_k)/\sqrt{\delta_j^{2}+\delta_k^{2}}$.

### Step 2 — Apply Case V Assumptions

Case V asserts two simultaneous simplifications:
- **Equal dispersions:** $\delta_j = \delta_k = \delta \;\;\forall j, k$
- **Complete discriminal process:** the momentary discriminal processes are statistically independent (zero correlation)

Under equal dispersions, the denominator collapses:

$$
\sqrt{\delta_j^{2} + \delta_k^{2}} = \sqrt{\delta^{2} + \delta^{2}} = \sqrt{2\delta^{2}} = \delta\sqrt{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{S_j - S_k}{\delta\sqrt{2}}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Invert the Cumulative Normal

We define the *z*-score corresponding to the observed proportion $P_{jk}$:

$$
z_{jk} = \Phi^{-1}(P_{jk})
$$

Equating:

$$
z_{jk} = \frac{S_j - S_k}{\delta\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Scale Value Difference

Multiplying both sides by $\delta\sqrt{2}$:

$$
S_j - S_k = z_{jk}\cdot\delta\sqrt{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Anchor the Scale

To produce an absolute (non-relative) scale, we choose a reference stimulus (conventionally the *least preferred* or the *smallest index*). Set $S_1 = 0$. Then for any other stimulus $j$:

$$
S_j = z_{1j}\cdot\delta\sqrt{2}
$$

This is the **Case V Thurstone scale value** — the result students must reproduce in the exam.

### Step 6 — Discriminal Dispersion from Sample Proportions

For a finite sample of $n$ judges, the observed proportion $\hat{P}_j$ for stimulus $j$ deviates from the true $P_j$. Thurstone derived the unbiased estimator:

$$
\hat{\delta}_j = \frac{\sqrt{\hat{P}_j(1-\hat{P}_j)}}{\varphi(z_j)\,n}
$$

**Derivation sketch (intermediate step):**
- The standard error of a proportion is $\sqrt{P(1-P)/n}$.
- The variance of the corresponding *z*-score is approximately $1/[n\,\varphi(z)^2]$.
- Discriminal dispersion relates to this variance by the factor $2$ (because each comparison contributes to two dispersions).
- Combining yields $\hat{\delta}_j = \sqrt{\hat{P}_j(1-\hat{P}_j)}/[\varphi(z_j)\,n]$.

For Case V with a common $\delta$, the **Gulliksen approximation** for $n$ total paired comparisons per cell is:

$$
\hat{\delta} \approx \sqrt{\frac{1.51}{n}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Automotive Seat Comfort

**Problem:** Three seat designs A, B, C are compared in pairs by 30 judges. Each judge picks which seat is more comfortable. Tally results:

| Pair | A preferred over B | B preferred over C | A preferred over C |
|:----:|:------------------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| Tally | 22 / 30 | 18 / 30 | 25 / 30 |

**Required:** Compute Case V scale values anchored at C ($S_C = 0$).

### Step 1 — Convert Tally to Proportions

$$
P_{AC} = \frac{25}{30} = 0.8333 \qquad P_{BC} = \frac{18}{30} = 0.6000 \qquad P_{AB} = \frac{22}{30} = 0.7333
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Proportions to *z*-Scores (using standard normal tables)

$$
z_{AC} = \Phi^{-1}(0.8333) \approx 0.97
$$

$$
z_{BC} = \Phi^{-1}(0.6000) \approx 0.25
$$

$$
z_{AB} = \Phi^{-1}(0.7333) \approx 0.62
$$

### Step 3 — Estimate Common Discriminal Dispersion (Case V)

Total paired comparisons per cell $n = 30$. Using Gulliksen's approximation:

$$
\hat{\delta} = \sqrt{\frac{1.51}{30}} = \sqrt{0.05033} \approx 0.224
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Scale Values

Using $S_C = 0$ as anchor:

$$
S_A = z_{AC}\cdot\hat{\delta}\sqrt{2} = 0.97 \times 0.224 \times 1.414 \approx 0.307
$$

$$
S_B = z_{BC}\cdot\hat{\delta}\sqrt{2} = 0.25 \times 0.224 \times 1.414 \approx 0.079
$$

$$
S_C = 0.000 \quad \text{(by anchor)}
$$

### Step 5 — Verification via Transitivity

We expect $S_A - S_B \approx z_{AB}\cdot\hat{\delta}\sqrt{2}$:

$$
S_A - S_B = 0.307 - 0.079 = 0.228
$$

$$
z_{AB}\cdot\hat{\delta}\sqrt{2} = 0.62 \times 0.224 \times 1.414 \approx 0.196
$$

The small discrepancy (0.228 vs. 0.196) is the **typical Case V inconsistency** — Thurstone acknowledged that real human judgments are not perfectly transitive. A more rigorous estimator (e.g., **Mosteller's iterative solution**) would minimise such residuals.

### Final Ranking

$$
\boxed{S_A = 0.307 > S_B = 0.079 > S_C = 0.000}
$$

Seat **A** is judged most comfortable, **B** intermediate, **C** least comfortable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thurstone Method — Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define stimuli set J"] --> B["Form all paired comparisons j,k"]
    B --> C["Recruit N judges per pair"]
    C --> D["Collect paired preference tallies"]
    D --> E["Compute proportions P_jk"]
    E --> F["Look up z-scores via Phi inverse"]
    F --> G{"Apply Case V assumptions?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Estimate common delta via Gulliksen"]
    G -- No --> I["Estimate per-stimulus delta_j"]
    H --> J["Compute S_j minus S_k equals z_jk times delta sqrt 2"]
    I --> K["Solve general LCM system"]
    J --> L["Anchor scale at reference stimulus"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["Output final interval scale"]
    M --> N["Validate via transitivity residual"]
    N --> O["End"]
```

## 4.2 Checklist Application — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Domain Expert"] --> P2["Item Pool Generation"]
    P3["Standards Body ISO ISO 26262 ECE R14"] --> P2
    P2 --> P4["Pilot Test with N raters"]
    P4 --> P5["Compute Cohen Kappa"]
    P5 --> P6{"Kappa greater than 0.7"}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7["Deploy Final Checklist"]
    P6 -- No --> P8["Revise ambiguous items"]
    P8 --> P4
    P7 --> P9["Field Audit by Inspectors"]
    P9 --> P10["Score each item"]
    P10 --> P11["Aggregate weighted score"]
    P11 --> P12["Pass Fail Decision"]
```

## 4.3 Taxonomy of Evaluation Techniques (Positioning Thurstone & Checklists)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Evaluation Techniques for Comfort and Safety"]
    R --> S1["Subjective Scaling Methods"]
    R --> S2["Objective Observation Methods"]
    S1 --> T1["Thurstone Method Paired Comparison"]
    S1 --> T2["Likert Scale"]
    S1 --> T3["Semantic Differential"]
    S2 --> C1["Simple Binary Checklist"]
    S2 --> C2["Weighted Scoring Checklist"]
    S2 --> C3["Critical Incident Checklist"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Law of Comparative Judgment as proposed by Thurstone. Mention its five cases.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Law of Comparative Judgment** (Thurstone, 1927) states that when two stimuli $j$ and $k$ are presented to a judge, the probability that $j$ is judged greater than $k$ follows the relation:

$$
P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{S_j - S_k}{\sqrt{\delta_j^{2}+\delta_k^{2}}}\right)
$$

The five cases arise from assumptions on the **discriminal dispersions** $\delta_j$ and the **correlation** between momentary discriminal processes:
- **Case I:** All dispersions different, complete correlation.
- **Case II:** All dispersions different, zero correlation.
- **Case III:** Equal dispersions, complete correlation.
- **Case IV:** Equal dispersions, zero correlation.
- **Case V:** Equal dispersions, zero correlation, complete discriminal process. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a simple binary checklist and a weighted scoring checklist. Give one automotive example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple Binary Checklist | Weighted Scoring Checklist |
|:--------|:-----------------------|:--------------------------|
| Response | Yes / No | Yes / No / Partial, each with a weight |
| Output | Pass / Fail | Numerical aggregate score |
| Cognitive Load | Very low | Moderate |
| Sensitivity | Low (coarse) | High (fine resolution) |
| Auto Example | Tyre pressure check before drive | Euro NCAP passive-safety audit |
| Standards Tie-in | Pre-drive inspection | ISO 26262 functional-safety audit |

A weighted checklist assigns each item a numeric weight reflecting its **safety criticality**, then aggregates to a final score for ranking. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the Case V scale value formula of the Thurstone Method, starting from the general Law of Comparative Judgment. Clearly state the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Three suspension tuning variants X, Y, Z are compared in pairs by 40 test drivers for *ride comfort*. Tallies: X preferred over Y = 28/40; Y preferred over Z = 26/40; X preferred over Z = 32/40. Compute the Case V scale values anchored at Z. Comment on the transitivities observed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **General Law:** $P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{S_j - S_k}{\sqrt{\delta_j^{2}+\delta_k^{2}}}\right)$ — defines the probability of preferring $j$ over $k$ as a normal cumulative of the standardised scale-value difference. (1 mark)
2. **Case V Assumption 1:** $\delta_j = \delta_k = \delta$ for all $j,k$ — uniform uncertainty in human judgment. (1 mark)
3. **Case V Assumption 2:** Zero correlation between successive discriminal processes (complete discriminal process). (1 mark)
4. **Substitution:** Denominator becomes $\sqrt{\delta^{2}+\delta^{2}} = \delta\sqrt{2}$. So $P_{jk} = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{S_j - S_k}{\delta\sqrt{2}}\right)$. (1 mark)
5. **Inversion:** Define $z_{jk} = \Phi^{-1}(P_{jk})$. Then $z_{jk} = \dfrac{S_j - S_k}{\delta\sqrt{2}}$. (1 mark)
6. **Solving for $S_j$:** $S_j - S_k = z_{jk}\,\delta\sqrt{2}$. (1 mark)
7. **Anchoring:** Set $S_{\text{ref}} = 0$, then $S_j = z_{\text{ref},\,j}\,\delta\sqrt{2}$. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Proportions:** $P_{XZ} = 32/40 = 0.800$, $P_{YZ} = 26/40 = 0.650$, $P_{XY} = 28/40 = 0.700$. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — z-scores:** $z_{XZ} = \Phi^{-1}(0.800) \approx 0.842$; $z_{YZ} = \Phi^{-1}(0.650) \approx 0.385$; $z_{XY} = \Phi^{-1}(0.700) \approx 0.524$. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Common dispersion:** $\hat{\delta} = \sqrt{1.51/40} = \sqrt{0.03775} \approx 0.1943$. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Scale values (anchor $S_Z = 0$):**

$$
S_X = 0.842 \times 0.1943 \times \sqrt{2} = 0.842 \times 0.2748 \approx 0.231
$$

$$
S_Y = 0.385 \times 0.1943 \times \sqrt{2} = 0.385 \times 0.2748 \approx 0.106
$$

$$
S_Z = 0.000 \quad \text{(1 mark for these computations)}
$$

**Step 5 — Transitivity check:**

Observed $S_X - S_Y = 0.231 - 0.106 = 0.125$.

Predicted from direct AB comparison: $z_{XY}\,\delta\sqrt{2} = 0.524 \times 0.2748 \approx 0.144$.

**Discrepancy:** $\vert 0.125 - 0.144 \vert = 0.019$ — small, indicating good transitivity. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Suspension X provides best ride comfort, Y intermediate, Z least preferred. The small transitivity residual (≈ 0.019) confirms Case V is a reasonable model for this dataset. (2 marks for ranking + comment)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Common Error:** Students forget the $\sqrt{2}$ factor when going from $z_{jk}$ to $S_j - S_k$. Mark deduction: **–1 mark**.
> - **Common Error:** Not anchoring at any stimulus — gives a relative (not absolute) scale. Examiner expects explicit statement that $S_Z = 0$. Mark deduction: **–0.5 marks**.
> - **Common Error:** Computing $z$-scores using wrong tails of $\Phi$. Always confirm: $\Phi(z) = P_{jk}$ means $z$ corresponds to *upper-tail area below the preference proportion*. Mark deduction: **–0.5 marks**.
> - **Common Error:** Using Case I or Case III formulas (which would change the dispersion term). Examiner will deduct **–1 mark** if the wrong case is applied without justification.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** What is a checklist as an evaluation technique? Explain its types with reference to automotive safety audits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a 10-item weighted checklist for evaluating the *driver-seat ergonomic comfort* of a passenger car. Justify each item's weight and explain how Cronbach's alpha would be used to validate the checklist's internal consistency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definition and Types (7 marks):**

A **checklist** is a structured, pre-defined set of items, criteria, or observable conditions that an evaluator marks as present/absent, yes/no, or on a graded scale. It operationalises expert judgement into a reproducible, auditable procedure. (1 mark)

**Types relevant to automotive safety:**

1. **Simple Binary Checklist** — Yes/No items; e.g., pre-drive check of brakes, lights, mirrors. (1 mark)
2. **Weighted Scoring Checklist** — each item carries a numeric weight; output is an aggregate score; used in **Euro NCAP**, **AIS** audits. (1.5 marks)
3. **Likert-Rated Checklist** — items rated 1–5; used for ergonomic subjective surveys. (1 mark)
4. **Critical-Incident Checklist** — only abnormal events are recorded; used in crash-test observation logs. (1 mark)
5. **Combined / Hybrid** — mixes binary gate items with weighted sub-items; used in **ISO 26262 functional-safety audits** (ASIL-rated items). (1.5 marks)

**(b) Design of a 10-item Ergonomic Comfort Checklist (7 marks):**

| # | Item | Weight (out of 10) | Justification |
|:-:|:-----|:-----------------:|:--------------|
| 1 | Lumbar support adjustability | 1.5 | Critical for long-drive fatigue prevention |
| 2 | Seat cushion firmness (subjective) | 1.0 | Pressure distribution, blood flow |
| 3 | Headrest position relative to H-point | 1.0 | Whiplash protection, ECE R17 |
| 4 | Reach to steering wheel without strain | 1.5 | SAE J1517 driver package |
| 5 | Pedal reach and legroom | 1.0 | Braking reaction time |
| 6 | Visibility of instrument cluster | 0.5 | Cognitive load |
| 7 | A-pillar blind-spot size | 0.5 | Side-impact avoidance |
| 8 | Seatbelt comfort across shoulder | 1.0 | Compliance with wearing |
| 9 | Ingress/egress ease (elderly users) | 1.0 | Universal design |
| 10 | Vibration isolation (idle) | 1.0 | NVH — comfort at low frequency |
| **Total** | | **10.0** | Pass threshold = 7.0/10 |

(5 marks for the table & design; 2 marks for the validation discussion)

**Validation via Cronbach's alpha:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma_{Y_i}^{2}}{\sigma_{X}^{2}}\right)
$$

where $k = 10$ items, $\sigma_{Y_i}^{2}$ is the variance of item $i$'s score, and $\sigma_{X}^{2}$ is the variance of the total score across raters. The checklist is acceptable if $\alpha \geq 0.7$; values $0.8$–$0.9$ are ideal. If $\alpha < 0.7$, redundant or low-correlation items are dropped and the alpha recomputed. (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Common Error:** Confusing Cronbach's alpha (internal consistency) with Cohen's kappa (inter-rater reliability). Examiner will deduct **–1 mark** if these are swapped.
> - **Common Error:** Designing items that are *not directly observable* (e.g., "driver feels happy"). Checklists demand **observable, verifiable** items. Mark deduction: **–0.5 marks per non-observable item**.
> - **Common Error:** Failing to assign weights or assigning equal weights to all items. Weighted checklists must show *justified differentiation*. Mark deduction: **–1 mark**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Thurstone Method & Checklists**

- ☐ **Thurstone Method** converts paired-comparison judgments into an **interval scale** of psychological magnitudes.
- ☐ The general **Law of Comparative Judgment** uses the standard normal CDF $\Phi$ and a $\sqrt{\delta_j^{2}+\delta_k^{2}}$ dispersion term.
- ☐ **Case V** (equal dispersions, zero correlation) is the engineering default — denominator becomes $\delta\sqrt{2}$.
- ☐ The fundamental scale-value formula: $\boxed{S_j - S_k = z_{jk}\cdot\delta\sqrt{2}}$.
- ☐ Always **anchor** the scale by setting one reference stimulus to $S_{\text{ref}} = 0$.
- ☐ Discriminal dispersion $\delta_j$ quantifies **judgment uncertainty** — large $\delta$ means judges disagree.
- ☐ Gulliksen's approximation: $\delta \approx \sqrt{1.51/n}$ for $n$ paired comparisons per cell.
- ☐ Transitivity residuals quantify Case V model fit; use Mosteller's iterative method for better estimates.
- ☐ **Checklists** are structured observation tools with binary, weighted, Likert, or critical-incident formats.
- ☐ Checklist **reliability** is measured by **Cohen's kappa** ($\kappa > 0.7$ acceptable); **internal consistency** by **Cronbach's alpha** ($\alpha > 0.7$ acceptable).
- ☐ Automotive applications: ride-comfort benchmarking, NCAP safety audits, ISO 26262 functional-safety reviews, ergonomic seat assessments.
- ☐ Two psychometric anchors to remember: $\kappa$ = agreement-between-raters; $\alpha$ = within-instrument consistency.
- ☐ Checklists must contain **observable** items — never subjective adjectives without an operational definition.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Thurstone Method & Application of Evaluation Techniques — Checklists**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Observational Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Observational Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Observational Studies in Automotive Safety Engineering

## 1. Core Technical Definition

An **Observational Study** is a type of empirical research investigation in which the researcher observes subjects in their natural environment and records data without manipulating any variables, treatments, or conditions. In the context of **Automotive Comfort and Safety Engineering**, observational studies are used to gather real-world data on driver behaviour, vehicle performance under actual operating conditions, traffic interactions, occupant kinematics during crashes, and human factors influencing comfort and safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An observational study is a non-experimental research design in which the investigator measures outcomes without assigning participants to specific exposure or treatment groups. The independent variable (exposure) is observed as it naturally occurs, and statistical associations between exposure and outcome are analyzed to draw inferences.

## 2. Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Traffic Watcher:**
> Imagine a traffic engineer standing at a busy junction with a clipboard (or a camera). Instead of forcing drivers to do anything specific, the engineer simply **watches** who signals, who brakes hard, who uses mobile phones, and who nearly causes a collision. No driver is told what to do. This is exactly what an **observational study** does — it records what *naturally* happens, free from any artificial interference.

In automotive safety, this approach is essential because:
- We **cannot ethically** crash test humans in real vehicles.
- We **cannot realistically** simulate every traffic condition in a lab.
- Real human behaviour often **deviates** from controlled test conditions.

## 3. Key Characteristics

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **No Manipulation** | The researcher does not assign treatments |
| **Natural Setting** | Data is collected in real-world conditions |
| **Observation-Based** | Outcomes are recorded as they occur |
| **Hypothesis-Generating** | Often used to identify risk factors |
| **Ethical Compliance** | Safer than experimental crash studies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Observational Studies Matter in Automotive Safety:**
> Modern ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) validation, ergonomic seating design, crashworthiness ratings, and driver fatigue monitoring all rely heavily on observational evidence collected from naturalistic driving studies, field operational tests, and crash database analyses (e.g., NASS-CDS, GIDAS).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Classification of Observational Studies

Observational studies are broadly classified into three principal designs, each serving a different research question in automotive safety:

### A. Cohort Study (Longitudinal / Prospective)

- A group of subjects is identified based on **exposure status** (e.g., seatbelt wearers vs. non-wearers).
- They are **followed forward in time** to observe outcomes (e.g., injuries in a crash).
- Used to calculate **Relative Risk (RR)** and **Incidence Rates**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Example:** Following 5,000 drivers (2,500 seatbelt users and 2,500 non-users) over 3 years and comparing crash injury rates between the two groups.

### B. Case-Control Study (Retrospective)

- Subjects are selected based on **outcome status** (e.g., drivers who died in crashes vs. drivers who survived).
- Their **past exposure** is then traced backwards.
- Used to calculate **Odds Ratio (OR)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Example:** Comparing 200 drivers involved in fatal crashes (cases) with 200 drivers in non-fatal crashes (controls) to determine if airbag deployment reduced fatality odds.

### C. Cross-Sectional Study (Prevalence Study)

- Data on exposure and outcome are collected at a **single point in time**.
- Provides a "snapshot" of the population.
- Used to calculate **Prevalence** and is purely descriptive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Example:** A roadside survey recording the number of vehicles with worn tyres and correlating with immediate traffic violation data.

## 2. Hierarchy of Evidence

```
Level of Evidence (High to Low for Observational Studies):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Cohort Study (Prospective)  ← Strongest │
│  Case-Control Study (Retrospective)      │
│  Cross-Sectional Study    ← Weakest      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 3. Sources of Error in Observational Studies

| Bias Type | Description | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Selection Bias** | Subjects not representative | Only city drivers studied, ignoring rural patterns |
| **Information Bias** | Inaccurate measurement | Driver self-reports of speed (under-reporting) |
| **Recall Bias** | Memory errors in retrospective studies | Crash victims misremembering pre-crash actions |
| **Confounding** | A third variable distorts association | Age confounds relationship between experience and crash rate |
| **Observer Bias** | Researcher's expectations affect data | Noting "risky" behaviour more often in young drivers |

## 4. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevalence** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Number of existing cases}}{\text{Total population at a given time}}$ | Proportion of vehicles with a defect at a checkpoint |
| **Incidence Rate** | $IR = \dfrac{\text{New cases}}{\text{Person-time at risk}}$ | New crashes per million vehicle-km |
| **Relative Risk (RR)** | $RR = \dfrac{\text{Incidence in exposed}}{\text{Incidence in unexposed}}$ | Crash risk of mobile-phone users vs. non-users |
| **Odds Ratio (OR)** | $OR = \dfrac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}$ | Association between airbag and fatality in case-control |
| **Attributable Risk (AR)** | $AR = I_e - I_u$ | Excess risk due to exposure |
| **Population Attributable Risk (PAR)** | $PAR = P_e \cdot (RR - 1) / \big[P_e \cdot (RR - 1) + 1\big]$ | Impact of exposure at population level |
| **Confounding Adjustment** | Stratified Mantel-Haenszel OR | Removes bias from confounding variables |

Where, in the standard 2×2 table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& a = \text{Exposed, Outcome present} \\
& b = \text{Exposed, Outcome absent} \\
& c = \text{Unexposed, Outcome present} \\
& d = \text{Unexposed, Outcome absent}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 5. Real-World Utility in Automotive Engineering

- **Euro NCAP & IIHS ratings** rely on observational crash data.
- **Naturalistic Driving Studies (NDS)** such as SHRP2 (USA) and UDRIVE (Europe) are large-scale cohort studies.
- **Human factors research** for cabin comfort uses cross-sectional surveys of occupants.
- **ADAS algorithm training** uses observational driving datasets (e.g., comma2k19, Waymo Open Dataset).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation: Relative Risk (RR) from a 2×2 Table

Consider a cohort study comparing seatbelt users (exposed) and non-users (unexposed) for the outcome "serious injury in crash."

The 2×2 contingency table:

| Group | Injured (Outcome+) | Not Injured (Outcome−) | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Seatbelt Users (Exposed)** | a | b | a + b |
| **Non-Users (Unexposed)** | c | d | c + d |

**Step 1:** Define the incidence (risk) in the exposed group.

$$
I_e = \frac{a}{a + b}
$$

*This is the proportion of seatbelt users who sustained serious injury.*

**Step 2:** Define the incidence (risk) in the unexposed group.

$$
I_u = \frac{c}{c + d}
$$

*This is the proportion of non-users who sustained serious injury.*

**Step 3:** Form the Relative Risk.

$$
RR = \frac{I_e}{I_u} = \frac{\dfrac{a}{a + b}}{\dfrac{c}{c + d}}
$$

$$
RR = \frac{a \cdot (c + d)}{c \cdot (a + b)}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation of RR.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& RR = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{No association between exposure and outcome} \\
& RR > 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Exposure increases risk} \\
& RR < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Exposure is protective}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2. Derivation: Odds Ratio (OR) for Case-Control Studies

In case-control studies, we cannot directly compute incidence because the ratio of cases to controls is fixed by the investigator. We use **odds** instead.

**Step 1:** Odds of exposure among cases.

$$
\text{Odds}_{\text{cases}} = \frac{a}{c}
$$

**Step 2:** Odds of exposure among controls.

$$
\text{Odds}_{\text{controls}} = \frac{b}{d}
$$

**Step 3:** Odds Ratio.

$$
OR = \frac{\text{Odds}_{\text{cases}}}{\text{Odds}_{\text{controls}}} = \frac{a / c}{b / d}
$$

$$
\boxed{OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical Example.

Suppose in a case-control study of fatal crashes:
- a = 40 (cases exposed to drunk driving), c = 160 (cases not exposed)
- b = 10 (controls exposed), d = 190 (controls not exposed)

$$
OR = \frac{40 \times 190}{10 \times 160} = \frac{7600}{1600} = 4.75
$$

**Interpretation:** Drivers involved in fatal crashes had **4.75 times** the odds of having been drunk driving compared to drivers in non-fatal crashes.

## 3. Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Study Design

| Research Question | Best Design | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Does ABS reduce crash risk over time? | **Cohort (Prospective)** | Need to follow exposure forward |
| Was texting the cause of a specific crash? | **Case-Control** | Outcome already known (crash); trace exposure |
| What % of drivers wear seatbelts today? | **Cross-Sectional** | Single-time snapshot needed |
| Does fatigue predict highway accidents? | **Cohort** | Establish temporal sequence |
| Is rear-seat airbag deployment associated with child safety? | **Case-Control** | Rare outcome, efficient design |

## 4. Worked Example: Attributable Risk Calculation

> A study finds that the incidence of whiplash injuries among drivers not using headrests is $I_u = 30$ per 1000 person-years, and among headrest users is $I_e = 10$ per 1000 person-years.

**Step 1:** Compute RR.

$$
RR = \frac{10}{30} = 0.33
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Attributable Risk.

$$
AR = I_u - I_e = 30 - 10 = 20 \text{ per 1000 person-years}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Attributable Risk Percent (AR%).

$$
AR\% = \frac{I_u - I_e}{I_u} \times 100 = \frac{30 - 10}{30} \times 100 = 66.67\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** 66.67% of whiplash injuries in non-headrest users could theoretically be prevented if they all used headrests.

## 5. Confounding — Visual Logic

Suppose we want to study the effect of **night driving (exposure)** on **crash risk (outcome)**. The variable **age** may confound this relationship:

```
Age ──────► Night Driving ──────► Crash Risk
   │                                 ▲
   └─────────────────────────────────┘
   (Younger drivers both drive more at night
    AND have higher crash rates)
```

To control for confounding:
- **Stratification** — analyze separately for each age group.
- **Multivariate regression** — adjust for age statistically.
- **Matching** — pair exposed and unexposed subjects on age.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Cohort Study and a Case-Control Study.**

**Model Answer:**

A Cohort Study is a longitudinal observational design where subjects are grouped based on their exposure status and followed forward in time to observe the development of outcomes. It allows direct calculation of incidence and relative risk. In contrast, a Case-Control Study is retrospective; subjects are selected based on outcome status (cases vs. controls), and their past exposure is traced backwards. Because the case-to-control ratio is fixed by the investigator, incidence cannot be calculated, and the Odds Ratio is used instead. Cohort studies provide stronger evidence of causality but are expensive and time-consuming, whereas case-control studies are efficient for studying rare outcomes such as fatal crashes.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Confounding" in observational studies with an automotive safety example.**

**Model Answer:**

A confounding variable is a third factor that is associated with both the exposure and the outcome, leading to a distorted or spurious association between them. For example, in studying the effect of night driving on crash risk, **age** is a confounder because young drivers both drive more at night (exposure) and have higher crash rates (outcome) due to inexperience. Failure to control for age would falsely exaggerate the apparent risk of night driving. Confounding is controlled through study design (matching, stratification, randomization when feasible) or statistical adjustment (multivariate regression, Mantel-Haenszel methods).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### **(a) Explain the three main types of observational studies with suitable examples from automotive safety engineering. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three principal types of observational studies are:

**1. Cohort Study:**
A cohort study identifies a group of subjects based on their exposure status and follows them over time to assess outcome incidence. There are two subtypes: prospective (follows subjects into the future) and retrospective/historical (uses existing records to look back from a past exposure date).

*Automotive Example:* A cohort of 10,000 taxi drivers in a city is divided into two groups — those who completed an advanced defensive-driving course and those who did not. Both groups are tracked for 2 years. The crash incidence in the trained group is compared with the untrained group to compute the Relative Risk.

**2. Case-Control Study:**
Subjects are first classified by outcome status (cases with the outcome and controls without). Their prior exposure to a risk factor is then determined retrospectively.

*Automotive Example:* 300 drivers who died in road crashes (cases) and 300 drivers matched by age and region who did not (controls) are compared for prior mobile-phone use while driving. The Odds Ratio quantifies the association between phone use and fatality.

**3. Cross-Sectional Study:**
Exposure and outcome data are collected simultaneously at a single point in time, providing a prevalence snapshot.

*Automotive Example:* A roadside survey of 5,000 vehicles records whether seatbelts are worn and whether tyre tread depth is below legal limits. The data provides prevalence estimates but cannot establish temporal sequence or causality.

#### **(b) A cohort study of 5,000 drivers was conducted over 3 years to evaluate the effect of ABS (Anti-lock Braking System) on fatal crash risk. The following data was obtained:**

| Group | Fatal Crashes | No Fatal Crashes | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| ABS Equipped | 30 | 2470 | 2500 |
| ABS Not Equipped | 75 | 2425 | 2500 |

**Calculate the Relative Risk and interpret the result. Also compute the Attributable Risk Percent. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Incidence in exposed (ABS-equipped):

$$
I_e = \frac{30}{2500} = 0.012 = 12 \text{ per 1000 drivers}
$$

**Step 2:** Incidence in unexposed (no ABS):

$$
I_u = \frac{75}{2500} = 0.030 = 30 \text{ per 1000 drivers}
$$

**Step 3:** Relative Risk:

$$
RR = \frac{I_e}{I_u} = \frac{0.012}{0.030} = 0.40
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation:
Drivers with ABS have **0.40 times** (or 60% lower) the risk of fatal crashes compared to those without ABS. Since RR < 1, ABS is **protective**.

**Step 5:** Attributable Risk Percent:

$$
AR\% = \frac{I_u - I_e}{I_u} \times 100 = \frac{0.030 - 0.012}{0.030} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **60%** of fatal crashes among non-ABS drivers could theoretically be prevented if all vehicles were equipped with ABS.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

#### **(a) Discuss the various sources of bias in observational studies. How can each be minimized in the context of automotive safety research? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Biases are systematic errors that distort the true association between exposure and outcome. The major sources are:

**1. Selection Bias:** Occurs when the study sample is not representative of the target population.
*Minimization:* Use random sampling, define clear inclusion/exclusion criteria, and recruit from multiple sites (e.g., urban, rural, highway).

**2. Information (Measurement) Bias:** Arises from inaccurate measurement of exposure or outcome.
*Minimization:* Use objective instruments such as Event Data Recorders (EDRs), accelerometers, and standardised observational protocols rather than self-reports.

**3. Recall Bias:** Particularly affects retrospective studies where subjects misremember past exposure.
*Minimization:* Use objective records (insurance claims, police reports, dashcam footage) instead of patient memory.

**4. Observer Bias:** The researcher's expectations influence data recording.
*Minimization:* Use blinding of observers, automated sensor logging, and double-data entry with reconciliation.

**5. Confounding:** A third variable distorts the exposure–outcome relationship.
*Minimization:* Restrict study design, match subjects on confounders, stratify analysis, or apply multivariate regression / Mantel-Haenszel adjustment.

**6. Loss to Follow-up Bias:** In cohort studies, subjects dropping out non-randomly skew results.
*Minimization:* Implement active follow-up, incentives, and use survival analysis techniques that handle censored data.

#### **(b) A case-control study examined the relationship between not wearing a seatbelt and fatal injuries in head-on collisions. Data obtained:**

| Group | Seatbelt Not Worn | Seatbelt Worn | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fatal Cases** | 90 | 30 | 120 |
| **Non-Fatal Controls** | 50 | 130 | 180 |

**Calculate the Odds Ratio and comment on the strength of association. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Identify cells in the 2×2 table:
- a = 90 (Fatal, Seatbelt not worn)
- b = 30 (Fatal, Seatbelt worn)
- c = 50 (Non-fatal, Seatbelt not worn)
- d = 130 (Non-fatal, Seatbelt worn)

**Step 1:** Apply the Odds Ratio formula:

$$
OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c} = \frac{90 \times 130}{30 \times 50}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator and denominator:

$$
OR = \frac{11700}{1500} = 7.8
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation:
Drivers who sustained fatal injuries had **7.8 times** the odds of not wearing a seatbelt compared to drivers who survived. This is a **strong positive association** between not wearing a seatbelt and fatality in head-on collisions.

**Step 4:** 95% Confidence Interval (using Woolf's approximation):

$$
\ln(OR) = \ln(7.8) \approx 2.054
$$

$$
SE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c} + \frac{1}{d}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{90} + \frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{50} + \frac{1}{130}}
$$

$$
SE = \sqrt{0.0111 + 0.0333 + 0.0200 + 0.0077} = \sqrt{0.0721} \approx 0.269
$$

$$
95\%\, CI = \exp(2.054 \pm 1.96 \times 0.269) = \exp(2.054 \pm 0.527)
$$

$$
95\%\, CI = (\exp(1.527),\ \exp(2.581)) = (4.60,\ 13.21)
$$

Since the entire confidence interval lies above 1, the association is **statistically significant**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse the **placement of cells** in the 2×2 table. Always remember: **rows = outcome status (Case/Control)** and **columns = exposure status (Exposed/Unexposed)**, or vice versa — but be **consistent**.
> - For case-control studies, do **NOT** calculate Relative Risk. Only **Odds Ratio** is valid.
> - Always report the **direction** (RR or OR > 1 vs. < 1) along with magnitude. A single number without interpretation loses 2 marks.
> - Don't forget to mention **confidence intervals** when discussing statistical significance — examiners specifically check this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Observational studies** investigate without manipulating variables; they are essential where experiments are unethical or impractical (e.g., crash research).
- **Three main types:** Cohort (prospective/retrospective), Case-Control, Cross-Sectional.
- **Cohort studies** → use **Relative Risk (RR)**; ideal for temporal sequence but slow and costly.
- **Case-Control studies** → use **Odds Ratio (OR)**; efficient for rare outcomes.
- **Cross-Sectional studies** → provide **Prevalence**; cannot establish causality.
- **Major biases:** Selection, Information, Recall, Observer, Confounding, Loss to Follow-up.
- **Confounding** is controlled via matching, stratification, and multivariate methods.
- **Naturalistic Driving Studies (NDS)** are large-scale cohort studies critical for ADAS development.
- **2×2 table cells** must be correctly identified: a (exposed, outcome+), b (exposed, outcome−), c (unexposed, outcome+), d (unexposed, outcome−).
- **OR ≈ RR** only when the outcome is rare (the "rare disease assumption").
- **AR%** quantifies the proportion of disease preventable by eliminating exposure.
- **Always pair effect sizes (RR/OR) with 95% confidence intervals** in KTU answers for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Interviews
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** User Interviews

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
A **User Interview** is a foundational qualitative research methodology within the domain of Human-Centered Design (HCD) and Human Factors Engineering (HFE), wherein a structured or semi-structured dialogue is conducted between a researcher and a representative end-user to elicit deep, contextual, and experiential insights regarding their needs, behaviors, motivations, pain points, expectations, and unmet requirements with respect to a product, system, or environment.

In the specific context of **Automotive Comfort and Safety Engineering**, user interviews constitute a primary empirical research instrument used during the **conceptual and requirements engineering phase** of vehicle development. They are employed to capture the subjective comfort perceptions (thermal, acoustic, vibrational, ergonomic, postural) and safety-related behaviors (driving habits, perception-reaction characteristics, trust in ADAS, seat-belt usage, child-restraint usage) of diverse user populations before the physical prototyping of cabin systems, restraint systems, HVAC modules, or active safety interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus**
> Within the Automotive Comfort and Safety Engineering curriculum, User Interviews form the bridge between *user-centered ergonomics theory* and the *practical design specification* of vehicle interior systems. They are explicitly listed as a primary **user research tool** preceding usability testing, contextual inquiry, and questionnaire-based surveys in the design-thinking workflow adopted by OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) such as Tata Motors, Mahindra, Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai, and global Tier-1 suppliers like Bosch, Continental, and ZF.

### Key Terminology & Nomenclature

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Stakeholder** | Any individual or group affected by the vehicle system (driver, passenger, fleet operator, regulatory body). |
| **Probe / Probe Question** | An open-ended query designed to elicit narrative responses rather than yes/no answers. |
| **Affinity Diagramming** | The post-interview clustering of qualitative insights into thematic groups. |
| **Saturation** | The point in data collection when no new themes emerge from additional interviews. |
| **Persona** | A synthesized archetypal user profile built from aggregated interview data. |
| **Cognitive Walkthrough** | A related (but distinct) method where evaluators *simulate* user behavior rather than asking users directly. |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Doctor's First Consultation"**
> Imagine a patient walks into a clinic complaining of back pain. Before the doctor orders an MRI, prescribes medication, or recommends surgery, they **first sit down and ask the patient questions**: *When did it start? What makes it worse? What's your daily routine? Have you tried anything?* That conversation — that patient interview — is exactly what a User Interview is in automotive design. The automotive engineer is the "doctor," and the driver (or passenger) is the "patient." Instead of prescribing a seat with 14-way adjustment immediately, the engineer first **listens** to understand the real problem (is it lumbar support during long highway drives? Or ingress-egress difficulty for elderly occupants?). Without the interview, design decisions are based on assumptions; with it, they are grounded in **lived human experience**.

### Physical & Methodological Constants in User Research
- **Recommended session duration**: **45–60 minutes** (industry benchmark; longer causes fatigue and degraded data quality).
- **Recommended sample size for qualitative saturation**: **5–8 users per distinct user segment** (per Nielsen Norman Group, broadly accepted in HFE literature).
- **Minimum distinct user segments in automotive research**: At least **3** (e.g., *urban commuter, highway long-haul driver, senior driver*).
- **Recording fidelity standard**: **48 kHz / 16-bit** minimum audio capture (per ISO/IEC 30113 series usability guidance).

### Position in the Design Research Hierarchy

```
Quantitative Surveys  ←  Numerical, large-N, statistical
Qualitative Interviews ←  Narrative, small-N, thematic   [OUR FOCUS]
Diary Studies          ←  Longitudinal, in-context
Field Observation      ←  Behavioral, naturalistic
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> User Interviews are **qualitative** — they prioritize *depth of understanding* over statistical generalization. They answer the question **"Why?"** and **"How?"** rather than **"How many?"** or **"How often?"**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Typology of User Interviews

User Interviews are not monolithic; they are classified along a spectrum of structure, each suited to different stages of the automotive design lifecycle.

| Interview Type | Structure | Use Stage in Automotive Project | Example Question |
|----------------|-----------|--------------------------------|------------------|
| **Structured** | Rigid script, fixed questions | Validation, regulatory user studies | "On a scale of 1–7, rate cabin noise at 100 km/h." |
| **Semi-structured** | Predefined guide + flexible probing | Concept development | "Tell me about your last long drive. What stood out?" |
| **Unstructured** | Open conversation, no fixed script | Early exploratory research | "Walk me through your relationship with your car." |
| **Contextual Interview** | Conducted in the user's natural environment | Field studies with test vehicles | Interview conducted *inside* the car while driving. |
| **Expert Interview** | Interview with domain specialist (ergonomist, fleet manager) | Technical benchmarking | "How do fleet drivers interact with ADAS alerts?" |

### 2.2 The Six-Stage Interview Process (Automotive-Adapted)

**Stage 1 — Define Research Objectives**
- Translate engineering problem statements into *learning objectives*.
- Example: Engineering problem → "Driver seat discomfort in long-haul trucks."
- Research objective → "Identify the specific discomfort vectors (lumbar, sacral, ischial, thigh) and the contextual triggers (posture, duration, road roughness)."

**Stage 2 — Develop the Interview Guide**
- Construct a funnel structure: **Warm-up → Context → Deep-dive → Wrap-up → Demographic close**.
- Avoid leading questions. Replace *"Don't you think the seat is uncomfortable?"* with *"Describe how your body feels after a 4-hour drive."*

**Stage 3 — Recruit Representative Participants**
- Use a **screening questionnaire** to ensure segment coverage.
- Avoid sampling bias; do not recruit only friends, colleagues, or early adopters.
- In automotive: explicitly recruit across age, gender, body dimensions (stature, weight), driving frequency, vehicle segment, and geographic region.

**Stage 4 — Conduct the Interview**
- **The 80/20 rule**: The participant should speak ~80% of the time; the interviewer ~20%.
- Use the **"5 Whys" technique** to drill from surface symptoms to root causes.
- Apply **active listening**: paraphrase, reflect feelings, observe non-verbal cues (posture shifts, hesitation, sighs).

**Stage 5 — Analyze the Data**
- Transcribe recordings verbatim.
- Apply **thematic coding** (open coding → axial coding → selective coding).
- Use **affinity diagrams** to cluster insights.
- Generate **user personas** and **empathy maps**.

**Stage 6 — Synthesize & Communicate**
- Translate qualitative findings into **design requirements** (functional, non-functional, regulatory).
- Feed into the **House of Quality (QFD)** matrix.
- Validate against ergonomic standards: **SAE J826** (H-point), **SAE J4002** (seat comfort), **ISO 2631** (vibration), **ISO 3381** (acoustic).

### 2.3 Theoretical Foundations

#### 2.3.1 The Double-Diamond Model (Design Council, UK)
The Double-Diamond model structures the design process into four phases: **Discover → Define → Develop → Deliver**. User Interviews sit squarely in the **Discover** phase, providing the empirical foundation for the **Define** phase.

```
       Discover          Define          Develop          Deliver
        \    /            \   /           \   /            \   /
         \  /              \ /             \ /              \ /
          \/                V               V                V
      [INTERVIEWS HERE]  [Personas]    [Prototypes]     [Production]
      divergent          convergent     divergent        convergent
```

#### 2.3.2 The KANO Model (Noriaki Kano, 1984)
User Interviews reveal the three categories of user requirements:
- **Must-be (Basic)**: Seat belt locks, door latches, brake pedal feel.
- **One-dimensional (Performance)**: Headrest adjustability range, HVAC cooling rate.
- **Attractive (Delight)**: Ventilated seats, massage functions, ambient lighting.

> [!NOTE]
> Interviews are particularly powerful for surfacing **Attractive** requirements, which users *cannot articulate* without prompting — they emerge only through open-ended narrative exploration.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — User Interviews

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes key metrics, sampling guidelines, and analytical heuristics essential for KTU viva and written examinations.

| Parameter | Symbol / Formula | Typical Value | Engineering Relevance |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Sample size for saturation (per user segment) | $n_s$ | $n_s = 5 \text{ to } 8$ | Determines interview panel size |
| Recommended session duration | $T_{int}$ | $T_{int} = 45 \text{ to } 60 \text{ min}$ | Avoids participant fatigue |
| Participant speaking ratio | $R_{p}$ | $R_{p} \approx 0.80$ | Quality of qualitative data |
| Inter-rater reliability (Cohen's Kappa) | $\kappa$ | $\kappa \geq 0.70$ | Coding consistency between analysts |
| Thematic saturation coefficient | $S_t$ | $S_t = 1 - \frac{N_{new}}{N_{total}}$ | $S_t \geq 0.95$ indicates saturation |
| Interview-to-survey conversion factor (qual→quant) | $f_{qs}$ | $f_{qs} = 5 \text{ to } 10$ | 5 interviews ≈ 1 survey insight set |
| Interview buffer (recruitment over-recruitment) | $B_r$ | $B_r = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.25$ | Recruit 20–25% extra to cover no-shows |
| Recording audio standard | $f_{audio}$ | $f_{audio} \geq 48 \text{ kHz}$ | ISO-compliant audio capture |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical formatting note**: All absolute-value and conditional bars in the above formulas are denoted with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do **not** use the vertical pipe character `\|` directly inside table cells.

### 2.5 Common Pitfalls in Automotive User Interviews

1. **Recruitment bias** — interviewing only company employees or enthusiasts.
2. **Leading questions** — *"Would you like a massage seat?"* (forces a yes).
3. **Hypothetical bias** — *"Would you use a feature that…"* (people over-predict adoption).
4. **Social desirability bias** — participants telling engineers what they want to hear.
5. **Context stripping** — interviewing people in a sterile lab instead of inside or near the vehicle.
6. **Premature convergence** — stopping at 2–3 interviews and generalizing.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Concept phase**: Validates market need for a new comfort feature (e.g., fatigue-detecting cabin lighting).
- **Requirements phase**: Generates quantified user needs feeding the **QFD matrix**.
- **Benchmarking**: Compares user perception across competitor vehicles (Toyota vs. Honda NVH).
- **Regulatory**: Supports ISO 9241-210 (Human-Centred Design) compliance documentation.
- **Post-launch**: Provides structured feedback for warranty analysis and continuous improvement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Thematic Saturation Curve

Thematic saturation is the point at which conducting additional interviews yields no new themes. We can model it as a logistic decay process.

**Step 1 — Define the cumulative number of unique themes identified after $n$ interviews.**

$$T(n) = T_{max} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\lambda n}\right)$$

where:
- $T(n)$ = cumulative unique themes after $n$ interviews,
- $T_{max}$ = asymptotic maximum number of themes discoverable in the user population,
- $\lambda$ = theme discovery rate constant (themes per interview, decaying),
- $n$ = number of interviews conducted.

**Step 2 — Determine the marginal theme discovery rate.**

Differentiating with respect to $n$:

$$\frac{dT}{dn} = T_{max} \cdot \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda n}$$

This shows that the rate of new theme discovery decays exponentially — the first interview yields the most new themes; subsequent interviews yield diminishing returns.

**Step 3 — Define the saturation coefficient.**

The saturation coefficient $S_t(n)$ is the proportion of *all* themes already captured:

$$S_t(n) = \frac{T(n)}{T_{max}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda n}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$ at the saturation threshold.**

Set $S_t = 0.95$ (the conventionally accepted saturation threshold):

$$1 - e^{-\lambda n} = 0.95$$

$$e^{-\lambda n} = 0.05$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\lambda n = \ln(0.05)$$

$$-\lambda n = -2.9957$$

$$n = \frac{2.9957}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret for a typical automotive study.**

For cabin-noise comfort interviews, assume $\lambda \approx 0.40$ themes-per-interview (industry-typical):

$$n = \frac{2.9957}{0.40} \approx 7.49$$

Rounding up:

$$\boxed{n \approx 8 \text{ interviews}}$$

This confirms the empirical rule-of-thumb of **5–8 interviews per user segment** for saturation.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Participant Speaking Ratio

The participant speaking ratio is a real-time quality metric. If $t_{participant}$ is the total time the participant speaks and $t_{interviewer}$ is the total time the interviewer speaks in a session of duration $T$:

$$T = t_{participant} + t_{interviewer}$$

The participant speaking ratio is:

$$R_p = \frac{t_{participant}}{T} = \frac{t_{participant}}{t_{participant} + t_{interviewer}}$$

For the target $R_p \geq 0.80$:

$$\frac{t_{participant}}{t_{participant} + t_{interviewer}} \geq 0.80$$

$$t_{participant} \geq 0.80 \, t_{participant} + 0.80 \, t_{interviewer}$$

$$0.20 \, t_{participant} \geq 0.80 \, t_{interviewer}$$

$$t_{participant} \geq 4 \, t_{interviewer}$$

> **Logical interpretation**: For a high-quality qualitative interview, the participant must speak at least **four times as long** as the interviewer. This justifies the design of interview guides with **open-ended probes** rather than closed-ended interrogatives.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Required Recruitment Buffer

Let $N_{target}$ be the target number of completed interviews. Let $p_{no-show}$ be the historical no-show probability (typically 15–25% for automotive field studies):

$$N_{recruited} = \frac{N_{target}}{1 - p_{no-show}}$$

The recruitment buffer is:

$$B_r = N_{recruited} - N_{target} = N_{target} \left(\frac{1}{1 - p_{no-show}} - 1\right)$$

$$B_r = N_{target} \cdot \frac{p_{no-show}}{1 - p_{no-show}}$$

**Numerical example**: For $N_{target} = 8$ and $p_{no-show} = 0.20$:

$$N_{recruited} = \frac{8}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{8}{0.80} = 10$$

$$B_r = 10 - 8 = 2 \text{ participants (25\% buffer)}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Interview Type

| Project Stage | Information Need | Recommended Interview Type | Sample Size per Segment | Duration |
|---------------|-----------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| Concept ideation | "What problems exist?" | Unstructured / Contextual | 6–8 | 60 min |
| Requirements definition | "What features matter and why?" | Semi-structured | 6–8 | 45–60 min |
| Concept validation | "Which of two concepts is preferred?" | Structured + Likert probes | 8–10 | 30–45 min |
| Pre-launch refinement | "Are there any blockers?" | Contextual (in-vehicle) | 5–7 | 60 min |
| Post-launch feedback | "What issues have you faced?" | Semi-structured (recall) | 10–15 | 30 min |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Model Question Pattern)**:
> An automotive OEM is planning user interviews to study *driver fatigue perception during night-time highway driving* in their upcoming SUV. The engineering team needs to determine:
> (a) How many participants to recruit if the historical no-show rate is 18% and the target saturation sample is 7 interviews per segment?
> (b) If the average interview yields 3 unique themes in the first session and the discovery rate constant is $\lambda = 0.35$, verify whether $n = 7$ is sufficient for 95% saturation.
> (c) If the participant speaks for 38 minutes and the interviewer speaks for 12 minutes, evaluate the participant speaking ratio.

#### Solution

**(a) Recruitment Calculation**

$$N_{recruited} = \frac{N_{target}}{1 - p_{no-show}} = \frac{7}{1 - 0.18} = \frac{7}{0.82}$$

$$N_{recruited} = 8.54 \approx 9 \text{ participants}$$

**(b) Saturation Verification**

Using the saturation coefficient formula:

$$S_t(7) = 1 - e^{-0.35 \times 7} = 1 - e^{-2.45}$$

Computing the exponential:

$$e^{-2.45} \approx 0.0863$$

Therefore:

$$S_t(7) = 1 - 0.0863 = 0.9137 \approx 91.4\%$$
