# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Roll Over Testing as applied to heavy commercial vehicles. Name the international regulation that governs it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Roll Over Testing is a quasi-static structural safety evaluation in which a complete vehicle (or a representative body section) is placed on a hydraulic tilt-table platform and rotated laterally at a rate not exceeding 5°/s through 0° → 90° → 180° → 270°. The purpose is to verify that the **superstructure of the vehicle maintains a defined "Survival Space" (Residual Space)** — a virtual rectangular envelope representing the position of the seated occupants — throughout the complete rollover. The regulation governing this test is **ECE Regulation No. 66 (UN R66)** — *"Uniform technical prescriptions concerning the approval of large passenger vehicles with regard to the strength of their superstructure."* In India, this regulation is adopted as **AIS-052** under the Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR).

> *Marking split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for procedure detail, 1 mark for regulation name.*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is Compliance Testing? List any four categories of compliance tests applicable to a passenger car.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Compliance Testing is the formal, legally-mandated process of verifying that a vehicle, sub-system, or component conforms to all applicable statutory safety and emission regulations before it can be type-approved and sold in a given market. It is conducted by accredited independent technical services (e.g., ARAI, TÜV) under the supervision of national regulatory authorities.

Four categories of compliance tests for a passenger car:

1. **Passive Safety Tests** — Frontal impact (UN R94), Side impact (UN R95), Pedestrian protection (UN R127).
2. **Active Safety Tests** — Braking performance (UN R13-H), Electronic stability control (UN R13-H Annex 9), ABS.
3. **Lighting and Visibility Tests** — Headlamp photometric performance (UN R112), Rear lamp installation (UN R48).
4. **Environmental/Emission Tests** — Tailpipe emission (Euro 6 / BS-VI), On-board diagnostics (UN R83), EVAP emissions (UN R83).

> *Marking split: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four test categories (0.5 each).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ECE R66 Quasi-Static Tilt-Table Test** procedure for a double-deck bus. Include a labelled schematic of the test setup, the test sequence, the criteria for pass/fail, and the energy absorption requirement equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-deck inter-city bus has a gross vehicle weight of **14,000 kg**, a half-track width $T = 0.95$ m, and its center of gravity is located at a height $h_{cg} = 1.55$ m above the ground.
   - (i) Calculate the **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** and comment on the rollover risk. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) Calculate the **total gravitational potential energy released** in a complete 180° rollover. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) Calculate the **minimum energy** the bus superstructure must absorb per ECE R66. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (a) — 7 marks

The **ECE R66 Quasi-Static Tilt-Table Test** is performed as follows:

1. **Test Article Preparation** (1 mark):
   The bus is loaded to its **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)**, including a representative distribution of passengers (mannequins or ballast) and luggage. All fuel tanks are filled to 90% capacity with a fluid of density similar to fuel. The **survival space** is marked using a physical frame (or measured virtually with photogrammetry).

2. **Platform Setup** (1 mark):
   The bus is driven onto a **hydraulic tilt platform** (tilting bed) capable of rotating about a longitudinal axis parallel to the vehicle's longitudinal axis. The platform surface is **lubricated to achieve a coefficient of friction of 0.5 ± 0.1** so that friction does not artificially prevent the rollover. The bus is anchored at the wheels to prevent rolling off prematurely.

3. **Instrumentation** (1 mark):
   The test is instrumented with:
   - High-speed cameras (minimum 250 fps) at all four sides and overhead,
   - Strain gauges on A, B, C, D pillars and roof rails,
   - Load cells at floor/wall anchorage points,
   - Inclinometers to record tilt angle in real time.

4. **Test Sequence** (2 marks):
   The platform is rotated at a **quasi-static rate of 5°/s** (or slower) through the following sequence:
   - **0° to 90°** — Bus tilts onto its **first roof rail edge**.
   - **90° to 180°** — Bus lies **inverted** on its roof.
   - **180° to 270°** — Bus continues to roll onto its **opposite side roof edge**.
   - At each key angle (0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150°, 180°, 210°, 240°, 270°), the rotation is **paused for ≥ 5 seconds** to allow inspection and photography.

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria** (1 mark):
   **PASS** if, at every stage, the survival space remains **unintrusive** — no part of the superstructure (pillars, roof rails, window frames, glass fragments) penetrates the residual space envelope. **FAIL** if any intrusion occurs; the test is halted and a redesign is mandated.

6. **Energy Requirement** (1 mark):
   Per ECE R66 §5.4, the superstructure must absorb at least:
   $$E_{min} = 0.475 \cdot M \cdot g \cdot h_{cg}$$

**Schematic (text-based):**
```
Side View of Tilt Platform:

  0°           90°              180°             270°
   |             |                 |                 |
   ▼ Bus         ▼ Bus             ▼ Bus             ▼ Bus
  ┌────┐       ┌────┐            ┌────┐            ┌────┐
  │BUS │  →    │BUS │     →      │BUS │     →      │BUS │
  │    │       │    │            │    │            │    │
  └────┘       └─┘  │            │ ┌─┘            └─┐  │
  ══════       ══════            ══════            ══════
  Platform     Platform          Platform          Platform
  (Upright)    (Tilted 90°)      (Inverted 180°)   (Tilted 270°)
```

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $M = 14{,}000 \; \text{kg}$
- $T = 0.95 \; \text{m}$
- $h_{cg} = 1.55 \; \text{m}$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**(i) Static Stability Factor (3 marks):**

$$
SSF = \frac{T}{2 \cdot h_{cg}} = \frac{0.95}{2 \times 1.55} = \frac{0.95}{3.10} = 0.3065
$$

> **Comment:** An SSF of **0.31 is significantly below the NHTSA safety threshold of 0.40**. This indicates a **high rollover risk**, typical of tall, narrow heavy vehicles. The bus is therefore **mandatorily subject to ECE R66** to ensure superstructure integrity.

**(ii) Total GPE released in 180° rollover (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta PE = 2 \cdot M \cdot g \cdot h_{cg}
$$

$$
= 2 \times 14{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 1.55
$$

$$
= 425{,}694 \; \text{J} \approx 425.7 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**(iii) Minimum absorbed energy per ECE R66 (2 marks):**

$$
E_{min} = 0.475 \cdot M \cdot g \cdot h_{cg}
$$

$$
= 0.475 \times 14{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 1.55
$$

$$
= 101{,}102 \; \text{J} \approx 101.1 \; \text{kJ}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Although 425.7 kJ of total energy is released, the regulation requires only ~101 kJ to be absorbed by the structure. The remaining 324 kJ (~76%) is dissipated through friction at the platform, internal damping, and rebound.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **complete compliance testing workflow** for a new passenger car model intended for the Indian market. Cover: (i) regulatory framework, (ii) the role of ARAI as the technical service, (iii) the list of applicable AIS/UN regulations, and (iv) the Conformity of Production (COP) requirement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **ECE R66 energy-based pass criterion**, derive the expression for the minimum energy that the superstructure of a coach must absorb, clearly stating the meaning of each term. Calculate the value for a coach of mass 18,000 kg with $h_{cg} = 1.7$ m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (a) — 7 marks

**1. Regulatory Framework for India (2 marks):**

India follows a **dual regulatory regime**:
- **Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR), 1989** — notified under the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.
- **Automotive Industry Standards (AIS)** — developed by the **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)** in consultation with the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)** and adopted into CMVR via gazette notifications. AIS standards are **harmonized with UN regulations** to enable global trade.

**2. Role of ARAI as the Technical Service (1.5 marks):**

ARAI is the **lead Designated Technical Service** under CMVR Rule 126. Its responsibilities include:
- Conducting type approval tests in accredited labs (e.g., ARAI Pune, iCAT Manesar, GARC Chennai),
- Issuing test reports for each applicable regulation,
- Verifying design drawings, FEM reports, BOM, and material certificates,
- Conducting periodic **COP audits** at the manufacturer's plant.

**3. Applicable AIS/UN Regulations (1.5 marks):**

For a typical passenger car, the following are applicable:

| Area | Regulation |
|---|---|
| Frontal impact | AIS-098 (UN R94) |
| Side impact | AIS-099 (UN R95) |
| Pedestrian protection | AIS-100 (UN R127) |
| Seat belts | AIS-005 (UN R16) |
| Brake systems | AIS-018 (UN R13-H) |
| Lighting | AIS-008/010 (UN R48, R112) |
| EMI/EMC | AIS-004 (UN R10) |
| Emission | AIS-137 (Bharat Stage VI) |
| Whole vehicle approval | AIS-053 (UN R0/whole vehicle type approval) |
| Electric vehicle safety | AIS-156 (UN R100) |

**4. Conformity of Production (COP) (2 marks):**

Once type approval is granted, the manufacturer must:
- Maintain **production consistency** with the approved type.
- Submit to **annual COP audits** by ARAI — random production vehicles are pulled from the line and re-tested on a sub-set of parameters.
- Maintain a **quality management system** (typically **IATF 16949** or ISO 9001) and a **traceability system** for every component.
- Failure of COP leads to **suspension or withdrawal** of type approval.

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a coach of mass $M$ with its center of gravity at perpendicular height $h_{cg}$ above the rollover axis (the tire contact line). When the coach is rotated from the upright position to the fully inverted position (a 180° rotation), the CG descends a vertical distance of exactly $2 h_{cg}$. The gravitational potential energy released in this process is:

$$
\Delta PE = M \cdot g \cdot (2 h_{cg}) = 2 M g h_{cg}
$$

However, not all of this energy must be absorbed by the superstructure. ECE R66 §5.4 specifies that the superstructure must absorb at least a **regulatory fraction** of the released energy, given by the empirical constant $k = 0.475$. Therefore:

$$
E_{min} = k \cdot M \cdot g \cdot h_{cg} = 0.475 \, M g h_{cg}
$$

**Meaning of terms:**
- $E_{min}$ — Minimum energy (in joules) the superstructure must absorb during a complete rollover.
- $M$ — Gross vehicle mass in kg (fully laden).
- $g$ — Gravitational acceleration = 9.81 m/s².
- $h_{cg}$ — Perpendicular height of the center of gravity above the rollover axis, in meters.
- $0.475$ — Dimensionless regulatory safety constant (≈ 47.5% of total GPE).

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given $M = 18{,}000 \; \text{kg}$, $h_{cg} = 1.7 \; \text{m}$:

$$
E_{min} = 0.475 \times 18{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 1.7
$$

$$
= 0.475 \times 18{,}000 \times 16.677
$$

$$
= 0.475 \times 300{,}186
$$

$$
= 142{,}588.35 \; \text{J} \approx 142.6 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Result:** The coach superstructure must be capable of absorbing **at least 142.6 kJ** of energy during a full rollover, without intrusion into the survival space.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $\Delta PE$** — Many students compute $M g h_{cg}$ instead of $2 M g h_{cg}$. This loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing SSF formula** — Some students use $SSF = T / h_{cg}$ (forgetting the factor 2). Correct is $T / (2 h_{cg})$.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — Tilt rate is in **degrees/second**, not radians/second. GPE must be in **joules**, not kJ directly.
> 4. **Naming the wrong regulation** — Roll-over for buses is **ECE R66**, not R29 (R29 is for commercial vehicle cabs, not for buses). R14 is for **seat belt anchorages**, not for rollover.
> 5. **Omitting the survival space concept** — The entire purpose of the test is to protect the survival space. Answers that only describe the tilting procedure **without mentioning the survival space** lose up to **2 marks**.
> 6. **Confusing Type Approval with COP** — Type Approval is **once-off** for a new model; COP is **continuous** for production. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Roll Over Testing** evaluates the **structural integrity of the superstructure** during a complete lateral rollover, using a **quasi-static tilt table** rotating at **≤ 5°/s**.
- [ ] The primary international regulation is **ECE R66** (UN R66); in India, this is adopted as **AIS-052**.
- [ ] The test must be conducted with the vehicle at **GVW**, on a platform with **friction coefficient 0.5 ± 0.1**.
- [ ] The **Survival Space (Residual Space)** is a virtual rectangular envelope representing the seated occupant. **No structural intrusion** is permitted at any angle.
- [ ] Total GPE released in 180° rollover: $\Delta PE = 2 M g h_{cg}$.
- [ ] Minimum absorbed energy per ECE R66: $E_{min} = 0.475 M g h_{cg}$.
- [ ] **Static Stability Factor**: $SSF = T / (2 h_{cg})$. Buses typically have SSF ≈ 0.30–0.40, which is **below the safe threshold of 0.40**.
- [ ] **Compliance Testing** is the **legally mandated** process of verifying that a vehicle meets all applicable safety and emission regulations.
- [ ] Compliance testing follows a **four-phase workflow**: Design Review → Type Approval Test → Type Approval Issuance → Conformity of Production (COP).
- [ ] The **Technical Service** (e.g., ARAI, TÜV, UTAC) conducts tests independently of the OEM.
- [ ] **Conformity of Production (COP)** is **continuous**, not one-time. It involves annual audits of production vehicles.
- [ ] Failure of COP leads to **suspension or withdrawal** of type approval.
- [ ] Compliance testing covers both **passive safety** (crash tests) and **active safety** (brakes, ESC, lighting) plus **emissions**.
- [ ] The **rollover threshold lateral acceleration** is $a_y = g \cdot T / h_{cg}$.
- [ ] Indian technical services include **ARAI** (Pune), **iCAT** (Manesar), **GARC** (Chennai), and **NATRIP** (Natrax Indore).
- [ ] Always remember: **Tilt rate = 5°/s**, **μ = 0.5 ± 0.1**, **k = 0.475**, **SSF safe threshold = 0.40** — these are the four numerical "magic numbers" examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Component Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — COMPONENT TESTING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Component Testing** in automotive engineering refers to the systematic, controlled, and standardized evaluation of individual vehicle subsystems or parts (e.g., brakes, airbags, HVAC units, seats, suspension, steering column) to verify their **functional performance, durability, safety compliance, and reliability** against design specifications, regulatory standards, and homologation requirements.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Component Testing is broadly classified into two macro-domains:

- **Safety Component Testing** — validation of restraint systems, braking systems, steering systems, passive structures, and electronic safety subsystems (ABS, ESC, ADAS sensors).
- **Comfort Component Testing** — validation of HVAC systems, seats, NVH components, ride-quality dampers, infotainment ergonomics, and interior trim.

The testing spectrum includes **destructive tests, non-destructive tests (NDT), functional/performance tests, environmental tests, fatigue/durability tests, and crash/impact tests** — each governed by standards from **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards, India), ECE (UN Economic Commission for Europe), ISO, SAE, and BIS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module expects students to describe testing methods for *brake systems, steering, suspension, seats, HVAC, airbags, and seatbelts* along with the **testing equipment** used (dynamometers, sled test rigs, shaker tables, environmental chambers).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Hospital"

Imagine a newly manufactured car is a *human body* about to enter the world. Before being allowed to operate, it must undergo a **full medical check-up**, organ-by-organ. Component Testing is exactly that:

- **Heart Test** $\rightarrow$ Brake dynamometer (stops the vehicle repeatedly to check stamina).
- **Reflex Test** $\rightarrow$ Airbag deployment test (reaction speed in milliseconds).
- **Bone Density Test** $\rightarrow$ Crumple zone compression test (energy absorption capability).
- **Lung Function Test** $\rightarrow$ HVAC performance test (cooling/heating capacity, airflow).
- **Ear, Nose, Throat Test** $\rightarrow$ NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) test (acoustic comfort).
- **Endurance Test** $\rightarrow$ Fatigue/lifecycle test (millions of cycles on shaker tables).
- **Allergy Test** $\rightarrow$ Environmental chamber (extreme temperature, humidity, dust, salt).

Just as a doctor uses calibrated instruments (stethoscope, MRI, blood test) and a **standard protocol** (WHO or medical council norms), the automotive engineer uses a **dynamometer, accelerometer, data logger, and homologation standards** (AIS, ECE R13, ISO 26262) to certify each component as "fit for road."

### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchor Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms Every KTU Student Must Know:**
> - **Homologation** — Formal regulatory approval of a vehicle/component for sale/road use.
> - **DUT (Device Under Test)** — The component currently being evaluated.
> - **HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop)** — Testing of embedded control units with simulated real-world inputs.
> - **Sled Test** — A crash simulation rig where a sled (carriage) is propelled into a rigid wall or decelerator to measure occupant kinematics.
> - **Accelerometer** — Sensor that measures proper acceleration (g-force); output in m/s² or g.
> - **Dummy (ATD — Anthropomorphic Test Device)** — Instrumented crash mannequin (e.g., Hybrid III, THOR) used in crash tests.
> - **Anechoic Chamber** — Room with sound-absorbing walls for NVH acoustic testing.
> - **DUT Life Cycle** — Total number of operational cycles before failure (e.g., 1,000,000 km equivalent for a brake pad).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- Standard gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$**
- Standard deceleration target (frontal crash): **$\leq 20\ g$** for occupant survivability (AIS / ECE R94).
- Brake fluid boiling point (DOT 4): **$\geq 230\ ^\circ\text{C}$** (wet), $\geq 155\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (dry).
- Airbag deployment time: **$20$–$30\ \text{ms}$** from crash trigger.
- Seatbelt pretensioner activation: **$\approx 10\ \text{ms}$** post-collision.
- HVAC cabin test ambient: **$+40\ ^\circ\text{C}$** (summer) and **$-10\ ^\circ\text{C}$** (winter) as per AIS.
- NVH cabin noise target: **$\leq 70\ \text{dB(A)}$** at cruise; **$\leq 55\ \text{dB(A)}$** at idle for premium sedans.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Component Testing

Component testing is a multi-layered discipline. The hierarchy is as follows:

### 2.1.1 By Destructiveness
1. **Destructive Testing (DT)**
   - Specimen is permanently damaged or destroyed.
   - Examples: Crash test, tensile test, fatigue-to-failure, drop test, salt-spray corrosion (end-point).
   - Used to determine ultimate strength, energy absorption, and mode of failure.

2. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**
   - Specimen remains serviceable after inspection.
   - Examples: Ultrasonic testing, X-ray radiography, dye-penetrant inspection, magnetic particle inspection, eddy current testing.
   - Used for crack detection, weld integrity, and quality screening.

### 2.1.2 By Test Objective
- **Functional/Performance Test** — verifies the component performs its intended function under nominal conditions (e.g., brake pedal force vs. deceleration).
- **Endurance/Durability Test** — checks long-term wear/fatigue (e.g., 100,000 km bench simulation).
- **Environmental Test** — exposure to temperature, humidity, dust, salt, UV (e.g., $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $+85\ ^\circ\text{C}$ cycling).
- **Crash/Impact Test** — simulates real-world collisions (frontal, side, rear, rollover).
- **EMC (Electromagnetic Compatibility) Test** — verifies electronics are immune to EMI per CISPR 25 / ISO 11452.
- **Vibration/Shaker Test** — random/sine vibration on electrodynamic shaker (per ISO 16750-3).
- **NVH Test** — acoustic and vibration comfort assessment.

### 2.1.3 By Component Type — KTU 2024 Specific
| Component | Primary Test Type | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Brake System | Dynamometer, stopping distance, fade test | AIS-069, ECE R13, ISO 26867 |
| Steering System | Rack load, torque vs. angle, durability | AIS-007, ISO 4151 |
| Suspension | K&C rig, damper dyno, fatigue | ISO 8606, SAE J670 |
| Airbag | Sled test, deployment timing, inflator | ECE R94, AIS-098, FMVSS 208 |
| Seatbelt | Webbing tensile, retractor lock, pretensioner | AIS-006, ECE R16 |
| HVAC | Psychrometric chamber, airflow, COP | AIS-069, ISO 8573 |
| Seat | Whiplash (rear impact), durability, H-point | ECE R17, AIS-008, FMVSS 207 |
| Crumple Zone | Crush test, energy absorption | ECE R94, FMVSS 301 |
| Wheel/Tire | Cornering stiffness, endurance, burst | AIS-044, ECE R30 |

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation — Crash Kinematics and Energy Absorption

During a frontal crash, the **kinetic energy** of the vehicle must be dissipated in a controlled manner over a short stopping distance and time. The energy balance is:

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = E_{\text{crush}} + E_{\text{occupant}} + E_{\text{restitution}}$$

Where $E_{\text{crush}}$ is the energy absorbed by the crumple zone, $E_{\text{occupant}}$ is energy managed by restraints, and $E_{\text{restitution}}$ is the small elastic rebound energy.

For a rigid body of mass $m$ decelerating from velocity $v$ to rest over distance $d$:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = F_{\text{avg}} \cdot d$$

The average crash force is therefore:

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{m v^2}{2d}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** for sizing crumple zone length and predicting occupant loading.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** — these equations are routinely tested in KTU ESE for both Part A (3 marks) derivations and Part B (14 marks) numericals.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | $m$ = kg, $v$ = m/s, $E_k$ = J | Base crash energy equation |
| 2 | Avg. crash force | $F_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{m v^2}{2 d}$ | $F$ = N, $d$ = crush distance (m) | Lower $d$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $F$ (worse) |
| 3 | Deceleration (avg) | $a = \dfrac{v^2}{2d} = \dfrac{F_{\text{avg}}}{m}$ | $a$ = m/s²; in $g$: $a/g$ | Survivability: $a \leq 20g$ |
| 4 | Occupant kinetic energy | $E_o = \tfrac{1}{2} m_o v_r^2$ | $m_o$ = occupant mass, $v_r$ = relative velocity | $v_r$ = closing speed |
| 5 | Energy absorbed (cushion) | $E_{\text{abs}} = \int F \, dx$ | Integrate force-displacement curve | Trapezoidal rule: $E \approx \sum F_i \Delta x_i$ |
| 6 | Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) | $\text{SEA} = \dfrac{E_{\text{abs}}}{m_{\text{structure}}}$ | J/kg | Material/process benchmark; foam: $5$–$15$ kJ/kg; steel: $20$–$40$ kJ/kg |
| 7 | Brake stopping distance | $d = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | $\mu$ = tire-road friction, $g = 9.81$ | Assumes pure rolling, no skid |
| 8 | Brake torque | $T = F_{\text{caliper}} \cdot r_{\text{eff}} \cdot n$ | $n$ = number of pistons, $r$ = effective radius (m) | Disc brake dominant |
| 9 | Brake fade (energy) | $E_{\text{brake}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \cdot n_{\text{stops}}$ | $n_{\text{stops}}$ = number of stops | Per fade test (e.g., 10 consecutive stops) |
| 10 | Heat dissipation | $Q = m c \Delta T$ | $c$ = specific heat (J/kg·K) | Disc $c \approx 500$ J/kg·K |
| 11 | Fatigue life (Miner) | $D = \sum \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}$ | Failure when $D \geq 1$ | Cumulative damage rule |
| 12 | S-N (Basquin) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2 N)^b$ | $\sigma_f'$ fatigue strength coeff., $b$ = strength exponent | High-cycle fatigue |
| 13 | Airbag deployment impulse | $I = \int F(t)\,dt = m_o \Delta v$ | $I$ = N·s | Impulse-momentum theorem |
| 14 | Pretensioner force | $F_p = m_o \cdot a_{\text{peak}}$ | $F_p$ = N | Tethers torso at peak $g$ |
| 15 | HVAC cooling capacity | $Q_{\text{c}} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $\dot{m}$ = air mass flow (kg/s) | $\Delta T = T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}$ |
| 16 | NVH SPL | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Reference sound pressure |
| 17 | Vibration transmissibility | $T_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | $r = \omega/\omega_n$, $\zeta$ = damping ratio | Isolation when $r > \sqrt{2}$ |
| 18 | Natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = stiffness (N/m) | Suspension/component mount |
| 19 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | $c$ = damping coefficient | $\zeta = 0.7$ for car suspension |
| 20 | Brake hydraulic pressure | $P = \dfrac{F_{\text{pedal}} \cdot A_{\text{master}}}{A_{\text{caliper}} \cdot \eta}$ | $\eta$ = system efficiency ($\approx 0.85$) | Pascal's law in brake circuit |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crash Testing** saves lives — direct contribution to UN/ECE R94, R95, R16, R17 regulations, reducing fatalities by **40%+** in modern cars vs. 1980s equivalents.
- **Component Testing** enables manufacturers to detect design flaws *before* mass production, saving millions in recall costs (a single airbag recall can cost \$100M+).
- **NDT** allows in-service inspection of safety-critical parts (axles, subframes) without disassembly, enabling predictive maintenance.
- **HIL testing** of ECUs (e.g., ABS, ESC) replaces thousands of km of road tests, compressing development time from years to months.
- **NVH testing** is now a *brand differentiator* — luxury buyers select cars based on cabin quietness (target: < 60 dB(A) at 120 km/h).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (2024 Scheme):** Numerical problems frequently appear in ESE (End Semester Examination) for this module. The derivations below are the most-likely 7-or-14-mark question frameworks. **Memorize the variable definitions, not just the final equation.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Deceleration and Survival Limit in Frontal Crash

### Problem Statement
A vehicle of mass $m = 1500\ \text{kg}$ collides head-on with a rigid barrier at $v = 50\ \text{km/h}$. The crumple zone crushes uniformly over a distance $d = 0.5\ \text{m}$. Compute the average deceleration, the average force on the vehicle, and check survivability against the $20g$ limit.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert velocity to SI units.**

$$v = 50\ \text{km/h} = 50 \times \frac{1000}{3600}\ \text{m/s} = 13.89\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Apply the work–energy principle.** The kinetic energy is dissipated over the crush distance $d$.

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = F_{\text{avg}} \cdot d$$

**Step 3: Solve for average force.**

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{m v^2}{2 d} = \frac{1500 \times (13.89)^2}{2 \times 0.5}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerically.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1500 \times 192.9}{1.0} \\
&= \frac{289{,}350}{1.0} \\
&= 289{,}350\ \text{N} \\
&\approx 289.4\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute average deceleration.**

$$\begin{aligned}
a_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{F_{\text{avg}}}{m} = \frac{289{,}350}{1500} \\
&= 192.9\ \text{m/s}^2 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to $g$ units:

$$a_{\text{avg}} = \frac{192.9}{9.81} = 19.66\ g$$

**Step 6: Survivability check.** Since $19.66\ g < 20\ g$, the occupant experiences a survivable deceleration (just at the limit). A shorter crush distance would be fatal — this is *why* crumple zones are engineered.

### Result Summary
- $F_{\text{avg}} \approx 289.4\ \text{kN}$
- $a_{\text{avg}} \approx 19.66\ g$ (at the survival threshold)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) of a Crumple Tube

### Problem Statement
A steel circular tube of mass $m_t = 2.5\ \text{kg}$, length $L = 0.4\ \text{m}$, and mean radius $R = 50\ \text{mm}$ is axially crushed in a quasi-static test. The mean crush force is $F_m = 80\ \text{kN}$ over a stroke $\delta = 0.25\ \text{m}$. Compute the **Specific Energy Absorption (SEA)** and compare with target values.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute total energy absorbed.** Energy absorbed equals the area under the force–displacement curve. For constant mean force:

$$E_{\text{abs}} = F_m \cdot \delta$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{abs}} &= 80{,}000 \times 0.25 \\
&= 20{,}000\ \text{J} \\
&= 20\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute SEA.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SEA} &= \frac{E_{\text{abs}}}{m_t} \\
&= \frac{20{,}000}{2.5} \\
&= 8000\ \text{J/kg} = 8\ \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compare with benchmarks.**
- Aluminum foam: $5$–$15\ \text{kJ/kg}$ ✓ (within range)
- Mild steel tube: $20$–$40\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (this SEA is low — material is suboptimal for this design)

### Engineering Interpretation
The SEA is a *figure of merit*: it tells the designer how much energy is absorbed **per unit mass** of the structure. Higher SEA = lighter, more efficient crumple zone.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brake Stopping Distance Under Skid Condition

### Problem Statement
A car traveling at $v = 90\ \text{km/h}$ on a wet road ($\mu = 0.6$) applies maximum braking. Find the stopping distance, the time to stop, and the brake energy dissipated. Take $m = 1200\ \text{kg}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert velocity.**

$$v = 90 \times \frac{5}{18} = 25\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute deceleration.** Maximum braking deceleration equals tire friction times gravity.

$$a = \mu \, g = 0.6 \times 9.81 = 5.886\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Stopping distance (using $v^2 = u^2 - 2 a d$ with $v = 0$).**

$$d = \frac{u^2}{2 a} = \frac{(25)^2}{2 \times 5.886} = \frac{625}{11.772} = 53.09\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Time to stop (using $v = u - a t$ with $v = 0$).**

$$t = \frac{u}{a} = \frac{25}{5.886} = 4.247\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5: Kinetic energy dissipated as heat in the brakes.**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m u^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1200 \times (25)^2 = 375{,}000\ \text{J} = 375\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 6: Per-disc heat capacity check.** If a single disc has mass $m_d = 8\ \text{kg}$, $c = 500\ \text{J/kg·K}$:

$$\Delta T = \frac{E_k}{m_d \cdot c} = \frac{375{,}000}{8 \times 500} = 93.75\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

A single emergency stop raises the disc by **~94 °C** — within DOT fluid limits but with no margin for repetition (fade test triggers 10 stops in succession).

### Result Summary
- Stopping distance: $\approx 53.1\ \text{m}$
- Stop time: $\approx 4.25\ \text{s}$
- Energy dissipated: $375\ \text{kJ}$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Miner's Rule Cumulative Fatigue Damage

### Problem Statement
A suspension coil spring is subjected to the following duty cycle:
- 50,000 cycles at stress amplitude $\sigma_1 = 400\ \text{MPa}$ (life $N_1 = 200{,}000$)
- 20,000 cycles at $\sigma_2 = 500\ \text{MPa}$ (life $N_2 = 80{,}000$)
- 10,000 cycles at $\sigma_3 = 600\ \text{MPa}$ (life $N_3 = 30{,}000$)

Use **Miner's Rule** to predict failure.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall Miner's Rule.**

$$D = \sum_{i} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

Failure is predicted when $D \geq 1$.

**Step 2: Compute individual damage fractions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \frac{n_1}{N_1} = \frac{50{,}000}{200{,}000} = 0.250 \\
D_2 &= \frac{n_2}{N_2} = \frac{20{,}000}{80{,}000} = 0.250 \\
D_3 &= \frac{n_3}{N_3} = \frac{10{,}000}{30{,}000} = 0.333
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Sum the cumulative damage.**

$$D = 0.250 + 0.250 + 0.333 = 0.833$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.** Since $D = 0.833 < 1$, the spring has consumed **83.3%** of its fatigue life. The component is **safe** for one duty cycle but will fail after approximately $\frac{1}{0.833} \approx 1.2$ repetitions of this exact cycle block.

### Result Summary
- Cumulative damage: $D = 0.833$
- Status: **Safe** with 16.7% life margin remaining.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Airbag Deployment Impulse

### Problem Statement
A $70\ \text{kg}$ occupant moves forward at $10\ \text{m/s}$ relative to the vehicle (closing velocity) during a crash. The airbag deploys and brings the occupant to rest over $t = 50\ \text{ms}$. Calculate:
1. The impulse required.
2. The average force on the occupant.
3. The peak chest deceleration in $g$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute impulse from momentum change.**

$$I = m_o \Delta v = 70 \times 10 = 700\ \text{N·s}$$

**Step 2: Compute average force over deployment time.**

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I}{t} = \frac{700}{0.050} = 14{,}000\ \text{N} = 14\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 3: Compute deceleration.**

$$a = \frac{F_{\text{avg}}}{m_o} = \frac{14{,}000}{70} = 200\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 4: Convert to $g$.**

$$a = \frac{200}{9.81} \approx 20.4\ g$$

### Engineering Interpretation
The deceleration is at the upper limit of human tolerance for chest loading (AIS-098 specifies $\leq 60\ g$ for brief, but lower for sustained). The calculation confirms the criticality of **fast airbag deployment** — increasing $t$ from 50 ms to 100 ms would halve the force, but increase the risk of the occupant "outrunning" the cushion and striking the steering wheel.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Test Method vs. Failure Mode

| Test Method | Detected Failure Mode | Typical Equipment | KTU-Mapped Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile test | Yield, ultimate strength, ductility | Universal Testing Machine (UTM) | ASTM E8 / ISO 6892 |
| Fatigue test | Crack initiation, S-N curve | Rotating beam / axial fatigue rig | ISO 1099 |
| Sled crash test | Occupant kinematics, restraint timing | Hydraulic sled, ATD dummies | ECE R94, AIS-098 |
| Brake dynamometer | Fade, recovery, efficiency | Inertia dyno | ECE R13, AIS-069 |
| Shaker / vibration test | Resonance, component loosening | Electrodynamic shaker | ISO 16750-3 |
| Salt spray test | Corrosion resistance | Salt spray chamber | ASTM B117, ISO 9227 |
| Thermal cycle test | Material expansion failure | Environmental chamber | ISO 16750-4 |
| Anechoic NVH | Squeak, rattle, booming | Microphone array, head | ISO 5128 |
| EMC test | EMI susceptibility, emissions | Stripline, GTEM cell | CISPR 25, ISO 11452 |
| HIL (Hardware-in-loop) | ECU logic fault | Real-time simulator | ISO 26262 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Component Testing Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **macro-architecture of component testing** as a functional flow — from test planning to homologation sign-off.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Design Specification] --> A1[Define Test Plan and Acceptance Criteria]
    A1 --> A2[Select DUT Sample n=5 to 30]
    A2 --> B1[Functional and Performance Test]
    A2 --> B2[Endurance and Fatigue Test]
    A2 --> B3[Environmental Test]
    A2 --> B4[Crash and Impact Test]
    A2 --> B5[NVH Test]
    A2 --> B6[EMC and Electrical Test]
    B1 --> C1[Data Acquisition and DAQ]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Data Analysis and Statistical Reduction]
    D1 --> E1{Acceptance Met?}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Homologation Sign Off]
    E1 -- No --> G1[Design Iteration]
    G1 --> A1
    F1 --> H1[Production Release]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sled Test Procedure for Airbag Validation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Instrument ATD Hybrid III Dummy] --> S2[Mount on Sled]
    S2 --> S3[Position DUT Airbag Module]
    S3 --> S4[Set Sled Velocity e.g. 50 km/h]
    S4 --> S5[Trigger Sled and Record with High Speed Camera]
    S5 --> S6[Acquire Data: Head, Chest, Femur Load Cells]
    S6 --> S7[Compute HIC and Chest g Limit]
    S7 --> S8{Limit Compliant?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Pass: Compliance to ECE R94]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Fail: Iterate Airbag Venting and Folding]
    S10 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Brake Dynamometer Fade Test Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Mount Brake Assembly on Inertia Dyno] --> T1[Run-in: 30 stops at 50 km/h]
    T1 --> T2[Fade Cycle: 10 stops from 100 to 5 km/h]
    T2 --> T3[Measure Temp, Mu, Pedal Force]
    T3 --> T4[Recovery Cycle: 5 stops at 50 km/h]
    T4 --> T5{Coefficient of Friction within Spec?}
    T5 -- Yes --> T6[Pass: Compliance to ECE R13]
    T5 -- No --> T7[Fail: Respec Pad Material]
    T7 --> T0
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Vibration Test Setup Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Shaker Controller sends PSD profile] --> V2[Power Amplifier]
    V2 --> V3[Electrodynamic Shaker Table]
    V3 --> V4[Test Fixture with DUT]
    V4 --> V5[Accelerometers on DUT]
    V5 --> V6[Data Acquisition System]
    V6 --> V7[Real-time FFT and RMS Analysis]
    V7 --> V8{Resonance Within Spec Window?}
    V8 -- Yes --> V9[Pass: ISO 16750-3 Compliant]
    V8 -- No --> V10[Fail: Add Damper or Stiffen Mount]
    V10 --> V4
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — HVAC Performance Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Conditioned Air Supply Unit] --> H2[Climate Chamber with Car Cabin]
    H2 --> H3[DUT HVAC Evaporator and Heater Core]
    H3 --> H4[Blower Motor with Variable Speed]
    H4 --> H5[Sensor Array: T RH, Air Velocity]
    H5 --> H6[Data Logger: P T RH]
    H6 --> H7[Compute Cooling COP and Heating Q]
    H7 --> H8{Meet AIS-069 Specs?}
    H8 -- Yes --> H9[Pass: Climate Control Validation]
    H8 -- No --> H10[Fail: Redesign Refrigerant Circuit]
    H10 --> H3
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Vehicle-Level Test Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Level 1: Material Tests] --> P2[Level 2: Component Tests]
    P2 --> P3[Level 3: Subsystem Tests]
    P3 --> P4[Level 4: Full Vehicle Tests]
    P4 --> P5[Level 5: Fleet and Field Tests]
    P1 --> P1A[NDT, Tensile, Fatigue]
    P2 --> P2A[Brake, Airbag, HVAC]
    P3 --> P3A[Powertrain, Suspension]
    P4 --> P4A[Crash, NVH, EMC]
    P5 --> P5A[Real World Fleet]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods used in automotive component inspection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) are passed through the component. A flaw reflects the wave, and the time-of-flight reveals defect depth. Used for axle, subframe, and weld inspection.
2. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT):** The component is magnetized; iron particles are sprinkled on the surface. Cracks cause flux leakage, attracting particles and revealing the defect. Used for ferrous castings like brake calipers.
3. **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT):** A liquid penetrant is applied, allowed to seep into surface cracks, then removed. A developer draws out the dye, exposing the defect. Used for non-ferrous parts (aluminum wheels, magnesium castings).

*(Any three NDT methods with 1 mark each for method name and 0.5 mark each for explanation. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define "Specific Energy Absorption (SEA)" and state its significance in crumple zone design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Specific Energy Absorption is the energy absorbed per unit mass of the structure, defined as:
$$\text{SEA} = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{m_{\text{structure}}} \quad \text{(units: J/kg)}$$
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** SEA is a **figure of merit** for crashworthy structures. A higher SEA means the structure absorbs more crash energy with less mass — critical for lightweight vehicle design. Typical values: aluminum foam $5$–$15\ \text{kJ/kg}$, steel tube $20$–$40\ \text{kJ/kg}$, CFRP composites $>50\ \text{kJ/kg}$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, Module 4]`

**Q. A(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **sled test procedure** used for validating an airbag restraint system. List the relevant AIS/ECE standards.

**Q. A(b) [7 marks]** A vehicle of mass **$m = 1800\ \text{kg}$** traveling at **$v = 60\ \text{km/h}$** collides with a rigid wall. The crumple zone crushes over **$d = 0.4\ \text{m}$**. Determine:
1. The average crash force on the vehicle.
2. The average deceleration in $g$.
3. Comment on occupant survivability.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. A(a)

**Sled Test Procedure (5 marks for procedure, 1 mark for sketch, 1 mark for standards):**

1. **Setup:** The airbag module (DUT) and steering wheel assembly are mounted on a hydraulically accelerated sled. An instrumented **ATD dummy** (e.g., Hybrid III 50th percentile male) is positioned in the driver's seat with calibrated load cells at head, chest, pelvis, and femurs.

2. **Instrumentation:** High-speed cameras (1000+ fps) record deployment. Accelerometers and angular rate sensors capture kinematic data.

3. **Test execution:** The sled is propelled to the target velocity (typically $48$–$56\ \text{km/h}$ for frontal). At a precise moment, the **sled is decelerated** by a hydraulic decelerator simulating a vehicle crash pulse.

4. **Trigger:** The airbag ECU fires the inflator (squib) within $5$–$10\ \text{ms}$ of the deceleration onset, deploying the cushion in $20$–$30\ \text{ms}$.

5. **Data acquisition:** Head Injury Criterion (HIC), chest acceleration (target $\leq 60\ g$ for 3 ms), and femur load are measured.

**Standards (1 mark):**
- **ECE R94** (Frontal collision)
- **AIS-098** (India equivalent)
- **FMVSS 208** (US reference)
- **ISO 12097** (Airbag module test)

**Sketch (1 mark):** A schematic showing the sled, decelerator, ATD dummy in driver's seat, and airbag module — clear with labels.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. A(b)

**Given:** $m = 1800\ \text{kg}$, $v = 60\ \text{km/h}$, $d = 0.4\ \text{m}$.

**Step 1: Convert velocity to SI units.**

$$v = 60 \times \frac{5}{18} = 16.67\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Average crash force (using $F_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{m v^2}{2 d}$).**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1800 \times (16.67)^2}{2 \times 0.4} \\
&= \frac{1800 \times 277.9}{0.8} \\
&= \frac{500{,}220}{0.8} \\
&= 625{,}275\ \text{N} \approx 625.3\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Average deceleration.**

$$\begin{aligned}
a_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{F_{\text{avg}}}{m} = \frac{625{,}275}{1800} = 347.4\ \text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to $g$:

$$a = \frac{347.4}{9.81} = 35.4\ g$$

**Step 4: Survivability comment.**
Since $35.4\ g \gg 20\ g$ (the survival limit for a 50th percentile occupant), this crash is **not survivable** with a crush distance of only 0.4 m. The crush distance must be increased (or additional crumple zones engineered) to bring deceleration below $20\ g$.

**Required crush distance for 20 g survival:**

$$d_{\text{req}} = \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot 20 g} = \frac{277.9}{2 \times 196.2} = 0.708\ \text{m}$$

The crumple zone must be **at least 0.71 m** for occupant survival — a key design takeaway.

**Mark split:** (1) $F_{\text{avg}}$: 2 marks, (2) $a_{\text{avg}}$ in $g$: 2 marks, (3) Survivability + required $d$: 3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, Module 4]`

**Q. B(a) [7 marks]** Describe the working principle of a **brake dynamometer** with a neat block diagram. Explain how it is used for the **fade test** and **recovery test** as per ECE R13.

**Q. B(b) [7 marks]** A car of mass $1200\ \text{kg}$ is tested on a brake dynamometer. It is required to perform 10 consecutive stops from $100\ \text{km/h}$ to rest at intervals of 45 seconds.
1. Calculate the total kinetic energy to be dissipated by the brakes.
2. If the brake disc has a mass of $10\ \text{kg}$ and specific heat $500\ \text{J/kg·K}$, calculate the average temperature rise per disc after 10 stops (assuming 80% energy goes to discs).
3. If the disc maximum operating temperature is $500\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and initial temperature is $30\ ^\circ\text{C}$, determine whether the disc exceeds its limit.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. B(a)

**Brake Dynamometer Working Principle (4 marks):**

A **brake dynamometer (inertia dyno)** simulates the road-load inertia using a flywheel set. The brake assembly (caliper + disc) is mounted on the dyno, and the flywheel is accelerated to a target speed equivalent to the vehicle's kinetic energy at test speed.

$$\text{Flywheel KE} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \approx \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{eq}} v^2$$

The brake is then applied, and the flywheel decelerates, replicating a real vehicle stop. Sensors measure brake torque, rotor temperature, pedal force, and stopping distance.

**Block diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Motor Drive] -> [Flywheel/Inertia] -> [Brake DUT] -> [Torque Transducer]
                                                        |
                                                        v
                                                [DAQ and Control]
```

**Fade test procedure (1 mark):**
Per ECE R13, a **fade test** consists of repeated brake applications at high deceleration (e.g., 10 stops from 100 to 5 km/h with 45 s intervals). Brake temperature rises progressively, and the **friction coefficient** is monitored. A drop of more than 15–20% indicates fade failure.

**Recovery test (1 mark):**
After the fade cycle, the brake is allowed to cool for a few minutes. The **efficiency recovery** is checked by a few baseline stops. The brake must recover to within 80% of the original friction coefficient (per ECE R13 Annex 4).

---

#### Model Solution — Q. B(b)

**Given:** $m = 1200\ \text{kg}$, $v = 100\ \text{km/h}$, $n_{\text{stops}} = 10$, $m_d = 10\ \text{kg}$, $c = 500\ \text{J/kg·K}$, $\eta = 0.80$.

**Step 1: Convert velocity.**

$$v = 100 \times \frac{5}{18} = 27.78\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Total kinetic energy over 10 stops.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= n_{\text{stops}} \times \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
&= 10 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1200 \times (27.78)^2 \\
&= 10 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1200 \times 771.6 \\
&= 10 \times 462{,}963 \\
&= 4{,}629{,}630\ \text{J} \approx 4.63\ \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Energy absorbed by each disc (assuming single front disc takes 80% of the energy).**

$$E_{\text{disc}} = 0.80 \times 4{,}629{,}630 = 3{,}703{,}704\ \text{J}$$

**Step 4: Average temperature rise per disc.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{E_{\text{disc}}}{m_d \cdot c} \\
&= \frac{3{,}703{,}704}{10 \times 500} \\
&= \frac{3{,}703{,}704}{5000} \\
&= 740.7\ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Check against limit.**
Final temperature:

$$T_{\text{final}} = T_{\text{initial}} + \Delta T = 30 + 740.7 = 770.7\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

Since $770.7\ ^\circ\text{C} \gg 500\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (max), the disc **exceeds its thermal limit**. This is a textbook case of brake fade — the disc will suffer **brake fade, scoring, and possibly thermal cracking**. The design is inadequate; solutions include:
- Larger disc mass or ventilated disc.
- Higher-performance pad material (e.g., ceramic).
- Improved cooling ducts.
- Reduction of absorbed energy fraction (split between front and rear discs more evenly).

**Mark split:** (1) Total KE: 2 marks, (2) $\Delta T$: 3 marks, (3) Limit check + recommendation: 2 marks.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors ($-2$ marks):** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s in crash/kinetic energy problems. Always use $v \times \frac{5}{18}$.
> 2. **Forgetting Gravitational Constant:** When asked for deceleration in $g$, students often leave it in m/s². Multiply/divide by $g = 9.81$.
> 3. **Skipping the Survivability Comment:** Numerical questions on crashes **always** ask for a comment. Writing only the value of $a$ (e.g., $35.4\ g$) without stating "exceeds $20\ g$, not survivable" loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Brake Fade vs. Recovery:** Fade = efficiency loss at high temp; Recovery = efficiency regain on cooling. Marks lost on this distinction.
> 5. **Not Mentioning Standards:** Every component test answer **must** include at least one AIS / ECE / ISO reference for full credit. A description without standards = $-2$ marks.
> 6. **Mixing Destructive and NDT:** Lists under "NDT" that include tensile testing (a destructive method) are penalized.
> 7. **Mermaid/Sketch Missing in Long Answers:** Part B (a) questions often require a diagram. Omission = $-1$ mark minimum.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam:**

- **Component testing** validates each subsystem for safety, comfort, durability, and regulatory compliance.
- **Two big categories:** *Destructive* (crash, tensile, fatigue) and *Non-Destructive* (ultrasonic, X-ray, dye-penetrant, eddy current).
- **Crash energy** is the foundation: $E = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$. **Average crash force:** $F = m v^2 / (2 d)$.
- **Survivability** requires $a \leq 20\ g$ (AIS / ECE R94).
- **SEA** (Specific Energy Absorption) = energy absorbed / mass — key figure of merit for crumple zones.
- **Airbag deploys in 20–30 ms**; pretensioner fires in ~10 ms. Delay > 50 ms risks occupant contact.
- **Brake dynamometer** uses flywheel inertia; fade test = high-temp efficiency loss; recovery test = post-cooling efficiency.
- **Miner's Rule** predicts fatigue: $D = \sum (n_i / N_i) \geq 1$ implies failure.
- **NVH target:** cabin SPL $\leq 70\ \text{dB(A)}$ cruise, $\leq 55\ \text{dB(A)}$ idle (premium).
- **HVAC test** uses psychrometric chamber; cooling capacity $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$.
- **Standards family:** ECE R13 (brakes), R94 (frontal crash), R16 (seatbelts), R17 (seats), R95 (side impact); AIS-006, 069, 098 (India).
- **Vibration transmissibility** $T_r$ — isolation when excitation frequency $f > \sqrt{2} \cdot f_n$.
- **Natural frequency** $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{k/m}$; **damping ratio** $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$.
- **Test pyramid:** Material $\rightarrow$ Component $\rightarrow$ Subsystem $\rightarrow$ Vehicle $\rightarrow$ Fleet.
- **HIL testing** of ECUs replaces road testing — saves time and cost.
- **Always** state units, **always** reference a standard, **always** add a survivability or design comment to crash numerics.
- **Examiner's mantra:** "Show your steps. Justify the assumptions. Cite a standard. Comment on the result."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Ratings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT861 - AUTOMOTIVE COMFORT AND SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Ratings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Vehicle Safety Ratings

A **Vehicle Safety Rating** is a standardized, comparative, and publicly communicated assessment of a vehicle's passive and active safety performance, derived from a series of reproducible crash tests, physical measurements, and instrumented evaluations conducted by an independent regulatory or consumer-oriented body. In the context of modern automotive engineering, safety ratings are issued by programs collectively known as **New Car Assessment Programs (NCAPs)**, which translate complex crash engineering data into an easy-to-understand consumer metric — most commonly expressed as a **5-Star Rating** in whole or half-star increments.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEAUT861, Module 4), safety ratings encompass both the institutional rating frameworks (Euro NCAP, Global NCAP, ANCAP, IIHS, US-NCAP) and the engineering parameters being rated (Adult Occupant Protection, Child Occupant Protection, Vulnerable Road User Protection, and Safety Assist).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEAUT861 Module 4):**
> Safety Ratings form the consumer-facing outcome of passive and active safety systems. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the methodology of NCAPs, the four assessment pillars, the star calculation matrix, and the role of safety ratings in OEM design decisions.

### 1.2 Key Rating Agencies (NCAP Family)

| Program | Region | Operator | Established |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Euro NCAP** | Europe | Joint govt + motoring clubs | 1997 |
| **US NCAP (NHTSA)** | USA | NHTSA | 1978 |
| **IIHS** | USA | Insurance Institute for Highway Safety | 1959 |
| **Global NCAP** | Emerging markets | FI Foundation | 2011 |
| **ANCAP** | Australia/NZ | ANCAP Pty Ltd | 1999 |
| **JNCAP** | Japan | NASVA / MLIT | 1995 |
| **Bharat NCAP** | India | Ministry of Road Transport | 2023 |
| **C-NCAP** | China | CATARC | 2006 |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Restaurant Hygiene Rating:**
> Think of a restaurant's "5-star hygiene rating" displayed on its front window. The number itself doesn't tell you *how* the kitchen was inspected, *what* bacteria were tested, or *which* surfaces were swabbed. It is a *condensed, trustworthy shorthand* issued by an independent food-safety authority so a customer can make a quick, safe decision.
>
> A vehicle's safety star rating works in exactly the same way. The crash engineering is the equivalent of the kitchen inspection. The **5-star mark** on the car's brochure is the trust signal. Crash tests like a **40 % frontal offset deformable barrier (ODB)** at **64 km/h**, a **side mobile deformable barrier (MDB)** at **50 km/h**, and a **far-side passenger dummy head excursion** are the "swab tests." The star is what the family buying a new car actually sees and trusts.

### 1.4 The Four Standard Assessment Pillars

A modern NCAP rating is a *weighted composite* of four distinct evaluation areas:

1. **Adult Occupant Protection (AOP)** — front and side impact performance of the driver and front passenger.
2. **Child Occupant Protection (COP)** — restraint performance, child seat installation, and airbag compatibility for pediatric occupants (typically P3, P6, P10 dummies).
3. **Vulnerable Road User Protection (VRU)** — pedestrian and cyclist headform / legform impactor tests on the bonnet, bumper, and windscreen cowl.
4. **Safety Assist (SA)** — presence and effectiveness of active driver-assist systems (AEB, lane support, speed assistance, electronic stability control, attention assist).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition – Star:**
> A *Star* is the discrete ordinal output of a NCAP, representing a quantile bucket of aggregate performance. The integer star (1 to 5) is the most common representation. Each star corresponds to a defined score range computed from the four pillars.

### 1.5 Why Safety Ratings Matter (Engineering Utility)

- **Regulatory pathway:** Many emerging-market NCAPs use ratings to drive *homologation* (type approval).
- **OEM design driver:** A higher star rating directly influences trim-level passive-safety content (more airbags, pretensioners, ESC, AEB, active bonnet).
- **Insurance pricing:** IIHS Top Safety Pick and Euro NCAP 5-star vehicles receive lower insurance premiums.
- **Consumer transparency:** The star collapses hundreds of measurements into a single comparable number.
- **Research benchmark:** Engineers use NCAP protocols to benchmark new restraint architectures (e.g., far-side airbags, centre airbags, dual-stage inflators).

### 1.6 The Five-Star Symbol (The Common Output)

The universally recognized five-star graphic is the **consumer-facing output**. The mapping between an aggregate weighted score and the star count is a defined function of the rating body, not a continuous free parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> A common misconception: "More airbags automatically means more stars." This is **false**. The number, type, deployment logic, and test performance of an airbag together determine the rating. A poorly tuned airbag can *lower* an AOP score by inflating too aggressively (head injury criterion $\text{HIC}_{36}$ violation) or too late.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Euro NCAP Rating Protocol (Reference Standard)

Euro NCAP is the de-facto global reference and is the version most frequently referenced in KTU theory questions. The adult occupant test cell uses four test modalities:

1. **Full Width Rigid Barrier (FWRB)** — $50 \text{ km/h}$, 100 % overlap, measures restraint of driver and rear passenger in a uniform crush.
2. **Mobile Progressive Deformable Barrier (MPDB / ODB frontal)** — $50 \text{ km/h}$ since 2020, 40 % overlap. Replaces the legacy $64 \text{ km/h}$ test.
3. **Side Mobile Deformable Barrier (MDB)** — $60 \text{ km/h}$ at 90° (car struck on driver side).
4. **Far-Side Occupant Test** — assesses head excursion of the front passenger in an oblique / oblique-moving-deformable-barrier event.
5. **Pole Side Impact** — $32 \text{ km/h}$ at 75° on a rigid pole, evaluating curtain airbag coverage and head thorax coupling.
6. **Whiplash (Rear-End)** — sled test for seat/head restraint geometry.
7. **VRU tests** — adult/child headform (impactor radius $165 \text{ mm}$ at $11.1 \text{ m/s}$), upper/lower legform (TRL flex-PLI) at $40 \text{ km/h}$.
8. **Safety Assist tests** — AEB Inter-Urban, AEB Pedestrian/Cyclist, Lane Support, Speed Assist, eCall, Attention Assist.

### 2.2 The Body of Mathematical Scoring Logic

The aggregate rating score is a **weighted linear sum** of pillar scores, where each pillar score is itself a clamped, normalized, weighted sum of sub-test scores. The general framework is:

$$
S_{\text{aggregate}} \;=\; w_{\text{AOP}}\,S_{\text{AOP}} \;+\; w_{\text{COP}}\,S_{\text{COP}} \;+\; w_{\text{VRU}}\,S_{\text{VRU}} \;+\; w_{\text{SA}}\,S_{\text{SA}}
$$

subject to $S_{\text{aggregate}} \in [0, 100]$ and $\sum w_i = 1$.

Within each pillar, a sub-test score for body region $k$ is computed from injury criteria and a colour-coded dummy reading:

$$
S_{\text{pillar}} \;=\; \sum_{k \in \text{regions}} w_k \, \mathrm{clamp}\!\left(\,\frac{M_k - I_k}{M_k - L_k},\; 0,\; 1\,\right) \cdot 100
$$

where:
- $I_k$ = recorded injury criterion value (e.g., $\text{HIC}_{36}$, chest deflection $3\text{ms}$).
- $L_k$ = lower (best possible) limit of the criterion.
- $M_k$ = upper (poorer) limit where score is zeroed.
- $w_k$ = region weighting, $\sum w_k = 1$.

The four-point colour mapping (Green / Yellow / Orange / Red) used in Euro NCAP publications corresponds to quartiles of the same injury criterion.

### 2.3 Key Injury Criteria Used in Rating

| Criterion | Symbol | Definition | Acceptable Limit (Driver, Frontal) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head Injury Criterion (36 ms) | $\text{HIC}_{36}$ | $\bigl(t_2 - t_1\bigr)\!\left[\,\tfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\!\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a \, dt\,\right]^{2.5}$ | $\le 1000$ |
| Neck Shear Force | $F_x$ (occipital condyle) | peak axial shear | $\le 3.1 \text{ kN}$ at $0 \text{ ms}$ |
| Neck Tension | $F_z$ | peak axial tension | $\le 2.62 \text{ kN}$ tension |
| Chest Compression | $C$ | sternum deflection | $\le 50 \text{ mm}$ (Hybrid III) |
| Viscous Criterion | $V \cdot C$ | $\text{rate} \times \text{compression}$ | $\le 1.0 \text{ m/s}$ |
| Femur Force | $F_{\text{femur}}$ | axial load | $\le 10 \text{ kN}$ |
| Tibia Index | $\text{TI}$ | upper / lower index | $\le 1.4$ each |
| Chest Acceleration (Side) | $a_{3\text{ms}}$ | 3 ms exceedance | $\le 60 g$ |
| Pelvis Acceleration | $a_{\text{pelvis}}$ | pubic symphysis force | $\le 6 \text{ kN}$ |
| Pedestrian Headform | $\text{HIC}_{15}$ | head impact on bonnet | $\le 1700$ (lower better) |

### 2.4 The Star Mapping (Euro NCAP 2024 Example)

| Stars | Aggregate Score Range |
|---|---|
| 5 Stars | $\ge 80\%$ + all minimum threshold checks |
| 4 Stars | $\ge 70\%$ |
| 3 Stars | $\ge 50\%$ |
| 2 Stars | $\ge 20\%$ |
| 1 Star | $< 20\%$ |

A vehicle **cannot** achieve 5 stars unless it scores at least the minimum in *every* sub-criterion (a "no-zero" rule), and a 2024 update requires **AEB** as a baseline for any star.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No-Zero Rule (Existence Threshold):**
> Euro NCAP 5-star requires the vehicle to reach the *score threshold* AND clear an *equipment minimum*. The most important baseline is that the vehicle must include ESC, AEB, and an indirect TPMS at minimum. If any of these are absent, the maximum rating is capped at 4 stars regardless of crash score.

### 2.5 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{HIC}_{36} = \bigl(t_2-t_1\bigr)\!\left[\,\tfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\!\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t) \, dt\,\right]^{2.5}$ | Head Injury Criterion, 36 ms window | dimensionless |
| 2 | $V \cdot C(t) = v_{\text{def}}(t) \cdot c(t)$ | Soft-tissue viscous response | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $S_{\text{agg}} = \sum_{i} w_i S_i$ | Weighted pillar score | $\% \in [0,100]$ |
| 4 | $S_{\text{region}} = \mathrm{clamp}\!\left(\,\frac{M-I}{M-L},\,0,\,1\right) \times 100$ | Sub-score normalization | $\% \in [0,100]$ |
| 5 | $N_{\text{stars}} = f(S_{\text{agg}})$ | Discrete step function, thresholds at $20, 50, 70, 80$ | integer |
| 6 | $\text{Kinetic Energy}_\text{crash} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Energy absorbed in deformable structure | $\text{J}$ |
| 7 | $E_{\text{abs}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m\left(v_{\text{in}}^2 - v_{\text{out}}^2\right)$ | Energy managed by crumple zone | $\text{J}$ |
| 8 | $\text{Crumple stroke} = \dfrac{m v^2}{2 F_{\text{avg}}}$ | Required crush length for constant force | $\text{m}$ |
| 9 | $H_{\text{bel}} = 200 \text{ mm}$ | Hybrid III mid-size male 95-th percentile H-point | $\text{mm}$ |
| 10 | $v_{\text{impact}} = 50 \text{ km/h}$ (MPDB), $60 \text{ km/h}$ (MDB), $32 \text{ km/h}$ (Pole) | Euro NCAP test velocities | $\text{km/h}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> The 5-star system is more than a marketing badge. For OEMs, it is a **multi-objective optimization** target: simultaneously minimizing $\text{HIC}$, neck forces, chest deflection, femur force, and tibia index, while *maximizing* VRU headform compliance, AEB responsiveness, and child restraint fitment. Automotive safety engineers call this the **"star map"** — a heatmap showing how each design variable moves the four pillar scores.

### 2.6 The Test-Cell Architecture (Engineering View)

The crash test cell is the heart of any NCAP. Key infrastructure:

- **Crash track** $\ge 120 \text{ m}$ long, $4 \text{ m}$ wide, polished steel.
- **Towing vehicle** accelerates the test car via a cable-release mechanism.
- **Barrier (rigid or deformable)** weighs $1300 \text{ kg}$ (MDB) or is hydraulically braked (MPDB).
- **High-speed cameras** — minimum $1000 \text{ fps}$ on board and off board.
- **Onboard Data Acquisition (ODAQ)** — 64-channel minimum, 25 kHz sampling.
- **Anthropomorphic Test Devices (ATDs):** Hybrid III 50th, Hybrid III 5th, THOR-50M, WorldSID 50th, Q6, Q10, P3, P6, P10.
- **Pedestrian Headform Impactor (PHI)** — aluminium sphere, $4.5 \text{ kg}$, $\phi 165 \text{ mm}$.

### 2.7 Active Safety Test Logic (Safety Assist)

The Safety Assist pillar scores systems that *prevent* crashes:

- **AEB Inter-Urban (AEB CCrs):** AEB responds to a stationary / slower lead vehicle from $10 \text{ km/h}$ to $80 \text{ km/h}$ subject car. Scored by *avoidance* or *mitigation*.
- **AEB VRU (pedestrian & cyclist):** AEB avoids / mitigates impact with adult / child crossing at $20 \text{–} 60 \text{ km/h}$.
- **Lane Support System (LSS):** Lane Departure Warning + Lane Keep Assist must be present and demonstrably functional.
- **Speed Assist System (SAS):** ISA — Intelligent Speed Assistance, mandatory for 5 stars in 2024.
- **Occupant Status Monitoring (OSM):** Driver drowsiness / attention monitoring, mandatory.
- **eCall:** Automatic emergency call system.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Trend Toward Active-Dominant Rating:**
> Since 2020, the Safety Assist pillar weight has been progressively increased because ADAS technologies (AEB, LSS, ESC) have shown $\sim 38\%$ real-world reduction in rear-end crashes (Euro NCAP 2023 statistics). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly highlights this shift toward "primary safety" — preventing the crash rather than surviving it.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the HIC Value (Head Injury Criterion)

The HIC is a non-linear time-windowed integral of resultant head acceleration. It is the single most-cited injury criterion in any NCAP.

$$
\text{HIC}_{36} \;=\; \max_{t_1, t_2} \left\{\,\bigl(t_2 - t_1\bigr)\!\left[\,\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t) \, dt\,\right]^{2.5}\,\right\}
$$

The expression inside the brackets is the **time-averaged resultant head acceleration** (in $g$ units) over the window $[t_1, t_2]$; the **window length is bounded by 36 ms**, hence the subscript.

**Worked Analytical Walk-Through (Synthetic Pulse):**

Suppose a head pulse is approximately triangular: it ramps linearly from $0$ to $a_{\max}$ over a time $t_{\text{rise}} = 10 \text{ ms}$, then linearly falls back to $0$ at $t_{\text{fall}} = 30 \text{ ms}$. Take $a_{\max} = 90 \, g$.

**Step 1 — Compute the time-averaged acceleration for the full window.**
For a triangle, the average is exactly half the peak:

$$
\bar{a} \;=\; \frac{a_{\max}}{2} \;=\; \frac{90}{2} \;=\; 45 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the window length.**
The peak is inside the head pulse; the optimal HIC window of $36 \text{ ms}$ will be centred near the peak.

$$
\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = 36 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the HIC formula.**

$$
\text{HIC}_{36} \;=\; (\Delta t)\left(\bar{a}\right)^{2.5} \;=\; (0.036)(45)^{2.5}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power term.**

$$
(45)^{2.5} \;=\; 45^{2} \cdot 45^{0.5} \;=\; 2025 \cdot \sqrt{45}
$$

$$
\sqrt{45} \;=\; 6.7082
$$

$$
2025 \cdot 6.7082 \;\approx\; 13584
$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication.**

$$
\text{HIC}_{36} \;\approx\; 0.036 \times 13584 \;\approx\; 489
$$

**Step 6 — Compare against the limit.**

The Euro NCAP upper limit is $\text{HIC}_{36} \le 1000$. Hence this pulse is well within tolerance and would receive full credit (no deduction) in the head sub-score.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 2.5 Exponent:** The exponent 2.5 is empirical, derived from WSU (Wayne State University) cadaver and volunteer tests by Ewing, Thomas, and Patrick in the 1960s–70s. It captures the non-linear skull-brain tolerance curve — a small change in average acceleration causes a disproportionately large change in injury risk.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Crumple-Zone Stroke Requirement

A simple but powerful derivation: how much crush distance is needed to keep the *average deceleration* below a survivable level.

**Statement:** A vehicle of mass $m$ travelling at $v$ must be decelerated over a stroke $s$ such that the *average* deceleration $a_{\text{avg}} = v^2 / (2 s)$ is below a target $\bar{a}^*$.

**Step 1 — Energy balance.**
The kinetic energy must equal the work done by the restraint:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \;=\; F_{\text{avg}} \cdot s
$$

**Step 2 — Average force is the average deceleration times mass.**

$$
F_{\text{avg}} \;=\; m \, a_{\text{avg}} \;\;\text{and}\;\; a_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \frac{v^2}{2 s}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required stroke $s$.**

$$
s \;=\; \frac{m v^2}{2 F_{\text{avg}}} \;=\; \frac{v^2}{2 a_{\text{avg}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical instantiation for a Euro NCAP MPDB test.**

- $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- $v = 50 \text{ km/h} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}$
- Target $\bar{a}^* = 25 \, g = 245.25 \text{ m/s}^2$

$$
s \;=\; \frac{(13.89)^2}{2 \times 245.25} \;=\; \frac{192.93}{490.5} \;\approx\; 0.393 \text{ m} \;\; \approx\; 393 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

A modern monocoque provides $\sim 400 \text{–} 500 \text{ mm}$ of front crumple stroke between the bumper beam and the firewall. This is **engineered to fit** with the MPDB impact energy.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Computing a Pillar Score

**Problem:** A vehicle completes the Euro NCAP Full-Width Rigid Barrier (FWRB) test. The driver-side injury readings are:

- $\text{HIC}_{36} = 720$ (limit $1000$)
- Chest compression $= 28 \text{ mm}$ (limit $50 \text{ mm}$)
- Femur force $= 6.4 \text{ kN}$ (limit $10 \text{ kN}$)
- Neck shear $F_x = 2.1 \text{ kN}$ (limit $3.1 \text{ kN}$)
- Tibia index (compressive) $= 0.95$ (limit $1.4$)

Each body region has a weight: head $0.40$, chest $0.30$, femurs $0.15$, neck $0.10$, tibiae $0.05$. Compute the driver-side FWRB score.

**Step 1 — Convert each injury reading to a normalized $[0, 1]$ score.**

$$
\text{head} \;=\; \frac{1000 - 720}{1000 - 500} \;=\; \frac{280}{500} \;=\; 0.560
$$

(Here $L = 500$, $M = 1000$.)

$$
\text{chest} \;=\; \frac{50 - 28}{50 - 22} \;=\; \frac{22}{28} \;\approx\; 0.786
$$

(Here $L = 22 \text{ mm}$, $M = 50 \text{ mm}$.)

$$
\text{femur} \;=\; \frac{10 - 6.4}{10 - 4} \;=\; \frac{3.6}{6.0} \;=\; 0.600
$$

$$
\text{neck} \;=\; \frac{3.1 - 2.1}{3.1 - 1.0} \;=\; \frac{1.0}{2.1} \;\approx\; 0.476
$$

$$
\text{tibia} \;=\; \frac{1.4 - 0.95}{1.4 - 0.4} \;=\; \frac{0.45}{1.0} \;=\; 0.450
$$

**Step 2 — Weighted sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{FWRB,driver}} &= 0.40(0.560) + 0.30(0.786) + 0.15(0.600) + 0.10(0.476) + 0.05(0.450)\\
&= 0.224 + 0.2358 + 0.090 + 0.0476 + 0.0225\\
&\approx 0.620
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
S_{\text{FWRB,driver}} \;\approx\; 62.0\%
$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the AOP pillar.**

Repeat for ODB driver, MDB driver, pole driver, FWRB passenger, ODB passenger, MDB passenger, pole passenger, far-side. The AOP pillar score is the weighted average across all eight. Assume symmetry and same numerical methodology, then aggregate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** In the KTU theory paper, students are often asked to **interpret** an NCAP bar chart, not reproduce all 47 injury criteria. Focus on explaining what the colour-coding means, the four pillars, the score weighting, and the role of AEB.

---

### 3.4 The Star Allocation Decision (Binary Tree)

The decision is **not** a single threshold check — it is a *conjunction* (AND) of threshold plus a no-zero check plus a minimum-equipment check.

$$
\text{Stars} \;=\; f(S_{\text{agg}}) \;\wedge\; \bigl[\forall k,\; I_k \le M_k\bigr] \;\wedge\; \bigl[\text{Equipment}_\text{min} = \text{present}\bigr]
$$

In Boolean logic:

$$
\text{5-stars} \;\iff\; S_{\text{agg}} \ge 80 \;\wedge\; \text{no-zero} \;\wedge\; \text{min-eq}
$$

$$
\text{4-stars} \;\iff\; S_{\text{agg}} \ge 70 \;\wedge\; \text{no-zero} \;\wedge\; \text{min-eq}
$$

$$
\text{3-stars} \;\iff\; S_{\text{agg}} \ge 50 \;\wedge\; \text{min-eq}
$$

$$
\text{2-stars} \;\iff\; S_{\text{agg}} \ge 20
$$

$$
\text{1-star} \;\iff\; S_{\text{agg}} < 20
$$

A vehicle that scores $S_{\text{agg}} = 95$ but is missing ESC **cannot** be 5 stars; it is capped at 4 stars.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Some students believe a 5-star aggregate guarantees 5 stars. This is **wrong** under Euro NCAP 2024 rules. The no-zero and minimum-equipment checks can cap the rating.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major NCAP Programs

| Feature | Euro NCAP | US-NCAP (NHTSA) | IIHS | Global NCAP | Bharat NCAP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frontal test | MPDB 50 km/h | Full-width 56 km/h | Small overlap 64 km/h | MPDB 50 km/h | ODB 64 km/h |
| Side test | MDB 60 km/h | MDB 61.9 km/h | IIHS Side 50 km/h | MDB 50 km/h | MDB 50 km/h |
| Pole | 32 km/h | none | none | none | none |
| Pedestrian | headform + legform | none in NHTSA | moderate overlap IIHS | headform + legform | legform only |
| AEB | mandatory for 5★ | forward collision warning | Top Safety Pick+ | optional | not yet |
| Stars | 1 to 5 | 1 to 5 | TSP / TSP+ | 1 to 5 | 1 to 5 |
| Operator | independent | gov | insurance-funded | FI Foundation | gov |
| Public release | full report | full report | full report | summary | summary |

This matrix is high-yield: the KTU 2024 paper routinely tests "Compare Euro NCAP and IIHS" or "Discuss Bharat NCAP" as a Part B 14-marker.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Four-Pillar Assessment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Safety Rating Program] --> B[Adult Occupant Protection]
    A --> C[Child Occupant Protection]
    A --> D[Vulnerable Road User Protection]
    A --> E[Safety Assist]

    B --> B1[Frontal FWRB 50 km/h]
    B --> B2[Frontal MPDB 50 km/h]
    B --> B3[Side MDB 60 km/h]
    B --> B4[Pole 32 km/h]
    B --> B5[Far Side Test]
    B --> B6[Whiplash Rear Sled]

    C --> C1[Dynamic 3 year old Q3]
    C --> C2[Dynamic 6 year old Q6]
    C --> C3[Dynamic 10 year old Q10]
    C --> C4[CRS Installation]
    C --> C5[Airbag Disabling Switch]

    D --> D1[Adult Headform PHI]
    D --> D2[Child Headform PHI]
    D --> D3[Upper Legform FlexPLI]
    D --> D4[Lower Legform TRL]
    D --> D5[AEB VRU]
    D --> D6[Cyclist AEB]

    E --> E1[AEB Inter Urban]
    E --> E2[Lane Support]
    E --> E3[Speed Assist]
    E --> E4[ESC mandatory]
    E --> E5[Occupant Status Monitoring]
    E --> E6[eCall System]
```

**How to read this diagram:** The root node is the entire rating program. Each pillar is independent. The four pillars are aggregated into the star. The leaves list the sub-tests (e.g., the FWRB at 50 km/h is one of seven sub-tests in the Adult Occupant Protection pillar). Each leaf has its own injury criteria (HIC, chest compression, etc.) and its own sub-score.

### 4.2 The Star Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Crash Test 1] --> A2[Injury Criterion Read]
    A2 --> A3[Clamp Normalize 0 to 1]
    A3 --> A4[Region Weight 0.40 Head etc]
    A4 --> A5[Sub Test Score percent]
    A5 --> A6[Pillar AOP Score]

    B1[Crash Test 2] --> A2
    C1[Crash Test N] --> A2

    A6 --> D1[Aggregate Weighted Sum]
    D2[COP Score] --> D1
    D3[VRU Score] --> D1
    D4[SA Score] --> D1

    D1 --> E1[Compare to Threshold 80 percent]
    E1 --> E2{No Zero Check}
    E2 --> E3{Min Equipment}
    E3 --> E4[Star Award]
```

This pipeline is exactly how a Euro NCAP engineer computes the rating. The three Boolean checks at the end are the "no-zero" and "minimum equipment" gates that KTU exam questions often test.

### 4.3 The Crash Test Cell (Block Diagram View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Towing Vehicle] --> B[Drive Cable]
    B --> C[Release Pin at Barrier Line]
    C --> D[Vehicle Under Test]
    D --> E[Barrier Frontal or Side]
    D --> F[Onboard Data Acquisition]
    F --> G[64 Channels at 25 kHz]
    G --> H[Injury Criteria Calculation]
    E --> I[Off board High Speed Camera]
    I --> J[1000 fps minimum]
    J --> K[Post Crash Deformation Index]
    H --> L[Euro NCAP Report]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Pillar Score]
    M --> N[Star Rating]
```

This shows the engineering data flow: a single test produces approximately 1.6 million samples ($64 \times 25000 = 1.6 \times 10^6$ per second), which are processed to injury criteria, normalized, weighted, and then aggregated into the star.

### 4.4 The Safety Rating Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
timeline
    title Evolution of Vehicle Safety Ratings
    1959 : IIHS founded USA Insurance Institute
    1978 : US NCAP established by NHTSA
    1995 : JNCAP Japan starts 5 star
    1997 : Euro NCAP first results published
    1999 : ANCAP Australia New Zealand
    2009 : Euro NCAP adds pedestrian star
    2014 : Euro NCAP introduces AEB requirements
    2020 : Euro NCAP moves to MPDB 50 km/h
    2022 : Euro NCAP introduces far side test
    2023 : Bharat NCAP launched India
    2024 : Euro NCAP adds AEB cyclist mandatory
    2026 : Bharat NCAP 5 star target
```

### 4.5 The OEM Design Decision Tree (5-Star Targeting)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OEM Begins Vehicle Program] --> B{Target 5 Star}
    B -- Yes --> C[Baseline Equipment ESC AEB LSS]
    C --> D[Add 6 or more airbags]
    D --> E[Centre airbag for far side]
    E --> F[Active bonnet VRU]
    F --> G[Pre crash seatbelt pretension]
    G --> H[Pedestrian friendly bumper]
    H --> I[Whiplash optimised seat]
    I --> J[Submit for Euro NCAP Test]
    J --> K{Score greater than 80 percent}
    K -- Yes --> L[Achieve 5 Star]
    K -- No --> M[Redesign Sub system]
    M --> J
    B -- No --> N[Target 3 or 4 Star]
    N --> O[Reduce Airbag Count]
    O --> P[Standard Restraint]
    P --> Q[Submit Lower Tier Test]
```

This is the **design-for-stars** view that automotive safety engineers use during the body-in-white phase. Each block represents a CAD/CAE/validation decision. The KTU question bank frequently asks "What design decisions influence the safety rating of a car?" — this diagram answers it.
