## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term "Vehicle Safety Rating" and list the four main assessment pillars used by Euro NCAP.

**Model Answer:**
A *Vehicle Safety Rating* is a standardized, comparative metric of a vehicle's passive and active safety performance, derived from reproducible crash tests and instrumented evaluations, and expressed as a star rating (typically 1 to 5).

The four pillars are:
1. **Adult Occupant Protection (AOP)** — measures injury risk to adult front occupants in front, side, pole and whiplash tests.
2. **Child Occupant Protection (COP)** — measures injury risk to pediatric occupants using Q3, Q6, Q10 dummies and child restraint systems.
3. **Vulnerable Road User Protection (VRU)** — measures injury risk to pedestrians and cyclists through headform and legform tests.
4. **Safety Assist (SA)** — assesses the presence and effectiveness of active driver-assistance systems (AEB, LSS, ESC, attention assist, eCall).

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for listing, 1 for brief explanation.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
What is the HIC and what is its acceptable limit under Euro NCAP for the head injury criterion?

**Model Answer:**
The **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** is a non-linear time-windowed integral of the resultant head acceleration, used as a predictor of skull-brain injury severity. It is calculated as:

$$
\text{HIC}_{36} \;=\; \max_{t_1, t_2} \left\{\,\bigl(t_2 - t_1\bigr)\!\left[\,\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t) \, dt\,\right]^{2.5}\,\right\}
$$

The window length is 36 ms, hence the subscript "36". The acceptable limit under Euro NCAP for an adult Hybrid III 50th-percentile male dummy is $\text{HIC}_{36} \le 1000$. Values below 500 typically score full marks; values above 1000 score zero.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for limit.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the Euro NCAP test protocols for Adult Occupant Protection. Describe the four main crash tests, the dummies used, and the key injury criteria evaluated. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the methodology used by Euro NCAP to compute the aggregate star rating from the four pillars. Explain the role of the "no-zero rule" and the minimum-equipment check. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Adult Occupant Protection (AOP) pillar of Euro NCAP consists of four major crash test events:

**1. Full-Width Rigid Barrier (FWRB) Test:**
The test vehicle impacts a rigid barrier at $50 \text{ km/h}$ with $100\%$ overlap. The barrier is non-deformable, so the entire front structure of the vehicle crushes. The purpose is to evaluate restraint performance in a uniform, symmetric frontal event. The driver seat uses a Hybrid III 50th-percentile male dummy, and the rear passenger seat uses a Hybrid III 5th-percentile female dummy. Key injury criteria measured include $\text{HIC}_{36}$ (head), neck shear $F_x$, neck tension $F_z$, chest deflection, viscous criterion $V \cdot C$, and femur force.

**2. Mobile Progressive Deformable Barrier (MPDB) Frontal Test:**
The test vehicle, fitted with driver and rear passenger Hybrid III 50th male and 5th female dummies, impacts a 1300 kg barrier at $50 \text{ km/h}$ with a 40% overlap on the driver side. The MPDB barrier represents a contemporary car-on-car collision. The smaller overlap makes this test the more challenging of the two frontal tests, because the load path is offset from the longitudinal rails. Injury criteria include $\text{HIC}_{36}$, chest compression, neck injury criteria (Nij), femur force, tibia index, and foot/ankle acceleration.

**3. Mobile Deformable Barrier (MDB) Side Test:**
A 1300 kg trolley travelling at $60 \text{ km/h}$ strikes the driver side of the stationary test car at 90° at the B-pillar. The driver seat contains a WorldSID 50th-percentile male dummy. Key injury criteria include rib deflection (chest), HIC, pelvis pubic symphysis force, and abdominal peak force. The rear seat contains a Q6 dummy for side impact protection. The purpose is to evaluate thorax airbag, curtain airbag, side door reinforcement, and seat design.

**4. Pole Side Test:**
The test vehicle, propelled at $32 \text{ km/h}$, slides sideways into a rigid vertical pole at 75° (driver head contact zone). The dummy is a WorldSID with head instrumentation. The criterion is the HIC for the head, designed to assess the head-protective coverage of the curtain airbag. A curtain that is too short or deploys too late will result in a high HIC and a low score.

(7 marks: 2 marks for listing, 3 marks for protocol description, 2 marks for criteria.)

**Model Solution for (b):**

The Euro NCAP aggregate star rating is computed as a weighted linear combination of the four pillar scores, where each pillar score is itself a normalized weighted sum of sub-test scores.

**Step 1 — Sub-Test Score Calculation:**
For each body region $k$ (head, chest, neck, femur, tibia, etc.) in a given sub-test, the injury criterion reading $I_k$ is mapped to a normalized $[0, 1]$ score:

$$
S_k = \mathrm{clamp}\!\left(\,\frac{M_k - I_k}{M_k - L_k},\,0,\,1\right)
$$

where $L_k$ is the lower (best) limit, $M_k$ is the upper (worst) limit, and $I_k$ is the measured value.

**Step 2 — Pillar Score:**
The sub-test score is the weighted sum of body-region scores:

$$
S_{\text{sub}} = \sum_{k} w_k S_k \times 100
$$

The pillar score (e.g., AOP) is the weighted average of all sub-test scores (FWRB driver, FWRB passenger, MPDB driver, MDB driver, Pole, Whiplash, etc.).

**Step 3 — Aggregate Score:**

$$
S_{\text{agg}} = w_{\text{AOP}}\,S_{\text{AOP}} + w_{\text{COP}}\,S_{\text{COP}} + w_{\text{VRU}}\,S_{\text{VRU}} + w_{\text{SA}}\,S_{\text{SA}}
$$

The weights are roughly: AOP $40\%$, COP $20\%$, VRU $20\%$, SA $20\%$.

**Step 4 — Star Mapping:**
A discrete function maps $S_{\text{agg}}$ to a star count: $\ge 80\%$ → 5 stars, $\ge 70\%$ → 4 stars, $\ge 50\%$ → 3 stars, $\ge 20\%$ → 2 stars, $<20\%$ → 1 star.

**Step 5 — The No-Zero Rule:**
Even if $S_{\text{agg}} \ge 80\%$, the vehicle **cannot** achieve 5 stars if any single body region in any test scores a *zero*. For example, a femur force of $11.5 \text{ kN}$ (exceeding the $10 \text{ kN}$ upper limit) would yield a zero femur score in that test. The 5-star gate requires that *every* sub-region clear the threshold.

**Step 6 — The Minimum-Equipment Check:**
Euro NCAP mandates a baseline of safety equipment for the highest stars. To achieve 5 stars, the vehicle must include: ESC, AEB Inter-Urban, AEB Pedestrian, Lane Support System, Speed Assist, and an attention assist system. A vehicle that lacks AEB is capped at 4 stars. A vehicle lacking ESC is capped at 3 stars.

(7 marks: 2 for steps 1-3, 2 for star mapping, 1.5 for no-zero, 1.5 for min-equipment.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** What is Bharat NCAP? Compare its protocol with Euro NCAP. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of active safety systems (AEB, Lane Support, ESC) in modern vehicle safety ratings. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Bharat NCAP** is India's New Car Assessment Program, launched in **2023** by the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH) under rule 126 of CMVR (Central Motor Vehicles Rules). It is the official Indian government rating system, applying to vehicles manufactured or imported into India with an engine capacity up to $3.5 \text{ t}$ GVW and up to $9$ seats including the driver.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Bharat NCAP | Euro NCAP |
|---|---|---|
| Frontal impact test | ODB $64 \text{ km/h}$ at $40\%$ overlap | MPDB $50 \text{ km/h}$ at $40\%$ overlap |
| Side impact test | MDB $50 \text{ km/h}$ at $90°$ | MDB $60 \text{ km/h}$ at $90°$ |
| Pedestrian test | Lower legform only (no headform in v1.0) | Adult + child headform, upper + lower legform |
| Star range | 1 to 5 | 1 to 5 |
| AEB required | No (in v1.0) | Yes (for 5★) |
| Pole test | Not included | Yes (at 32 km/h) |
| Far-side test | Not included | Yes |
| Whiplash | Not included | Yes |
| Adult dummy | Hybrid III 50th | Hybrid III 50th + 5th female |
| Child dummy | Not yet | Q3, Q6, Q10 |
| Operator | MoRTH + Global NCAP | Euro NCAP consortium |

**Key differences:**
1. Bharat NCAP uses the **legacy 64 km/h ODB test**, whereas Euro NCAP moved to MPDB 50 km/h in 2020.
2. Bharat NCAP is more **conservative** in scope (no pole, no far-side, no AEB scoring) in its first version.
3. Bharat NCAP awards stars for adult and child occupant only, not yet VRU and Safety Assist as separate pillars.

(7 marks: 1 for introduction, 3 for comparison, 2 for differences, 1 for verdict.)

**Model Solution for (b):**

Active safety systems have become the single biggest differentiator in modern 5-star ratings. Their role in safety ratings is now at parity with — and in some cases exceeds — passive restraint systems.

**1. Electronic Stability Control (ESC):**
ESC is the foundational active system. It uses individual wheel braking and torque vectoring to prevent skidding. Euro NCAP has mandated ESC for the top two star bands since 2009. Real-world studies (e.g., IIHS 2011) show ESC reduces single-vehicle crashes by $\sim 41\%$ and fatal single-vehicle rollovers by $\sim 56\%$. In the rating, ESC is *binary*: present or absent. Its absence caps the rating at 3 stars maximum.

**2. Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB):**
AEB uses forward radar, camera, and (optionally) lidar to detect a closing collision and apply the brakes autonomously if the driver fails to. Euro NCAP tests AEB in two regimes:
- **AEB Inter-Urban (CCrs):** AEB against a stationary or slower-moving lead vehicle from $10$ to $80 \text{ km/h}$. Scored by avoidance or mitigation.
- **AEB VRU (Pedestrian & Cyclist):** AEB against adult and child pedestrians / cyclists crossing the path. Scored by avoidance at $20$ to $60 \text{ km/h}$.

AEB is mandatory for the 5-star rating. Real-world data (Euro NCAP 2023) shows AEB reduces rear-end crashes by $\sim 38\%$.

**3. Lane Support System (LSS):**
LSS combines Lane Departure Warning (LDW) with Lane Keep Assist (LKA). The LDW warns the driver via haptic or audio cue, while LKA applies a corrective steering torque. Euro NCAP 5★ requires the system to be operational in $80\%$ of the time above $65 \text{ km/h}$. Real-world: LSS reduces lane-departure crashes by $\sim 30\%$.

**4. Speed Assist System (SAS) / Intelligent Speed Assistance (ISA):**
ISA uses camera + map data to detect the local speed limit and either warn or actively limit the vehicle. EU regulation mandates ISA in all new cars from **2024**. Euro NCAP 5★ requires ISA to be *active* (limiting), not just advisory.

**5. Driver Monitoring / Attention Assist:**
Detects drowsiness through steering-input analysis or a driver-facing camera. Euro NCAP 5★ requires camera-based attention monitoring for $2024+$.

**6. eCall:**
Automatically dials emergency services in the event of a deployment of any airbag. Mandatory in EU from 2018.

**Synthesis:** Modern 5-star cars are *active-safety dominated*. A 2024 Euro NCAP 5★ requires at least 6 airbags (front, side, curtain, centre, knee), ESC, AEB Inter-Urban, AEB VRU, LSS, ISA, attention assist, and eCall. The rating **motivates** the OEM to engineer active systems first, because they prevent the crash — the cheapest "crumple zone."

(7 marks: 1 for introduction, 4 for system-wise explanation, 2 for synthesis.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the "no-zero" rule with the "minimum score" rule.** No-zero means *no body region scores zero in any sub-test*. Minimum score is the *threshold* (e.g., 80% for 5★). These are two independent checks.
> 2. **Do not write "Euro NCAP rates cars out of 10."** It is rated *out of 5 stars*, computed from a percentage score.
> 3. **Do not omit the dummy names** (Hybrid III 50th, WorldSID, Q6, etc.) in protocol questions. Examiners explicitly award marks for ATD identification.
> 4. **Do not confuse HIC with HPC.** HPC is the Head Performance Criterion (a derivative of HIC for modern ATDs like THOR); HIC is used with Hybrid III.
> 5. **Do not write "more stars = safer."** Yes, in general, but the difference between 4★ and 5★ is small. The 5★ gate is the *equipment* gate, not the crash gate. Examiners expect you to note this nuance.
> 6. **Bharat NCAP is NOT Global NCAP.** Global NCAP is an independent foundation; Bharat NCAP is the Indian government program. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for confusing the two.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Safety Ratings (KTU PEAUT861 Module 4)**

**Core Concepts:**
- Vehicle Safety Rating = standardized output of NCAPs in 1 to 5 stars (or TSP/TSP+ for IIHS).
- Euro NCAP is the global reference; Bharat NCAP is the Indian program (launched 2023).
- Four pillars: AOP, COP, VRU, SA.
- Aggregate score is a weighted sum of pillar scores.
- No-zero rule + minimum equipment rule can cap the rating.

**Tests to remember:**
- FWRB: 50 km/h, full width, rigid barrier.
- MPDB: 50 km/h, 40% overlap, deformable.
- MDB side: 60 km/h, deformable.
- Pole: 32 km/h at 75°.
- Whiplash: sled-based.
- Pedestrian: headform + legform, 40 km/h.
- AEB: Inter-Urban and VRU.

**Dummies (ATDs) to remember:**
- Hybrid III 50th (driver), Hybrid III 5th (rear passenger), WorldSID 50th (side impact), Q3 / Q6 / Q10 (child), THOR-50M (modern research).

**Injury Criteria to remember:**
- $\text{HIC}_{36} \le 1000$ (head), chest compression $\le 50 \text{ mm}$, femur force $\le 10 \text{ kN}$, tibia index $\le 1.4$, neck $F_x \le 3.1 \text{ kN}$.

**Active Systems to remember:**
- ESC, AEB (Inter-Urban & VRU), LSS (LDW + LKA), ISA, attention assist, eCall.

**Formulas to remember:**
- $\text{HIC}_{36}$ integral, viscous criterion $V \cdot C$, aggregate score formula, crumple stroke $s = v^2 / (2a_{\text{avg}})$.

**Industrial Names to remember:**
- Euro NCAP, US NCAP, NHTSA, IIHS, ANCAP, JNCAP, Global NCAP, Bharat NCAP, C-NCAP.

**Star thresholds (Euro NCAP):**
- 5★ = $\ge 80\%$ + no-zero + min equipment.
- 4★ = $\ge 70\%$ + no-zero + min equipment.
- 3★ = $\ge 50\%$.
- 2★ = $\ge 20\%$.

**One-Liners for Last-Minute Revision:**
- *NCAP = crash tests + dummy readings → star rating.*
- *More airbags ≠ more stars; calibrated restraint matters more.*
- *Euro NCAP 5★ is equipment-driven, not just crash-driven.*
- *HIC$_{36}$ has 2.5 exponent — non-linear head injury predictor.*
- *Bharat NCAP 2023 = Indian program = ODB 64 km/h, MDB 50 km/h, 1 to 5 stars.*
